Here is the structured English summary for modern business readers, based solely on the provided passage.

### Executive Summary
Half of all business correspondence is unnecessary, and much of the rest is so poorly structured that a simple transaction requires multiple exchanges instead of two. Effective writing requires clear thinking and planning, not just strict adherence to rules. The only two essential goals are conveying the idea clearly and building goodwill. A letter that draws attention to its own style rather than its message is a failure. Brevity, courtesy, and completeness are paramount. Standard formats and high-quality, simple stationery build trust; flashy designs appear unprofessional. Each letter should cover one subject only, and all correspondence must be error-free regarding addresses and enclosures to avoid confusion and wasted effort.

### Five Principles of Effective Business Writing
1.  **Purpose Before Content:** Only write a letter if there is a clear, necessary reason behind it; avoid wasteful or aimless communication.
2.  **Message Over Style:** The structure and language of the letter must never distract the reader from the core message or call to action.
3.  **Clarity and Conciseness:** A letter must succeed in getting its idea across completely and courteously in the briefest possible space.
4.  **Standardization Builds Trust:** Adhering to conventional formats and using simple, high-quality stationery signals professionalism, not lack of originality.
5.  **Single-Subject Focus:** Each letter should handle only one subject to ensure efficient filing and retrieval.

### Table: Original-Era Advice vs. Modern Equivalent

| Original-era advice | Modern equivalent |
| :--- | :--- |
| Letters should be typewritten (except rare handwriting requests). | Emails and documents should be typed using a clean, professional font; handwritten notes are for rare personal touches. |
| Use plain white paper of good quality; avoid gaudy colors and ornate letterheads. | Use clean, readable email formatting and simple templates; avoid flashy signature blocks, distracting colors, or excessive graphics. |
| Each subject should be in a separate paragraph; a letter should handle only one subject. | Use clear subject lines and bullet points for each topic; send separate emails for separate issues to keep threads tidy. |
| The outside address must be complete, with no state abbreviations that cause confusion. | The "To," "CC," and "BCC" fields must be accurate; use correct domain names and avoid auto-fill errors. |
| A letter that draws attention to itself (instead of its message) is a failure. | An email with a clever subject line or flashy design that obscures the action required is a failure; clarity of intent is paramount. |

### Dated Advice and Modern Update

**Dated Advice:**
"The typing is taken care of by girls who have just entered business (and are irresponsible), so the dictator must check the letter’s layout."

**Underlying Principle:**
Review all outgoing correspondence to ensure it is professional, symmetrical, and error-free, because first impressions matter.

**Modern Update:**
Use spell-check, grammar tools (like Grammarly), and preview functions before sending an email or document. While you may not have a "stenographer," you are responsible for the final appearance of your communication. Run a final visual check to ensure formatting (margins, line breaks, proper bolding) is polished and easy to read, especially when your software does not default to a clean layout.

messages.content:
Read the public-domain passage below and produce a structured English summary.

Output requirements:
1. A 120-word maximum plot summary.
2. A bullet list of Mary’s character traits shown in the passage, each supported by a concrete detail.
3. A short paragraph explaining how the setting creates emotional distance or isolation.
4. Three discussion questions suitable for ages 10–12.
5. Do not add plot information from outside the passage.

素材来源：Frances Hodgson Burnett, The Secret Garden, opening excerpt
来源链接：https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/113
版权/授权说明：Project Gutenberg lists this eBook as public domain in the USA.

【待处理素材】
When Mary Lennox was sent to Misselthwaite Manor to live with her uncle everybody said she was the most disagreeable-looking child ever seen. It was true, too. She had a little thin face and a little thin body, thin light hair and a sour expression. Her hair was yellow, and her face was yellow because she had been born in India and had always been ill in one way or another. Her father had held a position under the English Government and had always been busy and ill himself, and her mother had been a great beauty who cared only to go to parties and amuse herself with gay people. She had not wanted a little girl at all, and when Mary was born she handed her over to the care of an Ayah, who was made to understand that if she wished to please the Mem Sahib she must keep the child out of sight as much as possible. So when she was a sickly, fretful, ugly little baby she was kept out of the way, and when she became a sickly, fretful, toddling thing she was kept out of the way also. She never remembered seeing familiarly anything but the dark faces of her Ayah and the other native servants, and as they always obeyed her and gave her her own way in everything, because the Mem Sahib would be angry if she was disturbed by her crying, by the time she was six years old she was as tyrannical and selfish a little pig as ever lived. The young English governess who came to teach her to read and write disliked her so much that she gave up her place in three months, and when other governesses came to try to fill it they always went away in a shorter time than the first one. So if Mary had not chosen to really want to know how to read books she would never have learned her letters at all.

One frightfully hot morning, when she was about nine years old, she awakened feeling very cross, and she became crosser still when she saw that the servant who stood by her bedside was not her Ayah.

“Why did you come?” she said to the strange woman. “I will not let you stay. Send my Ayah to me.”

The woman looked frightened, but she only stammered that the Ayah could not come and when Mary threw herself into a passion and beat and kicked her, she looked only more frightened and repeated that it was not possible for the Ayah to come to Missie Sahib.

There was something mysterious in the air that morning. Nothing was done in its regular order and several of the native servants seemed missing, while those whom Mary saw slunk or hurried about with ashy and scared faces. But no one would tell her anything and her Ayah did not come. She was actually left alone as the morning went on, and at last she wandered out into the garden and began to play by herself under a tree near the veranda. She pretended that she was making a flower-bed, and she stuck big scarlet hibiscus blossoms into little heaps of earth, all the time growing more and more angry and muttering to herself the things she would say and the names she would call Saidie when she returned.

“Pig! Pig! Daughter of Pigs!” she said, because to call a native a pig is the worst insult of all.

She was grinding her teeth and saying this over and over again when she heard her mother come out on the veranda with someone. She was with a fair young man and they stood talking together in low strange voices. Mary knew the fair young man who looked like a boy. She had heard that he was a very young officer who had just come from England. The child stared at him, but she stared most at her mother. She always did this when she had a chance to see her, because the Mem Sahib—Mary used to call her that oftener than anything else—was such a tall, slim, pretty person and wore such lovely clothes. Her hair was like curly silk and she had a delicate little nose which seemed to be disdaining things, and she had large laughing eyes. All her clothes were thin and floating, and Mary said they were “full of lace.” They looked fuller of lace than ever this morning, but her eyes were not laughing at all. They were large and scared and lifted imploringly to the fair boy officer’s face.

“Is it so very bad? Oh, is it?” Mary heard her say.

“Awfully,” the young man answered in a trembling voice. “Awfully, Mrs. Lennox. You ought to have gone to the hills two weeks ago.”

The Mem Sahib wrung her hands.

“Oh, I know I ought!” she cried. “I only stayed to go to that silly dinner party. What a fool I was!”

At that very moment such a loud sound of wailing broke out from the servants’ quarters that she clutched the young man’s arm, and Mary stood shivering from head to foot. The wailing grew wilder and wilder. “What is it? What is it?” Mrs. Lennox gasped.

“Someone has died,” answered the boy officer. “You did not say it had broken out among your servants.”

“I did not know!” the Mem Sahib cried. “Come with me! Come with me!” and she turned and ran into the house.

After that appalling things happened, and the mysteriousness of the morning was explained to Mary. The cholera had broken out in its most fatal form and people were dying like flies. The Ayah had been taken ill in the night, and it was because she had just died that the servants had wailed in the huts. Before the next day three other servants were dead and others had run away in terror. There was panic on every side, and dying people in all the bungalows.

During the confusion and bewilderment of the second day Mary hid herself in the nursery and was forgotten by everyone. Nobody thought of her, nobody wanted her, and strange things happened of which she knew nothing. Mary alternately cried and slept through the hours. She only knew that people were ill and that she heard mysterious and frightening sounds. Once she crept into the dining-room and found it empty, though a partly finished meal was on the table and chairs and plates looked as if they had been hastily pushed back when the diners rose suddenly for some reason. The child ate some fruit and biscuits, and being thirsty she drank a glass of wine which stood nearly filled. It was sweet, and she did not know how strong it was. Very soon it made her intensely drowsy, and she went back to her nursery and shut herself in again, frightened by cries she heard in the huts and by the hurrying sound of feet. The wine made her so sleepy that she could scarcely keep her eyes open and she lay down on her bed and knew nothing more for a long time.

message.content:
### Plot Summary (120 words max)
Mary Lennox, a sickly, sour-faced nine-year-old, is sent to Misselthwaite Manor after her parents die of cholera in India. Born unwanted to a beauty-obsessed mother and a busy, ill father, Mary was raised by servants who spoiled her into a tyrannical, selfish child. After her Ayah dies of cholera, Mary hides alone in the nursery, forgotten by everyone. During the panic, she eats leftover food, drinks wine, and falls into a deep sleep. She is later discovered and sent to England, where her uncle, Mr. Craven, lives at the isolated manor. The story opens with her arrival there, described as the most disagreeable-looking child anyone had ever seen.

### Mary’s Character Traits (with Concrete Details)
- **Sickly and sour-looking**: She has a “little thin face,” “thin light hair,” and a “sour expression.”
- **Tyrannical and selfish**: She throws a passion, beats, and kicks a servant who tries to replace her Ayah.
- **Spoiled by servants**: Her Ayah and other native servants “always obeyed her and gave her her own way in everything.”
- **Angry and insulting**: She mutters “Pig! Pig! Daughter of Pigs!” as the worst insult when frustrated.
- **Emotionally neglected**: Her mother “had not wanted a little girl at all” and kept Mary “out of sight as much as possible.”

### Setting’s Role in Emotional Distance or Isolation
The cholera outbreak in India creates sudden, total isolation for Mary. During the panic, she “hid herself in the nursery and was forgotten by everyone”—no one thinks of or wants her. The empty dining room, with hurriedly abandoned chairs and plates, emphasizes her aloneness. Afterward, she is sent to Misselthwaite Manor, a remote English estate, where her uncle’s absence and the cold, unfamiliar setting reinforce her emotional distance from any loving connection. The manor’s silence and mystery deepen her sense of being unwanted and cut off.

### Discussion Questions (Ages 10–12)
1. Why do you think Mary’s mother didn’t want to spend time with her, and how do you think that affected Mary’s behavior?
2. When Mary beat and kicked the servant, why do you think she acted so angrily? What could she have done instead?
3. If you were Mary, how would you have felt being completely forgotten during the cholera crisis? What would you do to feel less alone?

messages.content:
Read the public-domain science explainer below and produce a structured English summary.

Output requirements:
1. A concise 100-word overview.
2. A concept map in text form using arrows, showing how water moves among major pools.
3. A list of at least eight key terms with one-sentence definitions based only on the passage.
4. A section titled “Human influence” summarizing how human actions affect water movement, storage, or availability.
5. Do not introduce outside climate, policy, or regional examples.

素材来源：U.S. Geological Survey Water Science School: Water cycle
来源链接：https://www.usgs.gov/water-science-school/water-cycle
版权/授权说明：USGS-authored information is considered U.S. public domain.

【待处理素材】
The water cycle describes where water is on Earth and how it moves. Human water use, land use, and climate change all impact the water cycle. By understanding these impacts, we can work toward using water sustainably.

Viewed from space, the most striking feature of our planet is the water. In both liquid and frozen form, it covers 75% of the Earth's surface. It fills the sky with clouds. Water is practically everywhere on Earth, from inside the planet's rocky crust to inside the cells of the human body. What's important to keep in mind is that all of this water is in constant motion across our planet.

Water is stored in the atmosphere, on the land surface, and below the ground. It can be a liquid, a solid, or a gas. Liquid water can be fresh or saline. Water moves between the places it is stored. Water moves at large scales, through watersheds, the atmosphere, and below the Earth's surface. Water moves at very small scales too. It is in us, plants, and other organisms.

Oceans store 96% of all water on Earth. Ocean water is saline, meaning it’s salty. On land, saline water is stored in saline lakes. The rest of the water on Earth is fresh water. Fresh water is stored in liquid form in freshwater lakes, artificial reservoirs, rivers, and wetlands. Water is stored in solid, frozen form in ice sheets and glaciers, and in snowpack at high elevations or near Earth's poles.

As it moves, water can change form between liquid, solid, and gas. Circulation mixes water in the oceans and transports water vapor in the atmosphere. Water moves between the atmosphere and the surface through evaporation, evapotranspiration, and precipitation. Water moves across the surface through snowmelt, runoff, and streamflow. Water moves into the ground through infiltration and groundwater recharge. Underground, groundwater flows within aquifers. Groundwater can return to the surface through natural discharge into rivers, the ocean, and from springs.

Water moves naturally and because of human actions. Energy from the sun and the force of gravity drive the continual movement of water between pools. The sun’s energy causes liquid water to evaporate into water vapor. Evapotranspiration is the main way water moves into the atmosphere from the land surface and oceans. Gravity causes water to flow downward on land. It causes rain, snow, and hail to fall from clouds.

Humans and other organisms rely on water for life. The amount of water that is available depends on how much water there is in each pool, when and how fast water moves through the water cycle, and how clean the water is. By understanding human impacts on the water cycle, we can work toward using water sustainably.

message.content:
Here is the structured English summary based on the provided passage.

### 1. Concise 100-Word Overview
The water cycle describes the constant movement of water on Earth, which is stored in oceans, atmosphere, land surface, and underground. Oceans hold 96% of all water; the remainder is fresh water in lakes, rivers, ice, and groundwater. Water changes form between liquid, solid, and gas as it moves. The sun’s energy and gravity drive this movement. Water travels through evaporation, evapotranspiration, precipitation, runoff, infiltration, and groundwater flow. Human water use and land use impact this cycle. Understanding these impacts is key to using water sustainably.

### 2. Concept Map
```
Atmosphere (water vapor)
     ↑ evaporation/evapotranspiration ↓ precipitation
Oceans (saline, 96% of water) ← → Land surface (fresh/saline lakes, rivers, snowpack, ice)
     ↘ infiltration / groundwater recharge ↗ groundwater discharge
     ↓ Groundwater (aquifers)
```

### 3. Key Terms
- **Water cycle:** The description of where water is on Earth and how it moves.
- **Oceans:** The largest water pool, storing 96% of all Earth's water, which is saline.
- **Fresh water:** Water stored in liquid form in lakes, reservoirs, rivers, and wetlands, or as solid ice and snow.
- **Evaporation:** The process by which the sun's energy causes liquid water to turn into water vapor.
- **Evapotranspiration:** The main way water moves into the atmosphere from the land surface and oceans.
- **Precipitation:** The falling of rain, snow, or hail from clouds, driven by gravity.
- **Infiltration:** The process by which water moves into the ground.
- **Groundwater:** Water that flows underground within aquifers and can return to the surface through springs or discharge into rivers and oceans.

### 4. Human influence
Human water use and land use impact the water cycle, affecting the amount of water available in each pool, the timing and speed of water movement, and water cleanliness. By understanding these impacts, humans can work toward using water sustainably.

messages.content:
Write an 800–1,000 word executive update email from the COO of a fictional direct-to-consumer home goods company to all employees. Context: a warehouse management system migration has reduced shipping speed for one week, and customer support volume is up.

Requirements:
- Tone: accountable, calm, specific, and respectful of frontline teams.
- Explain what happened without blaming a team or vendor.
- Include current customer impact, what is being done today, what will change next week, and how teams should escalate urgent issues.
- Include a concise manager talking-points section with 4 bullets.
- Avoid clichés, excessive apology, legal language, or promises that cannot be guaranteed.

message.content:
**Subject:** Update on Warehouse System Migration: Current Status and Next Steps  

**Team,**  

I want to share a direct update on our recent warehouse management system (WMS) migration and the impact it’s having on our operations—and on the experience we’re delivering to customers.  

Last weekend, we completed a planned migration to a new WMS platform. This change was intended to improve long-term accuracy, scalability, and speed. The implementation has gone successfully in many areas, but it has also introduced specific, unanticipated delays in the picking and packing workflow for a subset of orders. As a result, shipping speed for certain products has been slower than our standard SLA for the past seven days.  

**What’s happening now**  

- **Customer impact:** About 18% of orders placed between [date range] have shipped one to three days later than expected. The remaining 82% are moving at normal speed. However, because slower orders are concentrated in a few high-volume SKUs, customer support volume has increased by roughly 40% this week. Most contacts are order-status inquiries and delay notifications.  

- **Root cause (direct, no finger-pointing):** The new WMS uses a revised bin-location algorithm. During high-velocity order windows, it occasionally assigns pickers to locations that are not physically adjacent—adding extra travel time per order. Our integration team identified this late in testing, but we chose to proceed because the fix required a code change that would have delayed migration by four weeks. That was a trade-off we accepted. Today, I am responsible for ensuring we resolve it quickly.  

- **What we are doing today:**  
  - A dedicated cross-functional squad (Warehouse Ops, IT, and Vendor Support) is in a live war room at [location]. They have already re-sequenced the algorithm for the top 10 affected SKUs. Early data shows a 30% reduction in pick-path deviation as of this morning.  
  - The customer support team has an additional 15 agents on shift this week, with overtime approved through next Friday. They have a daily email template for proactive “delay alert” sends to any order that will ship late.  
  - We have paused all non-essential inventory moves in the warehouse until Monday to free up picker capacity.  

**What will change next week**  

- By Tuesday, we will deploy a full fix to the bin-location logic. This has already passed internal QA and is being stress-tested this weekend. After deployment, we expect pick-path deviation to return to baseline (sub-5% variance).  
- We will launch a “ship-date transparency” update to the website and app, so customers see real-time estimated ship dates based on current algorithm performance (rather than static averages).  
- On Wednesday, the Operations team will run a 12-hour simulation of peak order volume using the new logic. If it passes, we will lift the inventory-move pause.  
- By Friday, I will share a post-mortem with metrics on recovery time, support closure rates, and any remaining gaps.  

**How to escalate urgent issues**  

If you are encountering a problem that is blocking your ability to serve customers or work safely, please use the following channels—do not wait to flag it:  

- **Warehouse-floor issues** (safety, equipment, or system access): Call [Emergency Ops Line] immediately.  
- **Customer escalation** (unresolved after standard support process): Tag #WMS-ESCALATE in Zendesk or Slack, and DM the on-call Ops manager at [Name].  
- **IT/system error** (code bug, data integrity issue): Post in #it-support-wms with the exact error message and time.  
- **Manager or team-level concern that feels stuck:** Email me directly at [COO email]. I will respond within 90 minutes during business hours.  

**Manager talking points (for team huddles)**  

Use the following with your teams—no need to read verbatim, but these cover the core message:  

1. **No one is at fault.** The migration was a calculated risk that turned out to have a specific, fixable bottleneck. We made the call together, and we’re fixing it together.  
2. **The warehouse team has been managing a difficult week with professionalism.** If you see a picker, packer, or support agent—please thank them. The pressure is real, and they are handling it well.  
3. **Customer communication is the priority right now.** If you interact with customers (in support, sales, or social), use the “delay alert” template. Do not promise a specific shipping date unless you have checked the live dashboard.  
4. **This is temporary.** We expect full recovery by next Thursday. If you see a potential issue that could delay that timeline, escalate immediately using the channels above.  

**A note about ownership**  

I chose to proceed with the migration on the scheduled date despite the known algorithm risk. I did so because delaying would have pushed the project into Q4, when peak demand would have magnified any disruption. That was my judgment call. It was the right decision in principle, but the execution has been harder than expected. I own that, and I am accountable for both the disruption and the recovery.  

Our frontline teams—warehouse, support, logistics—have absorbed this well. Thank you for the work you’ve done to keep orders moving and customers informed. We will get through this week, and we will come out with a better system and a stronger process for future migrations.  

Please direct questions to your manager or to me directly. I will send a brief daily update at 8:00 AM and 4:00 PM until we are fully stable.  

— [COO Name]  
Chief Operating Officer

messages.content:
Write an 800–1,000 word story for children ages 7–10. The main characters are a careful snail who keeps a map of every puddle in the garden and a young sparrow who wants to fly before she has learned how to land. The story should take place after a summer rain.

Requirements:
- Complete story arc with a gentle problem and resolution.
- Warm, concrete language suitable for children.
- Include one simple nature concept, such as how puddles dry or how birds use wind, explained naturally through the story.
- Theme: patience can be a kind of bravery.
- No violence, death, horror, or adult romance.

message.content:
Here is a story for children ages 7–10.

---

**The Snail’s Map and the Sparrow’s Leap**

The summer rain had washed the garden clean. Every leaf glittered, and the air smelled sweet with wet earth and honeysuckle. For a small snail named Silas, it was the most wonderful day of the year.

Silas was a careful snail. His shell was a perfect spiral of brown and cream, and he carried the most important thing he owned strapped to his back with a blade of grass: a map. It was a map of every puddle in the garden. There were big, round puddles near the rosebushes, like tiny lakes. There were long, skinny puddles under the dripping rainspout. And best of all, there was the Great Puddle behind the compost heap, which was so wide it reflected the whole sky.

Silas slid slowly along the wet flagstone path, his map unrolled in front of him. He was adding a brand-new puddle—a perfect, sparkling oval near the mint patch—with a tiny dot of berry juice.

“Hello down there!” chirped a bright, breezy voice.

Silas looked up. A young sparrow with a scruffy chest and impatient wings was hopping from the birdbath to the fence post. Her name was Pip.

“Why are you staring at a leaf?” Pip asked, tilting her head.

“It’s not a leaf,” said Silas politely. “It’s my puddle map. I keep track of them all. Some are deep, some are shallow. Some last all day, and some dry up in an hour.”

Pip fluffed her feathers. “Puddles are boring. You just sit in them. I want to *fly*.” She hopped to the very tip of the fence post and looked up at the sky. It was big and blue, with one slow, fluffy cloud. “I want to feel the wind in my wings. I want to swoop!”

“You’ve never flown before?” Silas asked.

“No,” said Pip, a little embarrassed. “My brothers and sisters are already zipping around. But every time I try, I get scared and land on my beak in the mud. The secret is, I don’t know how to land. So I never push off.”

Silas looked at his map. He knew all about careful steps. “It’s a long way down from that fence,” he said gently.

“That’s why I need to go *up* first! If I launch from the big oak branch, I’ll have so much room to fly! Come watch. Please?”

“I… I move very slowly,” said Silas. “By the time I get to the oak tree, the puddle by the mint might be gone.”

“Oh, you worry too much,” chirped Pip. “Watch this!”

She took a running hop, flapped her wings with all her might… and tumbled backward into the birdbath with a splash.

Silas winced. Pip came up spluttering, her tail feathers drooping.

“See?” she said miserably. “I just can’t do it.”

Silas felt a soft, squirmy feeling in his chest. He didn’t like to see his friend so sad. “Maybe you could try a smaller flight first,” he said. “The puddle I just drew—it’s shallow, but it’s perfect for practicing landing. It’s soft, too.”

Pip wrinkled her beak. “But I want to fly *high*. Flying low is for bugs.”

“Flying is flying,” said Silas. “And landing is part of flying. You can’t have one without the other.”

Pip thought about this. Her wings itched. The sky called to her. But she remembered the splash in the birdbath.

“Fine,” she sighed. “Show me your silly puddle.”

So Silas led the way. It took a long, long time. The sun came out from behind a cloud and began to dry the stones. Silas moved like a tiny, patient comma across the page of the world. Pip hopped ahead, then fluttered back, then chirped impatiently.

“You’re so *slow*!” she said.

“Yes,” said Silas. “But I always get there.”

When they finally reached the mint patch, the new puddle was there—a perfect oval, like a silver mirror on the ground. But something was wrong. The sun was warm on Silas’s shell. The puddle was shrinking.

“It’s getting smaller!” said Pip.

Silas pointed with his tentacles. “The sun is drinking it up. That’s how puddles dry. The sun turns the water into tiny invisible pieces that float up into the sky to make new clouds.”

“So it’s disappearing,” Pip whispered. “My chance is disappearing.”

Silas looked at the puddle. It was still big enough. “Don’t wait until it’s gone,” he said. “Be brave now. But be patient, too. Patience can be a kind of bravery, you know. Brave isn’t only about rushing into the sky. It’s also about waiting for the right moment.”

Pip took a deep breath. She stood at the edge of the shrinking puddle. It was not big. It was not high. It was just a spoonful of sky on the ground.

“I want to fly over it and land on the other side,” she said.

“Then try,” said Silas. “The water is soft.”

Pip fluffed her feathers. She jumped. Her wings flapped—one, two, three. Her feet left the ground! For one whole second, she was flying! She was in the air! And then, just like that, she came down—*splosh*—right in the middle of the puddle.

But this time, it was a good splash. Her feet landed on the soft mud, and she skidded a little, but she didn’t fall. She stood up, dripping and proud.

“I did it!” she chirped. “I flew! And I landed!”

Silas clapped his tiny tentacles together. “You did! See? The shallow puddle was just right.”

Pip looked at the puddle. It was now just a wet, shiny patch. The sun had almost finished drinking it. In another minute, it would be gone.

“It’s almost dry,” she said softly.

“Yes,” said Silas. “But you used it just in time.”

Pip hopped over to Silas and touched her beak gently to his shell. “You were right,” she said. “Patience *is* brave. You waited for me. You showed me the way. I was in such a hurry to fly high that I never learned to land low.”

Silas smiled a slow, snail smile. He drew a little star on his map next to the mint puddle, because it was special now.

The next morning, Pip tried the fence post again. This time, she didn’t think about flying high. She thought about landing low. She flapped. She lifted. She flew all the way across the garden and landed with a perfect two-foot hop right next to Silas, who was adding a puddle under the dripping hose.

“Good morning,” said Pip.

“Good morning,” said Silas.

And the garden was full of puddles, each one a lesson, waiting for any creature—fast or slow—to learn.

messages.content:
Write an 800–1,000 word press release in English based only on the fictional facts below. Do not invent funding amounts, celebrity endorsers, political supporters, or external partners. Use a professional, journalistic style with a headline, dateline, lead paragraph, supporting details, at least one direct quote, and a media contact block.

Fictional facts:
- Organization: Harborleaf Botanical Garden.
- Announcement: Launch of a new “Pollinator Path” public education trail.
- Opening date: July 12, 2026.
- Location: The east meadow loop, a 0.8-mile accessible trail.
- Features: native plant demonstration beds, night-blooming flower station, tactile signage, youth observation cards, shaded resting points, and a volunteer-led weekend walk.
- Motivation: visitors have asked for more practical guidance on planting for bees, butterflies, moths, and hummingbirds.
- Quote speaker: Maya Ortiz, Director of Learning Programs. Core message: small planting decisions can create connected habitats across neighborhoods.
- Keep the topic nonpolitical and educational.

message.content:
**FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE**

**Harborleaf Botanical Garden Unveils New Pollinator Path, a Hands-On Educational Trail for Habitat Conservation**

**HARBORLEAF, MN – July 1, 2026** – Harborleaf Botanical Garden today announced the launch of its newest public education initiative, the “Pollinator Path,” a dedicated trail designed to teach visitors how to support local bees, butterflies, moths, and hummingbirds through practical, small-scale planting choices. The 0.8-mile accessible loop, located in the garden’s east meadow, will officially open to the public on July 12, 2026.

Responding to growing visitor interest in actionable guidance for backyard and community gardening, the Pollinator Path transforms a scenic meadow loop into an interactive classroom. The trail features native plant demonstration beds that showcase species proven to attract and sustain pollinators native to the Upper Midwest, as well as a specialized night-blooming flower station highlighting plants that support nocturnal moths. Tactile signage along the route provides information in both English and braille, ensuring the experience is accessible to visitors with visual impairments. Youth observation cards, available at the trailhead, encourage children to document the insects and flowers they encounter.

“Our visitors have consistently asked for more practical, take-home knowledge about what they can plant to help pollinators,” said Maya Ortiz, Director of Learning Programs at Harborleaf Botanical Garden. “People want to move beyond general awareness. They want to know which specific flowers will feed a bumblebee in late summer, or how a small patch of milkweed in a city yard can connect to larger habitats. The Pollinator Path is our answer to that request.”

The trail is designed to be self-guided and family-friendly, with shaded resting points interspersed along the loop. In addition to the permanent features, the garden will host a volunteer-led weekend walk each Saturday and Sunday through the end of the growing season. Trained docents will lead small groups, offering deeper context on plant-pollinator relationships and answering individual questions. No registration is required for the weekend walks, which will begin at 10 a.m. at the east meadow trailhead.

The Pollinator Path emphasizes that even small, deliberate planting decisions can create a network of connected habitats across neighborhoods. The demonstration beds are planted exclusively with regionally appropriate, non-invasive native species, including bee balm, purple coneflower, butterfly weed, and evening primrose. Each bed is labeled with the plant’s common and scientific names, bloom period, and the specific pollinator species it supports.

“One of the most powerful messages we can share is that a single flowerpot on an apartment balcony or a strip of native shrubs along a sidewalk can be part of a larger pollinator corridor,” Ortiz added. “When neighbors coordinate even modest efforts—choosing a milkweed instead of an ornamental, or leaving a few fallen leaves for overwintering insects—the cumulative effect is profound. We hope the Pollinator Path inspires visitors to see their own outdoor spaces as extensions of this garden.”

The night-blooming flower station, a highlight of the trail, features plantings such as moonflower, evening stock, and night-blooming jasmine, paired with interpretive signage explaining how moths and other nocturnal pollinators navigate in darkness. The station is also lit with low-level, pollinator-safe LED lighting to allow evening viewing without disrupting insect behavior.

Youth observation cards, available free of charge, prompt children to record observations such as the color of a flower, the type of pollinator visiting it, and the time of day. Completed cards can be returned to the garden’s visitor center for a small, nature-themed sticker.

The east meadow loop itself is a previously underutilized section of the garden that has been renovated to meet accessibility standards. The trail surface is composed of compacted, fine-grained gravel suitable for wheelchairs and strollers. Rest areas are positioned at roughly quarter-mile intervals, each with a bench and interpretive panel.

The opening on July 12 will be marked by a brief introductory program at 9:30 a.m. at the trailhead, followed by the first official volunteer-led walk. The garden encourages early arrival, as parking is limited. The Pollinator Path is included with general garden admission, and no additional ticket is required.

Harborleaf Botanical Garden is a private, nonprofit organization dedicated to plant conservation, education, and community engagement. The garden is open year-round, with seasonal hours.

**Media Contact:**
Elena Vasquez
Communications Coordinator
Harborleaf Botanical Garden
555-010-3342
e.vasquez@harborleafgarden.org

###

*Note to editors: High-resolution images of the Pollinator Path demonstration beds, tactile signage, and the night-blooming station are available upon request. Interviews with Maya Ortiz can be scheduled on-site July 12 or by phone during the week prior.*

messages.content:
Write an 800–1,000 word non-erotic literary romance scene in English. Scenario: two former neighbors meet by chance in a small coastal laundromat during a power outage. Years ago, one of them left without saying goodbye. Tonight, a battery lantern, a half-finished load of towels, and an old voicemail become the center of the conversation.

Requirements:
- Focus on restrained emotional realism: regret, tenderness, defensiveness, and the difficulty of apologizing.
- Keep the entire piece to one scene.
- Do not use coercion, stalking, humiliation, explicit sexual content, or melodrama.
- Let physical details and subtext carry the emotion; dialogue should be sparse but meaningful.
- End with a quiet unresolved gesture rather than a full confession.

message.content:
The laundromat on Harbor Street smelled of salt, bleach, and the particular dampness of fabric softener sheets left too long in the dryer. Outside, the town had gone dark. The streetlights were dead, the neon “24HR LAUNDRY” sign was a cold tube of nothing, and the only sound was the low rumble of the sea a block away.

Inside, a single battery lantern sat on the folding counter, casting a weak, honey-colored circle. Mara stood inside it, feeding a damp towel into a dryer that wouldn’t start. She had already pressed the button three times. Nothing. The power had cut ten minutes ago, just as she’d transferred her load of beach towels from the washer.

“It’s out for the whole block,” a voice said from the dark near the door. “Heard someone say the transformer went.”

She knew the voice before she turned. Some sounds are wired into your memory like copper into a wall. She turned and saw Ben standing just beyond the lantern’s reach, holding a mesh bag of laundry. The years had sharpened his jaw and greyed his temples, but his posture was the same—slightly defensive, one hand in his pocket, as if he’d already decided to leave.

“Ben,” she said. Not a question.

He stepped forward into the light. His face was careful. “Mara. I wasn’t sure it was you.”

“It’s me.” She pressed the dryer button again, uselessly. “I’ve got wet towels and no power.”

“I’ve got dirty sheets and the same problem.” He held up the mesh bag. A corner of a striped pillowcase escaped. “I was passing through. Saw the lights on. Thought maybe this place ran on a generator.”

“No generator. Just a lantern.” She gestured to it. “The owner’s daughter brought it from the house next door. She said she’d be back in an hour with a repair guy.”

Ben set his bag on the bench near the door. He was wearing a wool sweater that looked too warm for the coastal night. “I can wait,” he said. “Or I can leave. Which do you want?”

It was the kind of question he used to ask—giving her the option, making her responsible for the answer. She hadn’t missed that about him.

“Stay,” she said. “It’s a long walk to the next town.”

He nodded and sat on the bench, folding his arms. The quiet settled between them, thick as the fog pressing against the windows.

She turned back to her wet towels. She pulled one out of the dryer and folded it into a neat square, then another. It was something to do with her hands. The motion felt close to prayer.

“You left,” she said, not looking up. “You just—went. One day the apartment was there, the next day it was empty. Your keys on the counter. No note.”

She heard him exhale. It was a long breath, like air leaving a tire.

“I know,” he said. “I’ve thought about that night more than you’d believe. But I didn’t know how to say it then, and I still don’t.”

She placed another folded towel on the pile. “Try.”

He was quiet for a long time. The lantern flickered once, then steadied. Outside, a car passed slowly, its headlights sweeping across the glass before disappearing.

“I had a voicemail from my father,” he said. “Three days before I left. He was sick. Terminal. I hadn’t spoken to him in seven years. He said he wanted to see me before he died. That was it. No apology. Just a request.”

Mara stopped folding. She looked at him. “You never told me.”

“I couldn’t.” His voice was low, uninflected. “I was ashamed. I was angry. I was a hundred things at once. And I thought if I told you, you’d try to fix it. You’d make me talk about it. And I didn’t want to be fixed. I wanted to disappear into that drive and not have to be seen feeling anything.”

She set the towel down. “So you disappeared from me, too.”

“Yes.” He met her eyes. “It was the cruelest thing I’ve ever done. And I’ve had years to measure it.”

She walked to the folding counter and sat on the edge, facing him. The lantern was between them, a small island of light. “I called you. I left messages. One of them I still have on my old phone. I never deleted it.”

“Why?”

“Because it was the last time I heard your voice.” She pulled her phone from her coat pocket. The screen glowed. “I listen to it sometimes. When I’m alone. When I miss you. When I want to remember what it felt like to be careless with someone.”

He didn’t look away. His hands were clasped between his knees, knuckles white. “Play it.”

She hesitated. Then she scrolled, tapped the screen, and held the phone out toward the lantern.

His voice came out of the small speaker, tinny and distant: *“Hey, it’s me. I’m sorry I missed your call. I’m driving. I’ll call you when I get there. I—look, I’m not good at this. But I wanted you to know I’m thinking of you. That’s all. Bye.”*

The recording ended. The silence rushed back.

“I was in a motel in Nevada when I left that,” Ben said. “I was three states away. I never called back.”

“I know.” She put the phone away.

He stood and walked to the window. The black glass reflected the lantern and their two figures, dim and overlapping. “I wanted to call a thousand times. But the longer I waited, the heavier the apology got. Until it was too heavy to lift.”

“It’s not too heavy,” she said. “It’s just words.”

He turned. “I’m sorry, Mara. I’m sorry I left without saying goodbye. I’m sorry I didn’t trust you with the truth. I’m sorry I let silence be my goodbye.”

She held his gaze. The lantern hummed. A moth tapped against the glass.

“Okay,” she said. “That’s a start.”

He smiled, just barely. “A start. After twelve years.”

“It’s still a start.”

He walked back and sat on the bench. She stayed on the counter’s edge. The wet towels lay forgotten. Outside, the sea kept its rhythm. The power was still out.

“Your father?” she asked.

“He died three weeks after I arrived. I was there. We didn’t reconcile fully, but we sat in the same room. We watched a baseball game. He fell asleep. That was enough.”

She nodded. “I’m glad you went.”

“I should have told you.”

“You should have.”

They sat in the quiet. The lantern flickered again, the battery draining.

“I’m staying at the Sea Breeze Motel,” he said. “For a few days. I’m not sure where I’m headed after.”

She looked at him. She could say *come home with me* or *stay* or *I missed you*. But the words felt too clean for what was between them—layers of hurt and hope, like old paint.

“The laundromat might be open tomorrow,” she said instead. “If the power comes back.”

He understood. He picked up his mesh bag of sheets. “I’ll check the machine before I leave. In case it starts.”

She unfolded a towel and began folding it again, smoothing the creases with her palm. He walked to the dryer and pressed the button. Nothing.

“Tomorrow, then,” he said.

“Tomorrow.”

He left. The door sighed shut. She listened to his footsteps fade on the pavement, then became just the sound of the sea.

She folded the last towel and set it on the pile. Then she picked up the battery lantern and carried it with her to the door. She stood there a moment, looking out at the dark street, before stepping into the night and pulling the door closed behind her.

messages.content:
Write an 800–1,000 word technical article for backend engineers titled by you. Topic: designing rate limiting for a multi-tenant API used by small teams and enterprise customers.

You must cover:
1. The difference between authentication, authorization, quotas, and rate limits.
2. Token bucket vs. fixed window vs. sliding window at a practical level.
3. How to avoid punishing an entire tenant for one noisy integration.
4. What headers and error messages should be returned to API clients.
5. Observability: metrics, logs, and alerts that show whether limits are protecting reliability without blocking legitimate use.

Constraints: no code; no vendor-specific services; include a short “recommended baseline design” section at the end.

message.content:
# Throttle Wars: Designing Rate Limits That Don't Break Your Multi-Tenant API

Backend engineers love building APIs. We hate when they fall over. Rate limiting is the unsung hero of API reliability—but in a multi-tenant environment serving both small teams and enterprise customers, getting it wrong means either starving startups or enraging Fortune 500s. Let's design a system that scales with the tenant, not against them.

## 1. Four Concepts You Must Separate (Or Pay the Price)

Most engineers conflate these terms. They are not interchangeable.

**Authentication** answers "who are you?" It's the API key or OAuth token presented with each request. Without it, you can't even start rate limiting meaningfully.

**Authorization** answers "what are you allowed to do?" It's the permission model. A free-tier tenant might be forbidden from calling the `/batch` endpoint entirely. Authorization failures should return 403, not 429.

**Quotas** are cumulative, time-bound allowances. "Your plan includes 100,000 API calls per month." Quotas are enforced at the end of the billing period. They are not rate limits. A tenant can burn through their monthly quota in one hour—that's fine, but your infrastructure might not survive it.

**Rate limits** are burst-and-sustain controls. "You can send 50 requests per second." They protect your infrastructure from short-term spikes. A tenant with high monthly quota still needs polite per-second boundaries.

The failure mode when you conflate them: an enterprise customer who hit their monthly quota suddenly can't make any requests at all—even though their rate limit hasn't been breached. This creates cascading failures in their deployment pipeline. Don't do that.

## 2. Token Bucket vs. Fixed Window vs. Sliding Window: The Practical Reality

**Fixed Window** is the simplest: "500 requests per minute" resets every 60 seconds on the clock. Problem: a client sends 500 requests at 11:59:59, then another 500 at 12:00:01. They just did 1000 requests in 2 seconds without technically violating any single window. Your backend sees a thundering herd. This is the most common "works in testing, fails in production" pattern.

**Sliding Window Log** tracks timestamps of individual requests. It's accurate but memory-inefficient at scale. For 10,000 tenants each making 100 req/s, you're storing 1M timestamps per second. Not practical without aggressive pruning.

**Token Bucket** is the pragmatic winner. Start with a bucket capacity (burst) and a refill rate (sustain). A tenant gets 1000 tokens with a refill of 50 tokens per second. They can burst to 1000, then settle at 50/sec. If they go idle, the bucket fills back to 1000. This naturally handles both spikes and steady state. Redis-backed token buckets are the industry standard for good reason—they're cheap to store (one integer + one timestamp per tenant), fast (O(1) set/check), and handle the quiet-then-burst pattern that real APIs face.

**Sliding Window Counter** sits between fixed and token bucket in complexity. You store two counters: current window and previous window. Approximate request count = previous_window_count * (1 - elapsed_fraction) + current_window_count. It smooths out the fixed window edge case without storing every timestamp. Works well if your traffic is predictable. Token bucket is more forgiving for uneven load.

**Bottom line**: Start with token bucket. It's the most forgiving for clients and the most defensible for your infrastructure.

## 3. Protecting Tenants From Themselves—and From Each Other

The cardinal sin of multi-tenant rate limiting: a noisy integration in Tenant A's workspace brings down Tenant A entirely.

Consider: Tenant A has 500 API keys across 4 different integrations. One integration goes rogue—sends 10,000 requests in 30 seconds. If you rate-limit at the tenant level, that single integration exhausts the shared pool. Every other integration stops working. The customer calls support irate that "your API is down."

**Three tiers of isolation**:

First, **key-level rate limits** that are stricter than tenant-level limits. Each API key gets its own bucket—say 20 req/s. Tenant-level limit is 200 req/s across all keys. One misbehaving key hits its 20 req/s ceiling and gets 429'd. The other 19 keys continue working. The noisy integration self-isolates.

Second, **priority sub-buckets** for critical endpoints. If the tenant has a read endpoint and a write endpoint, give writes a separate bucket. A burst of read requests shouldn't block status updates or data exports.

Third, **concurrent request limits** (max in-flight) separate from rate limits (max arrival rate). A single integration sending 50 requests that each take 30 seconds to process can exhaust connection pools even at low request-per-second rates. Set max concurrent per key at 5 or 10. This prevents long-running queries from consuming all worker threads.

## 4. Headers and Error Messages: The Client Engineer's Frenemy

Bad rate limiting makes clients blind. Good rate limiting tells them exactly what to do.

**Required response headers** on every request:

```
X-RateLimit-Limit: 50
X-RateLimit-Remaining: 37
X-RateLimit-Reset: 1712345678
```

*Limit* is the maximum per window (usually a sliding window's effective rate). *Remaining* is the count of requests left in current window. *Reset* is the Unix timestamp when the window resets—critical for clients to know when they can try again.

**On 429 Too Many Requests**, add:

```
Retry-After: 12
```

This is the number of seconds the client should wait. Not the timestamp, not a request count. A single integer. Why? Because `sleep(retry_after)` is the simplest correct thing a client can do, and most client libraries handle this header natively.

**Error response body** should be JSON, but more importantly:

```json
{
  "error": "rate_limit_exceeded",
  "message": "API key key_abc123 exceeded 20 requests per second. Limit resets in 12 seconds.",
  "retry_after_seconds": 12,
  "key_limit": 20,
  "organization_id": "org_456"
}
```

Include the organization ID so client-side debugging can distinguish between "my key is throttled" versus "my org is throttled." Never return internal debug info (which bucket, which Redis shard). Be specific enough to debug, vague enough to not leak architecture.

## 5. Observability: Are You Protecting or Persecuting?

Rate limiting that you can't observe is guessing. Here's what matters:

**Metrics per tenant, per key, per endpoint**:

- `rate_limit_total`: total requests received
- `rate_limit_passed`: requests allowed through
- `rate_limit_blocked`: requests that returned 429
- `rate_limit_bucket_level`: current token count (0 = blocked, max = fully refilled)

Graph these over time. A tenant with consistent 0 bucket level is perpetually throttled—maybe their limits are too low. A tenant with bucket always at max is never throttled—maybe their limits are too generous.

**Metrics per shard/region** (not per vendor, but per logical partition):

- Request latency under throttling vs. not
- Error rate of the rate limiter itself (Redis timeouts, connection pool exhaustion)

**Alerts**:

- P50 latency increase >20% when rate limiting is active (your limiter might be too aggressive or your implementation slow)
- Any tenant hitting 429 >5% of their total requests for >15 minutes (they're either misconfigured or your limits are wrong)
- Rate limiter component error rate >0.1% (your infrastructure is failing)
- Same tenant hitting 429 on multiple keys simultaneously (possible DDoS or bug in your tenant-level limit)

**Logs**:

Every 429 should be logged with: tenant ID, key ID, endpoint, reason (key-level limit hit? tenant-level limit? concurrency limit?). Never log the full request body—that's a data leak. Do log request path and headers (user-agent especially—you'll see which libraries are misbehaving).

**Dashboards**:

Build one that shows "top 10 most throttled tenants" and "top 10 most blocked keys." If a single tenant dominates the blocked count, their limits might need adjustment—or they might have a buggy integration that needs a conversation.

## Recommended Baseline Design

1. **Token bucket algorithm** backed by Redis (one key per API key + one key per tenant). Burst = 2x sustained rate for enterprise, 1.5x for small teams.
2. **Two-tier limits**: per API key (stricter) and per tenant (aggregate). A key hitting its limit gets 429; the tenant's other keys continue working.
3. **Per-key concurrency limit** set to 10 for standard endpoints, 5 for compute-heavy endpoints.
4. **Three rate limit headers** on all responses. `Retry-After` header on all 429 responses with a human-readable JSON body containing the offending key ID and reset time.
5. **Separate rate limit buckets** for read vs. write endpoints. Writes get tighter limits to protect data integrity.
6. **Observability pipeline**: log every 429, metric every decision, alert when any tenant exceeds 5% 429 rate over 15 minutes or when the rate limiter component itself degrades.
7. **Quarterly review** of top-throttled tenants. Raise limits for legitimate usage patterns, reach out to abusive ones.

Rate limiting is a conversation with your customers, not a wall. When you separate authentication from authorization from quotas from rate limits, isolate noisy tenants gracefully, and expose clear signals, you build trust. Your API becomes known as reliable—not because it's slow, but because it's predictable. And in multi-tenant systems, predictability is reliability.

请帮我写一个单文件 HTML 水族箱动画。

要求：
1. 画面里有多条不同颜色、不同大小的鱼，游动要尽量自然。
2. 用户左键点击水族箱时，可以在点击位置放下一粒鱼食。
3. 每条鱼会追逐离自己最近的鱼食并尝试吃掉它；没有鱼食时恢复自由游动。
4. 需要有完整的 HTML、CSS 和 JavaScript，能直接保存为一个 `.html` 文件运行。
5. 视觉效果要精致一些，不要只给伪代码或解释，直接给出完整代码。

请帮我创建一个单文件 HTML 时钟应用，要求直接给出完整代码。

功能和视觉要求：
1. 时钟表盘是圆形，有刻度、数字、时针、分针、秒针，并在表盘内部显示当前时间和日期。
2. 表盘需要有简洁的高级感：白色背景、深色边框、轻微 3D 阴影。
3. 用 JavaScript 动态生成 60 个刻度，整点刻度更粗更深。
4. 时间必须转换为中国标准时间（北京时区，Asia/Shanghai）。
5. 写一个 updateClock() 函数，计算时针、分针、秒针角度；秒针需要尽量平滑运动。
6. 使用 setInterval 或 requestAnimationFrame 驱动刷新。
7. 代码需要兼容 Edge 浏览器。

请将下面的中文商务写作材料翻译成英文。要求：面向现代职场读者，语气清楚、专业、克制；不要翻译成生硬腔调；保留作者关于“商务信函应传达信息并建立善意”的核心观点。

素材来源：根据公共领域英文书《The Book of Business Etiquette》相关章节改写的中文素材
来源链接：https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/23025
版权/授权说明：Project Gutenberg lists the source eBook as public domain in the USA; this Chinese passage is a benchmark rewrite.

【待处理素材】
许多商务信函本不必写，而已经写出的信中，又有不少因为信息残缺、结构混乱，使原本两封信便能完成并归档的交易，拖成六封或八封往返。好的信件来自清楚的思考和周密的安排。销售函有时需要数周打磨；普通业务往来只需几分钟也足够。关键不在于花了多久，而在于最终写出的信是否能准确完成任务。

衡量商务信函的规则很多，但真正不可缺少的只有两点：第一，信必须让收件人明白你的意思；第二，它必须为公司建立善意。最好的信，是用最简短、最礼貌、最完整的方式做到这两点。每封信背后都应有明确理由，哪怕只是向客户说一声谢谢。许多精力浪费在无目的的广告、无效的推销和多余的来信上；这些文字占用了打字机和纸张，却没有推动任何事情前进。

一封好信不应让读者注意它本身的文采，而应让读者接收到它要传达的消息。某出版社曾寄出一批推销新书的测试信，回信者称赞信写得漂亮，却无人订购图书。这说明文字若抢走了信息的焦点，再优雅也不是成功的商务写作。信纸、信头、版式、段落都应整洁、克制、清楚；过分花哨的设计和故作新奇的格式，只会让人分心。

商务写作还应尊重读者的时间。清楚的标题、适当的称呼、按主题分段的正文、明确的请求和完整的联系方式，往往比华丽措辞更能建立信任。若一封信需要收件人回复，写信者应说明希望对方在何时以前回复、需要提供哪些信息，以及下一步将如何处理。这样，信件不仅传递态度，也推动事务向前。

message.content:
Many business letters should never have been written in the first place, and of those that are written, a significant number suffer from incomplete information or poor structure, turning what could have been settled in two letters into six or eight rounds of correspondence. Good letters come from clear thinking and careful planning. A sales letter may require weeks of refinement; a routine business reply may take only a few minutes. The key is not how long it takes, but whether the final letter accomplishes its task precisely.

There are many rules for measuring a business letter, but only two are truly indispensable: first, the letter must make your meaning clear to the recipient; second, it must build goodwill for the company. The best letters achieve both in the shortest, most courteous, and most complete way possible. Every letter should have a clear reason behind it, even if it is simply to say thank you to a client. Too much energy is wasted on aimless advertisements, ineffective solicitations, and unnecessary correspondence—words that occupy typewriters and paper without moving anything forward.

A good letter should not draw attention to its own style, but to the message it conveys. A publishing house once sent out a batch of test letters promoting a new book. Recipients praised the elegant writing, but no one placed an order. This shows that when the language overshadows the message, even the most graceful prose is not successful business writing. Paper, letterhead, layout, and paragraphs should be clean, restrained, and clear. Overly flashy design or contrived formatting only distracts.

Business writing should also respect the reader’s time. Clear subject lines, appropriate salutations, paragraphs organized by topic, explicit requests, and complete contact information build trust far more effectively than ornate phrasing. If a letter requires a response, the writer should state by when the reply is needed, what information is required, and what the next steps will be. In this way, a letter not only conveys an attitude but also moves the matter forward.

请将下面的繁体中文寓言/哲理片段翻译成英文，目标读者为青少年读者。要求：保留想象力和节奏；遇到典故不要长篇解释；译文应清楚、流畅，适合作为儿童文学或寓言文本阅读。

素材来源：《莊子的故事》节选：逍遥游开篇
来源链接：https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/23913
版权/授权说明：Project Gutenberg lists this eBook as public domain in the USA.

【待处理素材】
北冥有魚，其名為鯤。鯤之大，不知其幾千里也。化而為鳥，其名為鵬。鵬之背，不知其幾千里也；怒而飛，其翼若垂天之雲。是鳥也，海運則將徙於南冥。南冥者，天池也。齊諧者，志怪者也。諧之言曰：「鵬之徙於南冥也，水擊三千里，摶扶搖而上者九萬里，去以六月息者也。」野馬也，塵埃也，生物之以息相吹也。天之蒼蒼，其正色邪？其遠而無所至極邪？其視下也，亦若是則已矣。且夫水之積也不厚，則其負大舟也無力。覆杯水於坳堂之上，則芥為之舟；置杯焉則膠，水淺而舟大也。風之積也不厚，則其負大翼也無力。故九萬里，則風斯在下矣，而後乃今掊風；背負青天而莫之夭閼者，而後乃今將圖南。蜩與學鳩笑之曰：「我決起而飛，槍榆枋而止，時則不至而控於地而已矣，奚以這九萬里而南為？」適莽蒼者，三餐而反，腹猶果然；適百里者，宿舂糧；適千里者，三月聚糧。之二蟲又何知！小知不及大知，小年不及大年。奚以知其然也？朝菌不知晦朔，蟪蛄不知春秋，此小年也。楚之南有冥靈者，以五百歲為春，五百歲為秋；上古有大椿者，以八千歲為春，八千歲為秋。此大年也。而彭祖乃今以久特聞，眾人匹之，不亦悲乎！湯之問棘也是已。窮髮之北有冥海者，天池也。有魚焉，其廣數千里，未有知其脩者，其名為鯤。有鳥焉，其名為鵬，背若泰山，翼若垂天之雲，摶扶搖羊角而上者九萬里，絕雲氣，負青天，然後圖南，且適南冥也。斥鴳笑之曰：「彼且奚適也？我騰躍而上，不過數仞而下，翱翔蓬蒿之間，此亦飛之至也，而彼且奚適也？」此小大之辯也。故夫知效一官，行比一鄉，德合一君，而徵一國者，其自視也亦若此矣。而宋榮子猶然笑之。且舉世而譽之而不加勸，舉世而非之而不加沮，定乎內外之分，辯乎榮辱之竟，斯已矣。彼其於世，未數數然也。雖然，猶有未樹也。夫列子御風而行，泠然善也，旬有五日而反。彼於致福者，未數數然也。此雖免乎行，猶有所待者也。若夫乘天地之正，而御六氣之辯，以遊無窮者，彼且惡乎待哉！故曰：至人無己，神人無功，聖人無名。

message.content:
Here is the translation of the excerpt from *Zhuangzi’s Stories*, adapted for young readers with clarity, rhythm, and imagination.

---

**The Story of the Great Fish and the Giant Bird**

In the Northern Ocean there is a fish. Its name is Kun. How big is Kun? No one really knows—maybe a thousand miles, or even more. One day, this fish transforms into a bird. Its name is Peng. How wide is Peng’s back? Again, no one truly knows—perhaps a thousand miles or more. When this bird rises in anger, its wings spread like great clouds hanging from the sky.

When the seas move, this bird sets out on a journey to the Southern Ocean—the Heavenly Lake.

There is a book called *Qi Xie*, a collection of strange tales. It says: “When Peng journeys to the Southern Ocean, it stirs the water for three thousand miles as it takes off. It whirls upward on a great wind for ninety thousand miles, and it flies for six months before it rests.”

Think of the mist that rises like wild horses, the dust that drifts in the air—all things are blown about by the breath of life. The sky is so deep blue—is that its true color? Or is it just so far away that we cannot see the end? When Peng looks down from high above, maybe it sees the same thing—just a blur of blue.

If water is not deep enough, it cannot carry a large boat. Pour a cup of water into a hollow in the floor, and a mustard seed will float like a little boat. But put a cup there, and it will stick fast—the water is too shallow and the boat too big. In the same way, if the wind is not strong enough, it cannot carry the great wings of Peng. So to fly ninety thousand miles, the wind must be piled up beneath it. Then Peng, riding the wind, with the blue sky on its back and nothing blocking its way, can finally set off for the south.

A little cicada and a small dove laughed at Peng. “We take off whenever we feel like it,” they said. “We fly up to the elm or the sandalwood tree, and if we don’t make it, we just drop down to the ground. That’s good enough for us. Why bother going ninety thousand miles to the south?”

If you are only going to the nearby woods, you can eat three meals and come back home still full. If you are going a hundred miles, you must grind grain for the journey. If you are going a thousand miles, you need to prepare food for three months. Those two little creatures—what do they know?

Small knowledge cannot compare to great knowledge. A short life cannot understand a long life. How do we know this? A morning mushroom never sees the night. A summer cicada never knows the spring and autumn. These are short-lived creatures. But in the south of Chu, there is a tree called Mingling. For it, five hundred years are like one spring, and another five hundred years are like one autumn. Long, long ago, there was a great Chun tree. For it, eight thousand years were like one spring, and another eight thousand years were like one autumn. Those are long-lived creatures. Yet people today think of Peng Zu, who lived for a very long time, as someone special. Isn’t that sad?

Even the wise king Tang once heard such stories. Far away in the barren north, there is a dark sea—the Heavenly Lake. In it lives a fish. It is several thousand miles wide, and no one knows how long. Its name is Kun. There is also a bird there, named Peng. Its back is like Mount Tai, its wings like clouds hanging from the sky. It whirls upward on a spiral wind for ninety thousand miles, cutting through the clouds, carrying the blue sky on its back. Then it sets off for the south—straight to the Southern Ocean.

A little quail laughed at Peng. “Where does he think he’s going?” it said. “I jump up and down, fly a few yards, and then flutter around among the bushes. That’s the best flying there is! So where is he going?”

This is the difference between small and great.

So it is with people. A man who is clever enough to do a small job, or good enough to help a village, or wise enough to please a ruler, or famous enough to lead a whole country—he often looks at himself just like that little quail. But Master Song Rongzi would laugh at such a man. Even if the whole world praised him, he would not try harder. Even if the whole world blamed him, he would not feel sad. He knew the difference between inner self and outer things, and he understood the boundaries of honor and shame. Still, though he had come this far, he had not yet reached the highest level.

Then there was Master Liezi. He could ride the wind and travel, light and graceful. After fifteen days, he would return. He did not go around chasing good fortune. Still, though he no longer needed to walk, he still depended on something—the wind.

But what about someone who rides the true nature of heaven and earth, and moves with the six energies of the world, wandering freely through the endless universe? What need does such a person have to depend on anything?

**Therefore, the Perfect Man has no self. The Holy Man has no merit. The Sage has no name.**

---

I hope this version captures the wonder and rhythm of the original while being clear and enjoyable for young readers. Let me know if you'd like any adjustments!

请将下面的中文新闻稿素材翻译成英文。目标读者为普通英语新闻读者。要求：准确、自然、新闻稿风格；不要添加事实；保留机构名的含义，可译为 U.S. Geological Survey；不要使用夸张宣传语。

素材来源：改写自 U.S. Geological Survey 水循环图发布新闻稿
来源链接：https://www.usgs.gov/news/national-news-release/new-usgs-diagram-re-envisions-how-earths-most-precious-commodity-cycles
版权/授权说明：USGS-authored information is considered U.S. public domain; this Chinese source is a benchmark rewrite based on that public-domain material.

【待处理素材】
美国地质调查局发布新版水循环教学图，帮助教育者更完整地解释地球水在自然系统与人类活动之间的流动。新版图示取代了自2000年以来被大量课堂使用的旧版本。旧图多以一个大循环表现蒸发、降水、径流等自然过程，而新版把人类用水、土地利用、蓄水工程、灌溉和地下水开采等因素纳入同一张图中，展示许多小循环如何彼此连接、相互影响。

该图的开发团队咨询了一百多名教育工作者和三十多名水文学专家，并参考了美国地质调查局长期积累的水文数据。新版图示不仅说明水如何在大气、地表和地下储存、转移和改变形态，也强调水量、水质和水到达时间对可用水资源的影响。例如，降水并不会简单地从天空落下再回到海洋；一部分水会进入土壤和含水层，一部分会被植物吸收并通过蒸腾回到空气中，一部分会被水库、农田、城市排水系统和家庭用水改变路径。

设计者采用信息设计原则，让读者能顺着水的路径观察沿海平原、干旱盆地、湿润盆地和农业盆地之间的联系。图中还强调，不同区域的用水选择会影响下游水量和水质，因此理解水循环不能只看自然过程，也要观察人类活动如何改变水的储存位置、移动速度和清洁程度。新版图示最初提供英文和西班牙文版本，预期将由使用者翻译成更多语言。发布方表示，这一工具旨在帮助学生、教师和自然资源管理者以更清晰的方式理解水的复杂旅程，并认识到日常用水选择会影响水资源的可持续利用。

message.content:
**U.S. Geological Survey Releases Updated Water Cycle Diagram for Educators**

The U.S. Geological Survey has released a new educational diagram of the water cycle, designed to help educators provide a more complete explanation of how Earth’s water moves through both natural systems and human activities.

The updated diagram replaces a previous version that had been widely used in classrooms since 2000. While the older diagram primarily depicted the natural processes of evaporation, precipitation, and runoff as a single large cycle, the new version incorporates human water use, land use, water storage infrastructure, irrigation, and groundwater extraction into a single illustration. It shows how many smaller cycles are connected and influence one another.

To develop the diagram, the team consulted more than 100 educators and over 30 hydrology experts, and drew on long-term hydrological data collected by the U.S. Geological Survey. The updated diagram not only illustrates how water is stored, transferred, and transformed in the atmosphere, on the surface, and underground, but also emphasizes how water quantity, water quality, and the timing of water availability affect usable water resources.

For example, precipitation does not simply fall from the sky and return to the ocean. Some water enters soils and aquifers, some is absorbed by plants and released back into the atmosphere through transpiration, and some is diverted by reservoirs, farmland, urban drainage systems, and household use.

The designers applied principles of information design to allow readers to follow water pathways across coastal plains, arid basins, humid basins, and agricultural basins. The diagram also highlights that water use choices in different regions can affect downstream water quantity and quality. Understanding the water cycle therefore requires looking not only at natural processes but also at how human activities alter where water is stored, how fast it moves, and how clean it is.

The new diagram is initially available in English and Spanish, and it is expected that users will translate it into additional languages. According to the agency, the tool is intended to help students, teachers, and natural resource managers better understand the complex journey of water and recognize that daily water use choices affect the sustainable availability of water resources.

请将下面的繁体中文小说片段翻译成英文。要求：保留人物心理、礼仪分寸和环境细节；英文要自然、有文学感，但不要过度现代化；人名可采用拼音，例如 Lin Daiyu、Jia residence。不要添加解释性段落。

素材来源：《紅樓夢》节选：林黛玉进贾府
来源链接：https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/24264
版权/授权说明：Project Gutenberg lists this eBook as public domain in the USA.

【待处理素材】
且說黛玉自那日棄舟登岸時，便有榮國府打發了轎子并拉行李的車輛久候了．這林黛玉常听得母親說過，他外祖母家与別家不同．他近日所見的這几個三等仆婦，吃穿用度，已是不凡了，何況今至其家．因此步步留心，時時在意，不肯輕易多說一句話，多行一步路，惟恐被人恥笑了他去．自上了轎，進入城中從紗窗向外瞧了一瞧，其街市之繁華，人煙之阜盛，自与別處不同．又行了半日，忽見街北蹲著兩個大石獅子，三間獸頭大門，門前列坐著十來個華冠麗服之人．正門卻不開，只有東西兩角門有人出入．正門之上有一匾，匾上大書"敕造宁國府"五個大字．黛玉想道：這必是外祖之長房了．想著，又往西行，不多遠，照樣也是三間大門，方是榮國府了．卻不進正門，只進了西邊角門．那轎夫抬進去，走了一射之地，將轉彎時，便歇下退出去了．后面的婆子們已都下了轎，赶上前來．另換了三四個衣帽周全十七八歲的小廝上來，复抬起轎子．眾婆子步下圍隨至一垂花門前落下．眾小廝退出，眾婆子上來打起轎帘，扶黛玉下轎．林黛玉扶著婆子的手，進了垂花門，兩邊是抄手游廊，當中是穿堂，當地放著一個紫檀架子大理石的大插屏．轉過插屏，小小的三間廳，廳后就是后面的正房大院．正面五間上房，皆雕梁畫棟，兩邊穿山游廊廂房，挂著各色鸚鵡，畫眉等鳥雀．台磯之上，坐著几個穿紅著綠的丫頭，一見他們來了，便忙都笑迎上來，說：“剛才老太太還念呢，可巧就來了。”于是三四人爭著打起帘籠，一面听得人回話：“林姑娘到了。”黛玉方進入房時，只見兩個人攙著一位鬢發如銀的老母迎上來，黛玉便知是他外祖母．方欲拜見時，早被他外祖母一把摟入怀中，心肝儿肉叫著大哭起來．當下地下侍立之人，無不掩面涕泣，黛玉也哭個不住．一時眾人慢慢解勸住了，黛玉方拜見了外祖母．____此即冷子興所云之史氏太君，賈赦賈政之母也．當下賈母一一指与黛玉：“這是你大舅母，這是你二舅母，這是你先珠大哥的媳婦珠大嫂子。”黛玉一一拜見過．賈母又說：“請姑娘們來．今日遠客才來，可以不必上學去了。”眾人答應了一聲，便去了兩個．不一時，只見三個奶嬤嬤并五六個丫鬟，簇擁著三個姊妹來了．第一個肌膚微丰，合中身材，腮凝新荔，鼻膩鵝脂，溫柔沉默，觀之可親．第二個削肩細腰，長挑身材，鴨蛋臉面，俊眼修眉，顧盼神飛，文彩精華，見之忘俗．第三個身量未足，形容尚小．其釵環裙襖，三人皆是一樣的妝飾．黛玉忙起身迎上來見禮，互相廝認過，大家歸了坐．丫鬟們斟上茶來．不過說些黛玉之母如何得病，如何請醫服藥，如何送死發喪．不免賈母又傷感起來，因說：“我這些儿女，所疼者獨有你母，今日一旦先舍我而去，連面也不能一見，今見了你，我怎不傷心！

message.content: And so it was that on the day Lin Daiyu left her boat and set foot on shore, the Rongguo Mansion had already sent a sedan-chair and carts for her luggage, which had been waiting for some time. This Lin Daiyu had often heard her mother speak of how her grandmother’s household was unlike others. The few third-class serving women she had seen of late, in their dress and bearing, were already beyond the ordinary; how much more so, then, would be the household itself? Thus she watched every step, heeded every moment, unwilling to speak a word too many or take a step too far, for fear that she might be laughed at. Once she had entered the sedan-chair and passed into the city, she looked out through the gauze window and saw that the streets were bustling with activity and thronged with people—a scene unlike any she had seen before. After traveling for some time, she suddenly noticed two great stone lions crouching at the northern side of the street, and beyond them a triple gate with beast-head ornaments. Before the gate sat more than a dozen men in splendid caps and fine robes. The main gate was closed; only the eastern and western side gates showed signs of passage. Above the main gate hung a horizontal tablet, inscribed in large characters: “Imperial-built Ningguo Mansion.” Daiyu thought to herself: This must be the senior branch of my grandfather’s house. As she pondered, the procession moved westward, and before long, another triple gate of the same kind appeared—this was the Rongguo Mansion. They did not enter by the main gate, however, but only by the western side gate. The sedan-chair bearers carried her inside for a distance of about a bowshot, then, when they were about to turn, set the chair down and withdrew. The old women who had been following behind had already descended from their own chairs and hurried forward. In their place, three or four young lads of seventeen or eighteen, neatly dressed and properly capped, lifted the sedan-chair once more. The old women, on foot, surrounded it and followed until they came to a gate with hanging flowers, where the chair was set down. The lads withdrew, and the old women stepped forward, raised the chair’s curtain, and helped Daiyu descend. Leaning on the arm of one of the old women, Lin Daiyu passed through the hanging-flower gate. On either side ran covered walkways, and in the center lay an open passage. Just there stood a large screen of marble set in a frame of purple sandalwood. Passing around the screen, she came upon a small three-bay hall, behind which lay the main courtyard and its principal rooms. Facing her were five main apartments, all with carved beams and painted rafters, and on either side ran covered galleries connecting the side chambers, where hung cages of parrots, thrushes, and other birds. On the steps sat several maids in red and green, who, upon seeing them approach, hurried forward with smiles and said, “Just now the old lady was speaking of you—how fortunate that you have arrived!” And so three or four of them vied with one another to raise the door-curtains, while a voice was heard reporting within: “Miss Lin has come.” As Daiyu entered the room, she saw an old woman with hair as silver as frost, supported by two attendants, coming forward to greet her. Daiyu knew at once that this was her grandmother. She was about to make her bows when the old lady caught her in her arms and, crying out “My heart! My flesh!” burst into loud sobs. All those who stood in attendance could not help but cover their faces and weep, and Daiyu herself wept without end. After some time, the company gradually coaxed them into calm, and then Daiyu was able to pay her respects to her grandmother. (This was the Lady Dowager known as née Shi, whom Leng Zixing had mentioned—the mother of Jia She and Jia Zheng.) At once, the Lady Dowager pointed out the others to Daiyu, saying: “This is your elder uncle’s wife; this is your second uncle’s wife; this is the wife of your late elder brother Zhu, whom you may call Sister-in-law Zhu.” Daiyu greeted each one in turn. Then the Lady Dowager said: “Summon the young ladies. Today a guest has come from afar—they need not go to school.” With a chorus of assent, two maids departed. Before long, three nurses and five or six maids were seen clustering around three sisters as they entered. The first was somewhat plump in figure, of medium height, with cheeks like fresh lychee and a nose as smooth as goose-fat; gentle and quiet in manner, she seemed approachable and kind. The second had sloping shoulders and a slender waist, a tall and graceful form, an oval face, fine eyes and long brows, a gaze full of spirit and intelligence; her refined bearing and literary elegance made one forget all vulgar thoughts. The third was still young, not yet fully grown in stature. In their hairpins, bracelets, skirts, and jackets, the three sisters were dressed alike. Daiyu rose quickly and went forward to greet them, and after they had all recognized one another, they took their seats. The maids poured tea. Then they spoke of nothing but how Daiyu’s mother had fallen ill, how doctors had been called and medicines administered, how the funeral had been conducted, and how the mourning had been observed. The Lady Dowager could not help but grow sad again, and said: “Of all my children, the one I loved most was your mother. And now, before my time, she has left me—I could not even see her face once more. When I see you now, how can I not be grieved!”
