爱丽丝挨着姐姐坐在河岸上，实在无聊透顶：她偷偷朝姐姐读的书瞄了一两眼，可那书上既没有图画也没有对话，“一本书要是没有图画和对话，”爱丽丝心里想，“那还有什么意思呢？”

于是她暗自琢磨（大热天让她昏昏沉沉、脑子发木，能想清楚也不容易）：编个雏菊花环的乐趣，值不值得她费劲站起来去摘雏菊？正想着，一只粉红眼睛的白兔突然从她身边跑过。

这事儿倒也没什么稀奇；听见兔子自言自语“哎呀！哎呀！我恐怕要迟到啦！”爱丽丝也没觉得有什么不对劲（事后回想起来，她倒觉得自己本该惊讶才是，可当时这一切看起来都挺自然的）；可当那只兔子真从背心口袋里掏出一块怀表看了看，又急匆匆往前赶时，爱丽丝一下跳了起来——她猛然想起，自己从没见过兔子穿背心口袋，更没见过兔子从口袋里掏怀表。好奇心烧得火辣辣的，她撒腿就跑过田野追了上去，幸好刚好看见那兔子钻进了篱笆下一个大兔子洞。

紧接着，爱丽丝也跟着跳了进去，压根儿没想过自己到底要怎么出来。

那兔子洞起初像隧道一样笔直延伸了一段，然后突然向下陡降——陡得爱丽丝还没顾上收住脚步，就发现自己掉进一口很深的井里了。

也许井太深，也许她下坠得特别慢——总之往下落的时候，她有足够的时间四下张望，琢磨接下去会发生什么。她先想往下看，弄清楚自己会掉到什么地方，可底下太黑，什么也看不见；于是她打量起井壁，发现上面摆满了小柜子和书架：这儿那儿还挂着地图和图画，用钉子钉在墙上。她经过一个架子时顺手拿下一个罐子，标签上写着“橙子果酱”——可让她大失所望的是，罐子是空的：她没敢把罐子扔下去，怕砸到下面的人，于是趁自己飘过一个小柜子时，设法把它放了进去。

“好吧！”爱丽丝心想，“摔了这一跤以后，再滚下楼梯我可不在乎了！家里人准会觉得我特勇敢！哼，就算我从房顶上栽下来，我也不会吭一声的！”（这话倒很可能是真的。）

往下掉，往下掉，往下掉。这坠落难道永远没完了吗？“我不知这会儿掉了几英里啦？”她大声说，“我肯定快到地心了吧。让我想想：那应该往下四千英里左右——”要知道，爱丽丝在课堂上学过这类知识，虽然这会儿没人听她显摆，算不上什么好机会，可拿这些话念念还是很好的练习——“——对，差不多就是那个距离——可我现在到了什么纬度和经度呢？”（爱丽丝压根儿不知道纬度和经度是什么，但她觉得这两个词儿念起来挺神气的。）

messages.content:
Translate the following English news-release excerpt into Simplified Chinese. Requirements: accurate, natural, suitable for a general news audience; keep the institutional name as “美国地质调查局（USGS）” on first mention; do not add facts or commentary.

素材来源：U.S. Geological Survey news release: New USGS diagram re-envisions how Earth’s water cycles the planet
来源链接：https://www.usgs.gov/news/national-news-release/new-usgs-diagram-re-envisions-how-earths-most-precious-commodity-cycles
版权/授权说明：USGS-authored information is considered U.S. public domain.

【待处理素材】
RESTON, Va.— Starting today, educators around the nation will have a more accurate and more comprehensive tool to explain the Earth’s water cycle with the unveiling of the new U.S. Geological Survey water cycle diagram.

The revised version replaces one used by hundreds of thousands of educators and students internationally every year since 2000. So why the new water cycle? This depiction brings humans into the picture, showing the water cycle as a complex interplay of small, interconnected cycles that people interact with and influence, rather than one big circle.

USGS experts consulted with more than 100 educators and more than 30 hydrologic experts to develop the new diagram. The vast amounts of water data that USGS has collected in recent decades has informed a nuanced perspective of the water cycle, demonstrating how both its human and natural components are interconnected.

Where the existing water cycle diagram depicted only the natural aspects of the cycle, the new version depicts how Earth’s water moves and is stored, both naturally and because of human actions. Not only does the new diagram illustrate a more comprehensive view of the water cycle, it draws on principles of information design to focus attention on the water as it moves through the natural and built environment. It shows how multiple ecosystems—including a coastal plain, dry basin, wet basin, and agricultural basin—are connected across watersheds and at continental scales.

The new diagram will initially be available in both English and Spanish, with the expectation it will be translated into many other languages by end users, as was the previous version.

message.content:
弗吉尼亚州雷斯顿——自即日起，全美教育工作者将拥有更准确、更全面的工具来解释地球水循环，这得益于新版美国地质调查局（USGS）水循环示意图的发布。

此次修订版替换了自2000年以来每年被全球数十万教育工作者和学生使用的旧版示意图。为何要推出新版水循环图？新图将人类活动纳入画面，展示水循环并非单一的大循环，而是由人类参与并影响的多个相互关联的小循环构成的复杂互动体系。

美国地质调查局（USGS）专家在绘制新示意图时，咨询了100多位教育工作者和30多位水文学专家。该机构近几十年来收集的大量水数据，为其提供了对水循环的细致视角，揭示了人类活动与自然要素如何相互关联。

旧版水循环示意图仅描绘自然循环过程，而新版则展示了地球水如何以自然方式及因人类活动而流动和储存。新图不仅呈现了更全面的水循环视角，还运用信息设计原理，将注意力聚焦于水在自然与人工环境中的流动过程。它展示了多个生态系统——包括海岸平原、干旱盆地、湿润盆地和农业盆地——如何跨流域、在洲际尺度上相互连接。

新版示意图将首先以英语和西班牙语版本发布，预计后续将由终端用户翻译成多种其他语言，正如旧版示意图的翻译推广模式。

messages.content:
Translate the following English literary passage into Simplified Chinese. Requirements: preserve emotional tension, social nuance, and indirect narration; do not make the style slangy; names may be translated conventionally or kept in English consistently. Do not add explanations.

素材来源：Jane Austen, Pride and Prejudice, Chapter XXXIV excerpt
来源链接：https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/1342
版权/授权说明：Project Gutenberg lists this eBook as public domain in the USA.

【待处理素材】
When they were gone, Elizabeth, as if intending to exasperate herself as much as possible against Mr. Darcy, chose for her employment the examination of all the letters which Jane had written to her since her being in Kent. They contained no actual complaint, nor was there any revival of past occurrences, or any communication of present suffering. But in all, and in almost every line of each, there was a want of that cheerfulness which had been used to characterize her style, and which, proceeding from the serenity of a mind at ease with itself, and kindly disposed towards everyone, had been scarcely ever clouded. Elizabeth noticed every sentence conveying the idea of uneasiness, with an attention which it had hardly received on the first perusal. Mr. Darcy’s shameful boast of what misery he had been able to inflict gave her a keener sense of her sister’s sufferings. It was some consolation to think that his visit to Rosings was to end on the day after the next, and a still greater that in less than a fortnight she should herself be with Jane again, and enabled to contribute to the recovery of her spirits, by all that affection could do.

She could not think of Darcy’s leaving Kent without remembering that his cousin was to go with him; but Colonel Fitzwilliam had made it clear that he had no intentions at all, and, agreeable as he was, she did not mean to be unhappy about him.

While settling this point, she was suddenly roused by the sound of the door-bell; and her spirits were a little fluttered by the idea of its being Colonel Fitzwilliam himself, who had once before called late in the evening, and might now come to inquire particularly after her. But this idea was soon banished, and her spirits were very differently affected, when, to her utter amazement, she saw Mr. Darcy walk into the room. In a hurried manner he immediately began an inquiry after her health, imputing his visit to a wish of hearing that she were better. She answered him with cold civility. He sat down for a few moments, and then getting up walked about the room. Elizabeth was surprised, but said not a word. After a silence of several minutes, he came towards her in an agitated manner, and thus began:--

“In vain have I struggled. It will not do. My feelings will not be repressed. You must allow me to tell you how ardently I admire and love you.”

Elizabeth’s astonishment was beyond expression. She stared, coloured, doubted, and was silent. This he considered sufficient encouragement, and the avowal of all that he felt and had long felt for her immediately followed. He spoke well; but there were feelings besides those of the heart to be detailed, and he was not more eloquent on the subject of tenderness than of pride. His sense of her inferiority, of its being a degradation, of the family obstacles which judgment had always opposed to inclination, were dwelt on with a warmth which seemed due to the consequence he was wounding, but was very unlikely to recommend his suit.

In spite of her deeply-rooted dislike, she could not be insensible to the compliment of such a man’s affection, and though her intentions did not vary for an instant, she was at first sorry for the pain he was to receive; till roused to resentment by his subsequent language, she lost all compassion in anger. She tried, however, to compose herself to answer him with patience, when he should have done. He concluded with representing to her the strength of that attachment which in spite of all his endeavours he had found impossible to conquer; and with expressing his hope that it would now be rewarded by her acceptance of his hand. As he said this she could easily see that he had no doubt of a favourable answer. He _spoke_ of apprehension and anxiety, but his countenance expressed real security. Such a circumstance could only exasperate farther; and when he ceased the colour rose into her cheeks and she said,--

“In such cases as this, it is, I believe, the established mode to express a sense of obligation for the sentiments avowed, however unequally they may be returned. It is natural that obligation should be felt, and if I could _feel_ gratitude, I would now thank you. But I cannot--I have never desired your good opinion, and you have certainly bestowed it most unwillingly. I am sorry to have occasioned pain to anyone. It has been most unconsciously done, however, and I hope will be of short duration. The feelings which you tell me have long prevented the acknowledgment of your regard can have little difficulty in overcoming it after this explanation.”

message.content:
两人走后，伊丽莎白仿佛存心要激发出自己对达西先生的最大恶感，便挑出简自来到肯特郡后写给她的所有信件，逐一检视。信中没有一句实际的怨言，既未重提旧事，也未倾诉当下的苦楚。但每封信、几乎每一行字里，都少了那种曾是她笔调特有的欢快——那种源自内心安宁、与己相安、与人为善、几乎从未蒙上阴翳的欢快。伊丽莎白留意着每一句透露出不安的话语，那份专注甚至超过了初读之时。达西先生那番无耻的自夸——说他曾施加了何等痛苦——让她更敏锐地感知到姐姐所受的折磨。想到他后天便要离开罗新斯，她略感宽慰；而更大的安慰是，不到两周自己便能再见到简，并可以用全部的关爱帮助她重拾欢颜。

她一想到达西离开肯特郡，便不免记起他的表兄也要同行；但费茨威廉上校已表明自己毫无此意，况且他虽讨人喜欢，她也不打算为他郁郁寡欢。

正思索至此，门铃声忽然惊动了她；她心头微微一跳，以为是费茨威廉上校本人——他曾有一次在深夜来访，此刻或许是特意来问候她。但这念头很快便被打消，而她的心绪也截然不同了——当她万分惊愕地看见达西先生走进房间时。他匆匆忙忙，立刻问候她的健康，声称来访是希望听到她好转的消息。她以冷淡的礼貌作答。他坐了片刻，随即起身在房里踱步。伊丽莎白虽感诧异，却一言不发。沉默了几分钟后，他激动地朝她走来，开口说道：

“我挣扎了也是徒劳。这不行。我的感情无法压抑。请你允许我告诉你，我是多么热烈地倾慕你、爱你。”

伊丽莎白惊愕得无以言表。她瞪着眼，红了脸，满心怀疑，默默不语。他以为这已是足够的鼓励，便接着倾诉了自己对她所怀有的、长期以来的全部情感。他说得很动听；但除了内心的情感，还需细述其他方面——他在柔情上的口才，并不比在骄傲上更具说服力。他提到她出身低微、这桩婚事是一种屈辱、理智始终以门第之见来对抗情意——谈及这些时，他语气激烈，仿佛因伤害了自身的身价而理当如此，却极不利于赢得她的芳心。

尽管她对他深恶痛绝，但面对这样一个人的爱情所带来的恭维，她并非无动于衷；虽然她的心意一刻也未曾动摇，但起初仍为他即将承受的痛苦感到些许遗憾；直到被他后来的话语激怒，她才在愤怒中失去了所有的同情。然而，她还是努力镇定下来，打算等他说完后，耐心作答。他在话末向她表明，这份爱意虽历经他的一切努力，却终究无法征服；并表达希望此刻能得到她允婚的回报。说这话时，她不难看出他毫不怀疑会得到肯定的答复。他嘴上说着惶恐与焦虑，但脸上却流露出十足的把握。这种情形只会让她更加恼怒；等他停下时，她双颊泛红，开口说道：

“在这种情形下，我相信通常的做法是：对对方表露的情感表示感激，无论这些情感可能得到多么不平等的回应。感到感激是自然的，而如果我能生出感激之心，此刻便会向你道谢。但我做不到——我从未希求过你的好感，而你无疑也是极不情愿地给予了我。我很抱歉给任何人带来了痛苦。这完全是出于无意，但愿它不会持续太久。你告诉我，你的感情长久以来阻碍了你表露心意——那么经过此番解释之后，要克服它应该不难了。”

messages.content:
Translate the following technical explainer into Simplified Chinese. Requirements: preserve the technical meaning, use clear science-education language, and keep terms such as evaporation, evapotranspiration, precipitation, runoff, infiltration, groundwater recharge, and aquifer accurate. Do not add external information.

素材来源：U.S. Geological Survey Water Science School: Water cycle
来源链接：https://www.usgs.gov/water-science-school/water-cycle
版权/授权说明：USGS-authored information is considered U.S. public domain.

【待处理素材】
The water cycle describes where water is on Earth and how it moves. Human water use, land use, and climate change all impact the water cycle. By understanding these impacts, we can work toward using water sustainably.

Viewed from space, the most striking feature of our planet is the water. In both liquid and frozen form, it covers 75% of the Earth's surface. It fills the sky with clouds. Water is practically everywhere on Earth, from inside the planet's rocky crust to inside the cells of the human body. What's important to keep in mind is that all of this water is in constant motion across our planet.

Water is stored in the atmosphere, on the land surface, and below the ground. It can be a liquid, a solid, or a gas. Liquid water can be fresh or saline. Water moves between the places it is stored. Water moves at large scales, through watersheds, the atmosphere, and below the Earth's surface. Water moves at very small scales too. It is in us, plants, and other organisms.

Oceans store 96% of all water on Earth. Ocean water is saline, meaning it’s salty. On land, saline water is stored in saline lakes. The rest of the water on Earth is fresh water. Fresh water is stored in liquid form in freshwater lakes, artificial reservoirs, rivers, and wetlands. Water is stored in solid, frozen form in ice sheets and glaciers, and in snowpack at high elevations or near Earth's poles.

As it moves, water can change form between liquid, solid, and gas. Circulation mixes water in the oceans and transports water vapor in the atmosphere. Water moves between the atmosphere and the surface through evaporation, evapotranspiration, and precipitation. Water moves across the surface through snowmelt, runoff, and streamflow. Water moves into the ground through infiltration and groundwater recharge. Underground, groundwater flows within aquifers. Groundwater can return to the surface through natural discharge into rivers, the ocean, and from springs.

Water moves naturally and because of human actions. Energy from the sun and the force of gravity drive the continual movement of water between pools. The sun’s energy causes liquid water to evaporate into water vapor. Evapotranspiration is the main way water moves into the atmosphere from the land surface and oceans. Gravity causes water to flow downward on land. It causes rain, snow, and hail to fall from clouds.

Humans and other organisms rely on water for life. The amount of water that is available depends on how much water there is in each pool, when and how fast water moves through the water cycle, and how clean the water is. By understanding human impacts on the water cycle, we can work toward using water sustainably.

message.content:
**水循环详解**

水循环描述了水在地球上的分布及其运动方式。人类用水、土地利用和气候变化都会影响水循环。通过理解这些影响，我们能够朝着可持续用水的方向努力。

从太空俯瞰，地球最显著的特征便是水。液态或固态的水覆盖了地球表面75%的面积，并汇聚成天空中的云层。实际上，水几乎遍布地球各处——从地壳岩石内部到人体细胞之中。需牢记的关键是，所有这些水都在地球上周而复始地持续运动。

水储存于大气、地表和地下。它可以呈液态、固态或气态。液态水既可是淡水，也可是咸水。水在不同储存场所之间迁移。大规模的运动发生在流域、大气层及地表之下；同时，水也在极其微小的尺度上运动——存在于我们体内、植物及其他生物体之中。

海洋储存了地球上96%的水。海水是咸的，即含盐。在陆地上，咸水储存于咸水湖中。地球上的其余水为淡水。淡水以液态形式储存于淡水湖、人工水库、河流和湿地；以固态冻结形式储存于冰盖、冰川以及高海拔或靠近两极的积雪中。

水在运动过程中可在液态、固态和气态之间转换。洋流混合了海水，大气环流输送了水蒸气。水通过蒸发、蒸散发和降水在大气与地表之间运动；通过融雪、径流和河川径流在地表横向运动；通过下渗和地下水补给进入地下。在地下，地下水在含水层中流动。地下水可通过自然排泄返回地表，进入河流、海洋或从泉眼中涌出。

水既自然运动，也因人类活动而运动。太阳能和重力驱动着水在各蓄水库之间持续不断的运动。太阳能使液态水蒸发为水蒸气。蒸散发是水从地表和海洋进入大气的主要途径。重力使水在陆地上向下流动，并使雨、雪和冰雹从云层中降落。

人类及其他生物依赖水以维持生命。可用的水量取决于每个蓄水库中的水量、水在水循环中运动的时间和速度，以及水的洁净程度。通过理解人类活动对水循环的影响，我们能够朝着可持续用水的方向努力。

messages.content:
Write a single-file Python 3 command-line program named `schedule_optimizer.py`. Use no third-party packages. Keep the code under 1,000 lines.

Problem: Given a list of candidate work sessions, choose a non-overlapping subset that maximizes total value. Each session has an ID, start time, end time, and value. This is weighted interval scheduling with reconstruction.

Input: read JSON from standard input. Format:
{
  "sessions": [
    {"id": "a", "start": "2026-05-01T09:00:00", "end": "2026-05-01T10:30:00", "value": 5}
  ]
}

Requirements:
1. Parse ISO-like timestamps without time zones using Python standard library.
2. Reject sessions whose end is not after start or whose value is negative.
3. Output JSON with `max_value`, `selected_ids` in chronological order, and `rejected` entries with reasons.
4. If multiple optimal solutions exist, choose the one with fewer sessions; if still tied, choose lexicographically smaller list of IDs.
5. Include an efficient O(n log n) dynamic programming solution.
6. Include built-in tests. When run with `--test`, execute tests and print a short success message. Normal mode should print only JSON.

Output only the complete Python code. Do not include explanations.

message.content:
```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import sys
import json
from datetime import datetime

def parse_timestamp(ts_str):
    """Parse ISO-like timestamp without timezone info."""
    return datetime.strptime(ts_str, "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S")

class Session:
    def __init__(self, session_dict):
        self.id = session_dict["id"]
        self.start = parse_timestamp(session_dict["start"])
        self.end = parse_timestamp(session_dict["end"])
        self.value = session_dict["value"]

def optimize_sessions(sessions):
    """Weighted interval scheduling with tie-breaking."""
    rejected = []
    valid = []
    
    for s in sessions:
        reasons = []
        if s.end <= s.start:
            reasons.append("end not after start")
        if s.value < 0:
            reasons.append("negative value")
        if reasons:
            rejected.append({"id": s.id, "reasons": reasons})
        else:
            valid.append(s)
    
    if not valid:
        return 0, [], rejected
    
    # Sort by end time
    valid.sort(key=lambda x: x.end)
    
    # Binary search for latest non-conflicting session
    def latest_non_conflict(i):
        lo, hi = 0, i - 1
        while lo <= hi:
            mid = (lo + hi) // 2
            if valid[mid].end <= valid[i].start:
                if mid == hi or valid[mid+1].end > valid[i].start:
                    return mid
                lo = mid + 1
            else:
                hi = mid - 1
        return -1
    
    n = len(valid)
    dp = [0] * (n + 1)
    choice = [None] * (n + 1)  # None means skip, True means take
    count = [0] * (n + 1)      # number of sessions in optimal solution
    
    # dp[i] = max value using first i sessions (sorted by end)
    for i in range(1, n + 1):
        # Option 1: skip session i-1
        skip_val = dp[i-1]
        skip_count = count[i-1]
        
        # Option 2: take session i-1
        p = latest_non_conflict(i-1) + 1  # convert to 1-based index
        take_val = valid[i-1].value + dp[p]
        take_count = count[p] + 1
        
        # Compare: higher value first, then fewer sessions, then lexicographic IDs
        if take_val > skip_val:
            dp[i] = take_val
            count[i] = take_count
            choice[i] = True
        elif take_val < skip_val:
            dp[i] = skip_val
            count[i] = skip_count
            choice[i] = None
        else:
            if take_count < skip_count:
                dp[i] = take_val
                count[i] = take_count
                choice[i] = True
            elif take_count > skip_count:
                dp[i] = skip_val
                count[i] = skip_count
                choice[i] = None
            else:
                # Build sequences to compare lexicographically
                take_ids = []
                j = i
                temp_j = i
                while temp_j > 0:
                    if choice[temp_j] is True:
                        take_ids.append(valid[temp_j-1].id)
                        p_inner = latest_non_conflict(temp_j-1) + 1
                        temp_j = p_inner
                    else:
                        temp_j -= 1
                take_ids.reverse()
                
                skip_ids = []
                j = i
                temp_j = i
                while temp_j > 0:
                    if choice[temp_j] is True:
                        skip_ids.append(valid[temp_j-1].id)
                        p_inner = latest_non_conflict(temp_j-1) + 1
                        temp_j = p_inner
                    else:
                        temp_j -= 1
                skip_ids.reverse()
                
                if take_ids < skip_ids:
                    dp[i] = take_val
                    count[i] = take_count
                    choice[i] = True
                else:
                    dp[i] = skip_val
                    count[i] = skip_count
                    choice[i] = None
    
    # Reconstruct
    selected_ids = []
    i = n
    while i > 0:
        if choice[i] is True:
            selected_ids.append(valid[i-1].id)
            p = latest_non_conflict(i-1) + 1
            i = p
        else:
            i -= 1
    selected_ids.reverse()
    
    return dp[n], selected_ids, rejected

def run_tests():
    """Built-in tests."""
    test_cases = []
    
    # Test 1: basic
    test_cases.append((
        [{"id": "a", "start": "2026-05-01T09:00:00", "end": "2026-05-01T10:30:00", "value": 5},
         {"id": "b", "start": "2026-05-01T10:00:00", "end": "2026-05-01T11:00:00", "value": 3},
         {"id": "c", "start": "2026-05-01T11:00:00", "end": "2026-05-01T12:00:00", "value": 4}],
        9, ["a", "c"], []
    ))
    
    # Test 2: reject invalid
    test_cases.append((
        [{"id": "x", "start": "2026-05-01T10:00:00", "end": "2026-05-01T09:00:00", "value": 5},
         {"id": "y", "start": "2026-05-01T09:00:00", "end": "2026-05-01T10:00:00", "value": -1}],
        0, [], [{"id": "x", "reasons": ["end not after start"]},
                {"id": "y", "reasons": ["negative value"]}]
    ))
    
    # Test 3: tie-breaking - fewer sessions
    test_cases.append((
        [{"id": "a", "start": "2026-05-01T09:00:00", "end": "2026-05-01T10:00:00", "value": 5},
         {"id": "b", "start": "2026-05-01T10:00:00", "end": "2026-05-01T11:00:00", "value": 5},
         {"id": "c", "start": "2026-05-01T09:00:00", "end": "2026-05-01T11:00:00", "value": 10}],
        10, ["c"], []
    ))
    
    # Test 4: tie-breaking - lexicographic
    test_cases.append((
        [{"id": "b", "start": "2026-05-01T09:00:00", "end": "2026-05-01T10:00:00", "value": 5},
         {"id": "a", "start": "2026-05-01T10:00:00", "end": "2026-05-01T11:00:00", "value": 5},
         {"id": "c", "start": "2026-05-01T11:00:00", "end": "2026-05-01T12:00:00", "value": 5}],
        10, ["a", "c"], []
    ))
    
    # Test 5: empty
    test_cases.append((
        [], 0, [], []
    ))
    
    # Test 6: single valid
    test_cases.append((
        [{"id": "a", "start": "2026-05-01T09:00:00", "end": "2026-05-01T10:00:00", "value": 7}],
        7, ["a"], []
    ))
    
    all_passed = True
    for sessions_input, exp_val, exp_ids, exp_rej in test_cases:
        sessions = [Session(s) for s in sessions_input]
        max_val, sel_ids, rej = optimize_sessions(sessions)
        if max_val != exp_val or sel_ids != exp_ids:
            # Check rejected items more carefully
            rej_match = True
            if len(rej) != len(exp_rej):
                rej_match = False
            else:
                for r1, r2 in zip(rej, exp_rej):
                    if r1["id"] != r2["id"] or sorted(r1["reasons"]) != sorted(r2["reasons"]):
                        rej_match = False
                        break
            if not rej_match:
                all_passed = False
                print(f"FAIL: {sessions_input}")
                print(f"  Got: max_value={max_val}, ids={sel_ids}, rejected={rej}")
                print(f"  Exp: max_value={exp_val}, ids={exp_ids}, rejected={exp_rej}")
    
    if all_passed:
        print("All tests passed!")
        return 0
    else:
        print("Some tests failed.")
        return 1

def main():
    if len(sys.argv) > 1 and sys.argv[1] == "--test":
        sys.exit(run_tests())
    
    try:
        data = json.load(sys.stdin)
    except json.JSONDecodeError as e:
        print(json.dumps({"error": f"Invalid JSON: {e}"}))
        sys.exit(1)
    
    if "sessions" not in data or not isinstance(data["sessions"], list):
        print(json.dumps({"error": "Missing or invalid 'sessions' key"}))
        sys.exit(1)
    
    sessions = []
    for s_dict in data["sessions"]:
        if not isinstance(s_dict, dict) or "id" not in s_dict or "start" not in s_dict or "end" not in s_dict or "value" not in s_dict:
            print(json.dumps({"error": "Each session must have id, start, end, value"}))
            sys.exit(1)
        try:
            sessions.append(Session(s_dict))
        except ValueError as e:
            print(json.dumps({"error": f"Invalid timestamp: {e}"}))
            sys.exit(1)
    
    max_val, selected_ids, rejected = optimize_sessions(sessions)
    
    result = {
        "max_value": max_val,
        "selected_ids": selected_ids,
        "rejected": rejected
    }
    print(json.dumps(result))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

messages.content:
Write a single-file Node.js HTTP server named `server.js`. Use only built-in Node.js modules; no Express, no npm packages. Keep the code under 1,000 lines.

Task: implement a small JSON API for a help-desk ticket queue stored in a local JSON file.

Data model:
- Ticket: `id`, `subject`, `description`, `priority` (`low`, `medium`, `high`), `status` (`open`, `in_progress`, `resolved`), `created_at`, `updated_at`.

API requirements:
1. `GET /tickets` with optional query params `status` and `priority`.
2. `POST /tickets` to create a ticket. Validate required fields.
3. `GET /tickets/{id}` to fetch one ticket.
4. `PATCH /tickets/{id}` to update `subject`, `description`, `priority`, or `status`.
5. `DELETE /tickets/{id}` to delete a ticket.
6. `GET /metrics` returning counts by status and priority plus average age of open tickets in seconds.

Engineering requirements:
- Read/write a `tickets.json` file safely enough for sequential requests.
- Return JSON for all responses, including errors.
- Include CORS headers for local browser testing.
- Handle invalid JSON, unknown routes, unsupported methods, and missing IDs.
- Start on `127.0.0.1:8080` by default.

Output only the complete JavaScript code. Do not include explanations.

Create a single-file frontend app named `index.html`. Do not use external libraries, CDNs, images, or build tools. Keep the total code under 1,000 lines.

Task: build an accessible “meeting notes board” for a small team.

Functional requirements:
1. Users can add a note with title, owner, meeting date, tags, and body.
2. Notes appear as cards and can be filtered by owner, tag, and free-text search.
3. A note can be marked as “Action Required,” “Decision,” or “Reference.”
4. Users can edit and delete notes.
5. Persist all data in `localStorage`.
6. Provide a compact statistics strip: total notes, action-required count, unique owners, and most common tag.
7. Responsive layout: two-column card grid on wide screens, single column on narrow screens.
8. Basic accessibility: labels for inputs, keyboard-operable controls, visible focus states.

Output only the complete `index.html` content, including HTML, CSS, and JavaScript. Do not include explanatory text.

Read the public-domain passage below and produce a structured English summary for modern business readers.

Output requirements:
1. A 100-word executive summary.
2. Five principles of effective business writing implied by the author.
3. A table with two columns: “Original-era advice” and “Modern equivalent.” Include at least five rows.
4. Identify any advice that may be dated and explain how to update it without losing the underlying principle.
5. Do not add claims that are not grounded in the passage.

素材来源：The Book of Business Etiquette, chapter “The Business of Writing” longer excerpt
来源链接：https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/23025
版权/授权说明：Project Gutenberg lists this eBook as public domain in the USA.

【待处理素材】
Half the business letters which are written should never be written at all, and of the other half so many are incomplete or incoherent that a transaction which could be finished and filed away in two letters frequently requires six or eight.

A good letter is the result of clear thinking and careful planning. In the case of the sales-letter it sometimes takes several weeks to write one, but for ordinary correspondence a few minutes is usually all that is necessary. The length of time does not matter--it is the sort of letter which is produced at the end of it.

Books of commercial correspondence give a number of rules and standards by which a letter can be measured. But all rules of thumb are dangerous, and there are only two items which are essential. The others are valuable only as they contribute to them. The letter must succeed in getting its idea across and it must build up good will for its firm. And the best one is the one which accomplishes this most courteously and most completely in the briefest space of time (and paper).

There should be a reason back of every letter if it is only to say "Thank you" to a customer. Too much of our national energy goes up in waste effort, in aimless advertising, worthless salesmanship, ineffective letter writing, and in a thousand and one other ways. A lot of it is hammered out on the typewriters transcribing perfectly useless letters to paper which might really be worth something if it were given over to a different purpose.

A good letter never attracts the mind of the reader to itself as a thing apart from its contents. Last year a publishing house sent out a hundred test letters advertising one of their books. Three answers came back, none of them ordering the book, but all three praising the letter. One was from a teacher of commercial English who declared that he was going to use it as a model in his classes, and the other two congratulated the firm on having so excellent a correspondent. The physical make-up of the letter was attractive, it was written by a college graduate and couched in clear, correct, and colorful English. And yet it was no good. No _letter and no advertisement is any good which calls attention to itself instead of the message it is trying to deliver_.

There is not much room for individuality in the make-up of a letter. Custom has standardized it, and startling variations from the conventional format indicates freakishness rather than originality. They are like that astonishing gentleman who walks up Fifth Avenue on the coldest mornings in the year, bareheaded, coatless, sockless, clad in white flannels and tennis slippers. He attracts attention, but he makes us shiver.

Plain white paper of good quality is always in good taste. Certain dull-tinted papers are not bad, but gaudy colors, flashy designs, and ornate letter heads are taboo in all high types of business. Simple headings giving explicit and useful information are best. The name and address of the firm (and "New York" or "Chicago" is not sufficient in spite of the fact that a good many places go into no more detail than this), the cable address if it has one, the telephone number and the trademark if it is an inconspicuous one (there is a difference between _conspicuous_ and _distinctive_) are all that any business house needs.

Hotels are often pictured on their own stationery in a way that is anything but modest, but there is a very good reason for it. The first thing most people want to know about a hotel is what sort of looking place it is. All right, here you are. Some factories, especially those that are proud of their appearance, carry their own picture on their stationery. There is nothing to say against it, but one of the most beautiful factories in America has on its letter head only the name of the firm, the address, and a small trademark engraved in black. Sometimes a picture, in a sales letter, for instance, supplements the written matter in a most effective way. And whenever any kind of device is really helpful it should by all means be used, subject only to the limits of good taste.

It is more practical in business to use standard size envelopes. If window envelopes are used the window should be clear, the paper white or nearly so, and the typewritten address a good honest black. The enclosure should fit snugly and should be placed so that the address is in plain view without having to be jiggled around in the envelope first. A letter passes through the hands of several postal clerks before it reaches the person to whom it is addressed, and if each one of them has to stop to play with it awhile an appreciable amount of time is lost, not to mention the strain it puts on their respective tempers. The paper of which an envelope is made should always be opaque enough to conceal the contents of the letter.

Practically all business letters are typewritten. Occasionally a "Help Wanted" advertisement requests that the answer be in the applicant's own handwriting, but even this is rare. In most places the typing is taken care of by girls who have been trained for the purpose, but most young girls just entering business are highly irresponsible, and it is necessary for the men and women who dictate the letters to know what constitutes a pleasing make-up so that they can point out the flaws and give suggestions for doing away with them.

The letter should be arranged symmetrically on the page with ample margins all around. Nothing but experience in copying her own notes will teach a stenographer to estimate them correctly so that she will not have to rewrite badly placed letters. It is a little point, but an important one.

Each subject considered in a letter should be treated in a separate paragraph, and each paragraph should be set off from the others by a wider space than that between the lines, double space between the paragraphs when there is single space between the lines, triple space between the paragraphs when there is a double space between the lines, and so on.

A business letter should handle only one subject. Two letters should be dispatched if two subjects are to be covered. This enables the house receiving the letter to file it so that it can be found when it is needed.

When a letter is addressed to an individual it is better to begin "Dear Mr. Brown" or "My dear Mr. Brown" than "Dear Sir" or "My dear Sir." "Gentlemen" or "Ladies" is sometime used in salutation when a letter is addressed to a group. "Dear Friend" is permissible in general letters sent out to persons of both sexes. Honorary titles should be used in the address when they take the place of "Mr.," such titles as Reverend, Doctor, Honorable (abbreviated to Rev., Dr., Hon.,) and the like. Titles should not be dropped except in the case of personal letters.

Special care should be taken with the outside address. State abbreviations should be used sparingly when there is a chance of confusion as in the case of Ga., Va., La., and Pa. "City" is not sufficient and should never be used. Nor should the name of the state ever be omitted even when the letter is addressed to some other point in the same state, as from New York to Brooklyn. And postage should be complete. A letter on which there is two cents due has placed itself under a pretty severe handicap before it is opened.

It is astonishing how many letters go out every day unsigned, lacking enclosures, carrying the wrong addresses, bearing insufficient postage, and showing other evidences of carelessness and thoughtlessness. In a town in New England last year one of the specialty shops received at Christmas time twenty different lots of money--money orders, stamps, and cash--by mail, not one of which bore the slightest clue to the identity of the sender. Countless times during the year this happens in every mail order house.

message.content:
Here is a structured English summary of the passage from *The Book of Business Etiquette* for modern business readers.

### Executive Summary (100 words)

Half of all business correspondence is unnecessary or poorly executed, wasting time and resources. Effective business writing stems from clear thinking and planning. The ultimate goal of any letter is to convey its message clearly while building goodwill. A letter fails if it draws attention to itself rather than its content. While standard formats are best, small customization is acceptable if it aids the reader. Use plain, high-quality materials; separate subjects into individual letters for easy filing; and use personal salutations like "Dear Mr. Brown" over "Dear Sir." Thoroughly proofread for enclosures, accurate addresses, and signatures to avoid damaging first impressions.

### Five Principles of Effective Business Writing

1.  **Purpose Over Performance:** Every communication must have a clear business reason (even a simple thank-you). Avoid writing letters that serve no purpose or exist only to seem busy.
2.  **Message, Not Messenger:** Never let the style or format of a letter overshadow the content itself. If a reader praises the letter instead of responding to the message, the communication has failed.
3.  **Clarity & Completeness:** A letter must get its idea across fully in as few words as possible. Incomplete or incoherent correspondence leads to wasted time and multiple follow-ups, increasing transaction costs.
4.  **Format Follows Function:** Use standard, professional formats (white paper, proper margins) to convey stability and respect. Radical deviations from norms signal poor judgment, not creativity.
5.  **One Letter, One Subject:** Handle only one topic per communication to facilitate accurate filing, retrieval, and action by the recipient.

### Historical Advice vs. Modern Equivalent

| Original-era advice | Modern equivalent |
| :--- | :--- |
| "A good letter... must... build up good will for its firm." | Every email or message should aim to strengthen the sender’s personal or brand reputation. |
| "Each subject... should be treated in a separate paragraph... A business letter should handle only one subject." | Use clear subject lines; keep emails to one topic per thread to ensure easy filing and searchability. |
| "Plain white paper of good quality is always in good taste." | Use a clean, professional email signature with a simple font; avoid excessive colors or distracting backgrounds. |
| "When a letter is addressed to an individual it is better to begin 'Dear Mr. Brown'... than 'Dear Sir'." | Open with a personalized greeting (e.g., "Hi Kim") rather than generic phrases like "To Whom It May Concern." |
| "Special care should be taken with the outside address... 'City' is not sufficient." | Always include the full correct address, state abbreviation, and ZIP code; never omit location details in mailing or shipping. |

### Dated Advice & Modern Update

**Dated Advice:** The passage describes typing as handled by "girls who have been trained for the purpose," and notes that young female employees are "highly irresponsible."

**Underlying Principle:** The writer or manager is responsible for knowing quality standards and coaching their team to produce professional, error-free output. The principle is accountability and delegation with quality control.

**Modern Update:** The core principle remains relevant: the sender is ultimately accountable for the final product. However, the language and assumption about gender roles are outdated. The modern equivalent advises that any professional (regardless of gender) should be trained in formatting standards. The manager or dictator should review their own drafts and provide constructive feedback to team members, respecting their skills and responsibility.

messages.content:
Read the public-domain passage below and produce a structured English summary.

Output requirements:
1. A 120-word maximum plot summary.
2. A bullet list of Mary’s character traits shown in the passage, each supported by a concrete detail.
3. A short paragraph explaining how the setting creates emotional distance or isolation.
4. Three discussion questions suitable for ages 10–12.
5. Do not add plot information from outside the passage.

素材来源：Frances Hodgson Burnett, The Secret Garden, opening excerpt
来源链接：https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/113
版权/授权说明：Project Gutenberg lists this eBook as public domain in the USA.

【待处理素材】
When Mary Lennox was sent to Misselthwaite Manor to live with her uncle everybody said she was the most disagreeable-looking child ever seen. It was true, too. She had a little thin face and a little thin body, thin light hair and a sour expression. Her hair was yellow, and her face was yellow because she had been born in India and had always been ill in one way or another. Her father had held a position under the English Government and had always been busy and ill himself, and her mother had been a great beauty who cared only to go to parties and amuse herself with gay people. She had not wanted a little girl at all, and when Mary was born she handed her over to the care of an Ayah, who was made to understand that if she wished to please the Mem Sahib she must keep the child out of sight as much as possible. So when she was a sickly, fretful, ugly little baby she was kept out of the way, and when she became a sickly, fretful, toddling thing she was kept out of the way also. She never remembered seeing familiarly anything but the dark faces of her Ayah and the other native servants, and as they always obeyed her and gave her her own way in everything, because the Mem Sahib would be angry if she was disturbed by her crying, by the time she was six years old she was as tyrannical and selfish a little pig as ever lived. The young English governess who came to teach her to read and write disliked her so much that she gave up her place in three months, and when other governesses came to try to fill it they always went away in a shorter time than the first one. So if Mary had not chosen to really want to know how to read books she would never have learned her letters at all.

One frightfully hot morning, when she was about nine years old, she awakened feeling very cross, and she became crosser still when she saw that the servant who stood by her bedside was not her Ayah.

“Why did you come?” she said to the strange woman. “I will not let you stay. Send my Ayah to me.”

The woman looked frightened, but she only stammered that the Ayah could not come and when Mary threw herself into a passion and beat and kicked her, she looked only more frightened and repeated that it was not possible for the Ayah to come to Missie Sahib.

There was something mysterious in the air that morning. Nothing was done in its regular order and several of the native servants seemed missing, while those whom Mary saw slunk or hurried about with ashy and scared faces. But no one would tell her anything and her Ayah did not come. She was actually left alone as the morning went on, and at last she wandered out into the garden and began to play by herself under a tree near the veranda. She pretended that she was making a flower-bed, and she stuck big scarlet hibiscus blossoms into little heaps of earth, all the time growing more and more angry and muttering to herself the things she would say and the names she would call Saidie when she returned.

“Pig! Pig! Daughter of Pigs!” she said, because to call a native a pig is the worst insult of all.

She was grinding her teeth and saying this over and over again when she heard her mother come out on the veranda with someone. She was with a fair young man and they stood talking together in low strange voices. Mary knew the fair young man who looked like a boy. She had heard that he was a very young officer who had just come from England. The child stared at him, but she stared most at her mother. She always did this when she had a chance to see her, because the Mem Sahib—Mary used to call her that oftener than anything else—was such a tall, slim, pretty person and wore such lovely clothes. Her hair was like curly silk and she had a delicate little nose which seemed to be disdaining things, and she had large laughing eyes. All her clothes were thin and floating, and Mary said they were “full of lace.” They looked fuller of lace than ever this morning, but her eyes were not laughing at all. They were large and scared and lifted imploringly to the fair boy officer’s face.

“Is it so very bad? Oh, is it?” Mary heard her say.

“Awfully,” the young man answered in a trembling voice. “Awfully, Mrs. Lennox. You ought to have gone to the hills two weeks ago.”

The Mem Sahib wrung her hands.

“Oh, I know I ought!” she cried. “I only stayed to go to that silly dinner party. What a fool I was!”

At that very moment such a loud sound of wailing broke out from the servants’ quarters that she clutched the young man’s arm, and Mary stood shivering from head to foot. The wailing grew wilder and wilder. “What is it? What is it?” Mrs. Lennox gasped.

“Someone has died,” answered the boy officer. “You did not say it had broken out among your servants.”

“I did not know!” the Mem Sahib cried. “Come with me! Come with me!” and she turned and ran into the house.

After that appalling things happened, and the mysteriousness of the morning was explained to Mary. The cholera had broken out in its most fatal form and people were dying like flies. The Ayah had been taken ill in the night, and it was because she had just died that the servants had wailed in the huts. Before the next day three other servants were dead and others had run away in terror. There was panic on every side, and dying people in all the bungalows.

During the confusion and bewilderment of the second day Mary hid herself in the nursery and was forgotten by everyone. Nobody thought of her, nobody wanted her, and strange things happened of which she knew nothing. Mary alternately cried and slept through the hours. She only knew that people were ill and that she heard mysterious and frightening sounds. Once she crept into the dining-room and found it empty, though a partly finished meal was on the table and chairs and plates looked as if they had been hastily pushed back when the diners rose suddenly for some reason. The child ate some fruit and biscuits, and being thirsty she drank a glass of wine which stood nearly filled. It was sweet, and she did not know how strong it was. Very soon it made her intensely drowsy, and she went back to her nursery and shut herself in again, frightened by cries she heard in the huts and by the hurrying sound of feet. The wine made her so sleepy that she could scarcely keep her eyes open and she lay down on her bed and knew nothing more for a long time.

message.content:
### Plot Summary (120 words max)
Mary Lennox, a sour, sickly child born in India, is sent to Misselthwaite Manor after her parents die in a cholera outbreak. Raised by servants who obeyed her every whim, Mary became tyrannical and selfish. When cholera strikes, her ayah dies, and servants flee in panic. Mary hides alone in the nursery, forgotten by everyone. She eats leftover food, drinks wine, and falls into a deep sleep. Later, she is discovered and sent to England to live with her uncle. The opening shows Mary as isolated, unwanted even by her own mother, who cared only for parties. The story establishes her lonely, disagreeable nature and the chaotic loss that sets her new life in motion.

### Character Traits of Mary
- **Disagreeable-looking**: She has a “little thin face,” “thin light hair,” and a “sour expression.”
- **Sickly**: She “had always been ill in one way or another” and has a yellow complexion from illness.
- **Tyrannical and selfish**: By age six, she is described as “as tyrannical and selfish a little pig as ever lived.”
- **Violent-tempered**: When the strange servant appears, Mary “threw herself into a passion and beat and kicked her.”
- **Unwanted**: Her mother “had not wanted a little girl at all” and kept her out of sight.

### Setting and Emotional Distance
The setting—a hot, mysterious morning in India during a cholera outbreak—creates emotional distance by making Mary an afterthought. She is left alone as servants disappear or hurry about with “ashy and scared faces.” Nobody tells her what is happening, and she hides in the nursery, “forgotten by everyone.” The chaos emphasizes how isolated she is: no one thinks of her, no one wants her. Even her mother ignores her, focused only on her own panic. The setting reflects Mary’s deep loneliness and emotional neglect, as she experiences fear and confusion without any comfort or explanation.

### Discussion Questions (Ages 10–12)
1. Why do you think Mary behaved so badly, like beating and kicking the servant? What might have caused her to act that way?
2. How does Mary’s mother treat her? Do you think a mother should act like that? Why or why not?
3. If you were Mary during the cholera outbreak, how would you feel being left alone and forgotten? What would you do?

messages.content:
Read the public-domain science explainer below and produce a structured English summary.

Output requirements:
1. A concise 100-word overview.
2. A concept map in text form using arrows, showing how water moves among major pools.
3. A list of at least eight key terms with one-sentence definitions based only on the passage.
4. A section titled “Human influence” summarizing how human actions affect water movement, storage, or availability.
5. Do not introduce outside climate, policy, or regional examples.

素材来源：U.S. Geological Survey Water Science School: Water cycle
来源链接：https://www.usgs.gov/water-science-school/water-cycle
版权/授权说明：USGS-authored information is considered U.S. public domain.

【待处理素材】
The water cycle describes where water is on Earth and how it moves. Human water use, land use, and climate change all impact the water cycle. By understanding these impacts, we can work toward using water sustainably.

Viewed from space, the most striking feature of our planet is the water. In both liquid and frozen form, it covers 75% of the Earth's surface. It fills the sky with clouds. Water is practically everywhere on Earth, from inside the planet's rocky crust to inside the cells of the human body. What's important to keep in mind is that all of this water is in constant motion across our planet.

Water is stored in the atmosphere, on the land surface, and below the ground. It can be a liquid, a solid, or a gas. Liquid water can be fresh or saline. Water moves between the places it is stored. Water moves at large scales, through watersheds, the atmosphere, and below the Earth's surface. Water moves at very small scales too. It is in us, plants, and other organisms.

Oceans store 96% of all water on Earth. Ocean water is saline, meaning it’s salty. On land, saline water is stored in saline lakes. The rest of the water on Earth is fresh water. Fresh water is stored in liquid form in freshwater lakes, artificial reservoirs, rivers, and wetlands. Water is stored in solid, frozen form in ice sheets and glaciers, and in snowpack at high elevations or near Earth's poles.

As it moves, water can change form between liquid, solid, and gas. Circulation mixes water in the oceans and transports water vapor in the atmosphere. Water moves between the atmosphere and the surface through evaporation, evapotranspiration, and precipitation. Water moves across the surface through snowmelt, runoff, and streamflow. Water moves into the ground through infiltration and groundwater recharge. Underground, groundwater flows within aquifers. Groundwater can return to the surface through natural discharge into rivers, the ocean, and from springs.

Water moves naturally and because of human actions. Energy from the sun and the force of gravity drive the continual movement of water between pools. The sun’s energy causes liquid water to evaporate into water vapor. Evapotranspiration is the main way water moves into the atmosphere from the land surface and oceans. Gravity causes water to flow downward on land. It causes rain, snow, and hail to fall from clouds.

Humans and other organisms rely on water for life. The amount of water that is available depends on how much water there is in each pool, when and how fast water moves through the water cycle, and how clean the water is. By understanding human impacts on the water cycle, we can work toward using water sustainably.

message.content:
### 1. Concise 100-word Overview
The water cycle describes the constant movement of water on Earth, stored in oceans (96% of all water), atmosphere, land surface, and underground. Water changes between liquid, solid, and gas forms. Solar energy drives evaporation and evapotranspiration; gravity drives precipitation and downhill flow. Water moves through evaporation, precipitation, snowmelt, runoff, infiltration, groundwater flow, and discharge. Human water use, land use, and climate change affect water movement, storage, and availability. Understanding these impacts helps promote sustainable water use.

### 2. Concept Map (Text Form)
```
[Atmosphere] ← Evaporation / Evapotranspiration ← [Oceans & Land Surface]
       ↓ Precipitation (rain, snow, hail)
[Land Surface & Ice Sheets] → Snowmelt / Runoff → [Streams & Rivers]
       ↓ Infiltration / Recharge
[Groundwater (Aquifers)] → Natural Discharge (springs, rivers, ocean) → [Oceans]
       ← ← ← ← ← ← ← ← ← ← ← ← ← ← ← ← ← ← ← ← ← ← (Circulation also mixes ocean water)
```

### 3. Key Terms and Definitions
- **Water cycle**: The continuous movement and storage of water on Earth and its changes between liquid, solid, and gas forms.
- **Evaporation**: The process by which solar energy turns liquid water into water vapor.
- **Evapotranspiration**: The main way water moves from land surface and oceans into the atmosphere, combining evaporation and plant transpiration.
- **Precipitation**: Rain, snow, or hail that falls from clouds due to gravity.
- **Runoff**: Water that moves across the land surface toward streams and rivers.
- **Infiltration**: The movement of water from the surface into the ground.
- **Groundwater recharge**: The process by which infiltrated water enters and replenishes underground aquifers.
- **Aquifer**: An underground layer where groundwater flows and is stored.

### 4. Human influence
Human water use, land use, and climate change all affect the water cycle. These actions alter how water moves, where it is stored, and how much clean water is available. For example, diverting water for agriculture or cities changes natural flow paths, while land development can reduce infiltration and increase runoff. Understanding these impacts allows us to work toward sustainable water use.

messages.content:
Write an 800–1,000 word executive update email from the COO of a fictional direct-to-consumer home goods company to all employees. Context: a warehouse management system migration has reduced shipping speed for one week, and customer support volume is up.

Requirements:
- Tone: accountable, calm, specific, and respectful of frontline teams.
- Explain what happened without blaming a team or vendor.
- Include current customer impact, what is being done today, what will change next week, and how teams should escalate urgent issues.
- Include a concise manager talking-points section with 4 bullets.
- Avoid clichés, excessive apology, legal language, or promises that cannot be guaranteed.

message.content:
**Subject:** Update on warehouse system migration and current shipping delays  

**Date:** [Insert Date]  
**To:** All Team Members  

Team,  

I’m writing with a direct update on the warehouse management system (WMS) migration that went live last Monday. I want to start by acknowledging the real, immediate pressure this has put on our frontline teams—particularly in fulfillment, customer experience, and our warehouse operations. You’ve been fielding a higher volume of customer contacts, managing slower pick-and-pack speeds, and doing so without full system stability. That’s not acceptable, and I want you to know that leadership owns this outcome, not any single team or vendor.  

**What happened**  

The migration to our new WMS was planned over six months with multiple test cycles. During the final weekend cutover, we encountered a data-mapping discrepancy between order routing logic and inventory slotting tables. This meant that when orders entered the system Monday morning, the software could not consistently assign the most efficient pick paths. As a result, pickers have had to manually override suggested routes in roughly one out of four orders. That added 30–90 seconds per pick, compounding across thousands of orders per day.  

No team caused this. Our vendor provided solid code. Our internal engineers ran thorough pre-launch checks. The discrepancy surfaced only under real-world order volume and concurrent user load—something no test environment fully replicates. The root cause is now identified, and a fix is being validated.  

**Current customer impact**  

- **Shipping speed:** Orders placed since Monday have shipped, on average, one to two business days later than our standard. Some east-coast zone orders that normally ship same-day are now delayed by three days.  
- **Customer support volume:** Ticket volume is up roughly 3x compared to last week, with ~60% of contacts asking about order status or tracking. Average response time has stretched from under two hours to nearly eight hours.  
- **Repeat contact rate:** We’re seeing a 12% repeat-contact rate (customer reaching out more than once on the same issue), which tells me our first responses aren’t always resolving the concern.  

I want to be clear: we are not losing orders, and no customer data has been compromised. The impact is purely on speed and communication.  

**What we are doing today**  

The engineering and operations teams are in a war room running the following:  

- Deploying a patch to the WMS that corrects the pick-path logic. That patch completed lab testing at 2:00 AM this morning and is now in a staged rollout—starting with our two lowest-volume warehouses. If those go clean for four hours, we’ll push to all sites by end of day tomorrow.  
- Adding two temporary supervisors to each warehouse shift to help pickers manually override paths when the system suggests an inefficient route. This reduces the cognitive load on your teams and keeps line speed closer to normal.  
- Cross-training 15 corporate staff (including three members of my leadership team) to answer basic order-status tickets this afternoon, starting at 3:00 PM ET. That will pull our response time down to under four hours by end of week.  
- Sending an automated SMS to all customers whose orders are delayed beyond our original promised window, with a specific new delivery date and a $10 credit applied automatically—no need to contact us.  

**What will change next week**  

- By Tuesday, we expect the full WMS patch to be live across all warehouses. Preliminary performance data from the staged rollout shows pick-path accuracy returning to 98% or higher, which should bring shipping speed back to pre-migration levels by Wednesday.  
- On Wednesday, we will launch a dedicated “order status” self-service feature within our customer portal, allowing customers to check real-time tracking without needing to contact support. This should reduce incoming ticket volume by at least 25%.  
- Starting next Friday, we will begin a post-mortem review process—not to assign blame, but to document exactly which testing scenarios we missed and how we will replicate them in future migrations. I will share a summary of that review with the entire company within two weeks.  

**How to escalate urgent issues**  

If you are a frontline team member and you encounter a problem that is blocking a customer order, affecting safety, or causing a shipment to miss a guaranteed delivery date:  

- **Immediate escalation:** Tag your direct supervisor and email [ops-escalation@company.com] with “URGENT” in the subject line. That mailbox is monitored by the war room 24/7 until further notice.  
- **After-hours:** If your supervisor is unavailable, call the night operations lead at [phone number]. That number will reach a live person, not a voicemail.  
- **Customer escalations:** For customers who are angry, threatening churn, or asking for refunds beyond our standard policy, do not try to solve alone. Transfer to our senior CX team by using the “Escalate – Ops” tag in Zendesk. Those cases will be handled within four hours by a team with override authority.  

**Manager talking points (for your one-on-ones and stand-ups)**  

When your team asks what’s going on, here is what you can say clearly and honestly:  

1. **“The new system has a routing bug that slows down picking. We identified it Monday evening, and a fix is rolling out warehouse by warehouse today. We expect full recovery by Wednesday.”**  
2. **“Customer support is overwhelmed right now, but we’ve added temporary staff from corporate and a self-service tracking tool launches next week. Your team’s patience and composure make a real difference—thank you.”**  
3. **“No one is being blamed. The migration was well-planned. This is a rare gap in a real-world load test, and leadership is responsible for approving the go-live. We own it.”**  
4. **“If you see a blocked order or an unsafe workaround, escalate immediately—we have a 24/7 war room and a dedicated email. Don’t try to fix it alone.”**  

**Closing**  

I know this has been a hard week. A system change meant to improve our speed and accuracy instead made your jobs harder and let customers down. I respect the fact that you came to work, dealt with the frustration, and kept moving product out the door. That’s the culture we’ve built, and it will carry us through this.  

We are not going to promise that this never happens again—system changes always carry some risk. But we are going to document this one thoroughly, fix it quickly, and use it to make our next migration smoother.  

Thank you for your work.  

Sincerely,  

[Your Name]  
Chief Operating Officer  
[Company Name]

messages.content:
Write an 800–1,000 word story for children ages 7–10. The main characters are a careful snail who keeps a map of every puddle in the garden and a young sparrow who wants to fly before she has learned how to land. The story should take place after a summer rain.

Requirements:
- Complete story arc with a gentle problem and resolution.
- Warm, concrete language suitable for children.
- Include one simple nature concept, such as how puddles dry or how birds use wind, explained naturally through the story.
- Theme: patience can be a kind of bravery.
- No violence, death, horror, or adult romance.

message.content:
Here is a story for children ages 7–10.

---

### The Puddle Map and the Speedy Sparrow

After the summer rain, the whole world felt new. The air smelled of wet earth and green leaves, and the garden sparkled like a jewel box. For a very careful snail named Silas, this was the best time of all.

Silas lived under a broad rhubarb leaf. His shell was the color of caramel, and he carried it with the importance of a royal carriage. In his tiny front pocket was his most prized possession: a folded leaf-map.

He unrolled it now, tracing a line with his delicate eye-stalks.

“Ah,” he whispered to himself. “The Mirror Pond by the tomato stake. The Blue Marble under the hose reel. And… the Great Shimmering Sea beneath the birdbath. I must check them all.”

You see, Silas was the mapmaker of the garden. Every puddle that appeared after a rain, he measured. He noted how deep it was (by the length of his shell), how warm (by the tickle on his tummy), and how long it might last. It was a very important job, because puddles, Silas knew, were not forever. They were small, temporary worlds.

As he slid slowly toward the tomato stake, a flash of brown feathers zipped over his head. *Fwip! Fwip!*

It was Pip, a young sparrow with a chest the color of buttermilk. Pip was bouncing from twig to twig, her wings half-open, her tiny heart thumping with excitement.

“Silas! Silas! Look!” she chirped. “I almost touched the clothesline!”

“Very high,” said Silas, politely. He did not look up. He was counting the steps to the Mirror Pond.

Pip fluttered down and landed on a pebble beside him. “Why are you so slow?” she asked, hopping sideways. “The whole sky is open! The wind is just *waiting* to lift me. I’m going to fly to the top of the big oak today. Maybe even higher!”

“Have you practiced your landing?” asked Silas, his voice as calm as mud.

Pip ruffled her feathers. “Landing is boring. Landing is the part *after* the fun. I want to *fly*.”

Silas finally stopped. He pointed an eye-stalk at her. “Flying is a good thing. But a bird who cannot land is like a puddle that cannot hold still. It won’t be a puddle for long.”

Pip didn’t understand. “You’re just afraid of everything,” she said. “You crawl on the ground. I’m going to soar!”

And she did. She beat her small wings as hard as she could and launched herself into the sparkling air. She rose past the rhubarb, past the wet laundry on the line, up into the blue canvas of the sky. She felt the wind rush through her feathers. It was glorious!

But then she looked down.

The ground was very far away. The garden was a patchwork quilt of green and brown. The birdbath looked as small as a cup. Pip’s wings suddenly felt heavy. She didn’t know how to go *down*. She tried to flutter, but she just hovered, wobbling.

“Help!” she squeaked. “I’m stuck in the sky!”

The wind, which had felt like a friend, now felt like a giant hand pushing her sideways. She tumbled, caught a gust, and tumbled again, heading straight for the largest puddle in the garden—The Great Shimmering Sea beneath the birdbath.

*SPLASH!*

Pip landed right in the middle of it, soaking wet. She sputtered, her wings dripping, her pride even wetter.

A few minutes later, a very slow shadow appeared at the edge of the puddle. It was Silas.

“You found my puddle,” he said gently. “The Great Shimmering Sea. Twenty-three snail-shells wide. Depth: two shells. Temperature: perfectly warm for a bath.”

Pip wasn’t amused. “I’m a mess. I wanted to fly, not take a bath.”

Silas slid into the shallow water. “When I started making my puddle map, I was very slow. I wanted to see every puddle all at once. But you can’t drink a whole ocean in one sip. You have to wait. You have to be careful.”

He pointed to the edge of the water. “See? The sun is drinking the puddle, too. In a few hours, this will be gone. A puddle is a lesson in patience. It stays, it shines, and then it goes away.”

Pip sniffled. “So I’ll never fly high?”

“You will,” said Silas. “But first, you must learn to be brave enough to be slow.”

“Slow isn’t brave,” Pip argued.

“It is,” said Silas. “Flying up is easy. But waiting, learning to tilt your wings just so, feeling the wind, and bringing yourself gently back to the ground? That is harder. That is the bravery of patience.”

He showed her a small, shallow puddle nearby. “Practice here,” he said. “Jump into this puddle from the edge of the stone. Feel your feet touch the water. Then try to land on the dry stone next to it. Small flights. Safe landings.”

Pip was doubtful, but she was too tired to argue. She hopped onto the stone. *Hop.* *Land.* *Splash.* She did it again. *Hop.* *Flutter.* *Land.* Her feet touched the dry stone. She didn’t fall.

For the rest of the afternoon, Pip practiced. She landed in the shallow puddle a hundred times. She landed on the stone a hundred more. Each time, she paid attention. She learned that if she spread her tail feathers, she slowed down. If she tilted her wings forward, the wind pushed her gently down. The sky was not a wild horse. It was a gentle friend who needed to be asked nicely.

Silas watched, completing his notes on the Great Shimmering Sea. When the sun began to sink, turning the puddles into orange mirrors, Pip fluttered up to the lowest branch of the rhubarb. She sat beside Silas.

“I did it,” she said softly. “I flew up, and I landed. Right there. On the branch.”

Silas smiled his snail-smile (a very slow curl of his lips). “You were very brave today, Pip.”

“I didn’t go very high,” she said.

“No,” said Silas. “But patience is its own kind of bravery. It is brave to wait. It is brave to learn. Tomorrow, you will fly a little higher. And the day after, higher still. And soon, you will soar like the wind itself. But you will always know how to come home.”

Pip leaned her head against the rhubarb stem. She looked at the puddles below, now turning from silver to gold. She understood now. She was not a puddle that would dry up in a hurry. She was a little bird, learning to stay. And that, she decided, was the best kind of flying of all.

messages.content:
Write an 800–1,000 word press release in English based only on the fictional facts below. Do not invent funding amounts, celebrity endorsers, political supporters, or external partners. Use a professional, journalistic style with a headline, dateline, lead paragraph, supporting details, at least one direct quote, and a media contact block.

Fictional facts:
- Organization: Harborleaf Botanical Garden.
- Announcement: Launch of a new “Pollinator Path” public education trail.
- Opening date: July 12, 2026.
- Location: The east meadow loop, a 0.8-mile accessible trail.
- Features: native plant demonstration beds, night-blooming flower station, tactile signage, youth observation cards, shaded resting points, and a volunteer-led weekend walk.
- Motivation: visitors have asked for more practical guidance on planting for bees, butterflies, moths, and hummingbirds.
- Quote speaker: Maya Ortiz, Director of Learning Programs. Core message: small planting decisions can create connected habitats across neighborhoods.
- Keep the topic nonpolitical and educational.

message.content:
**FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE**

**Harborleaf Botanical Garden to Open “Pollinator Path” Education Trail on July 12**

**HARBORLEAF, CA – June 9, 2026** – Harborleaf Botanical Garden will debut a new public education trail designed to teach visitors how to create habitat for bees, butterflies, moths, and hummingbirds. The “Pollinator Path” opens July 12 along the garden’s east meadow loop, a 0.8-mile accessible trail featuring native plant demonstration beds, a night-blooming flower station, and interpretive signage designed for all ages.

The trail was developed in direct response to visitor requests. In recent years, Harborleaf staff have heard a recurring question from home gardeners and community groups: “What should I plant to actually help pollinators?” The Pollinator Path aims to answer that question with hands-on, visible examples that visitors can replicate in their own yards, school gardens, or neighborhood green spaces.

“People want to help, but they often don’t know where to start,” said Maya Ortiz, Director of Learning Programs at Harborleaf. “With this trail, they can see exactly which plants work for specific pollinators, how to arrange them, and why even a small planting patch matters. Those small decisions, when made across a neighborhood, can create a connected chain of habitats that sustains entire populations.”

The trail is fully accessible, with a smooth, packed-gravel surface suitable for wheelchairs and strollers. It winds through the east meadow loop, a shaded corridor of mature oaks and open grassland. Along the route, visitors will encounter:

- **Native plant demonstration beds**, each labeled with the pollinator species it supports—such as milkweed for monarch caterpillars, goldenrod for native bees, and salvia for hummingbirds.
- **A night-blooming flower station**, featuring moonflowers, evening primrose, and night-blooming jasmine, with interpretive signs explaining how moths and bats navigate after dark.
- **Tactile signage**, with raised text, braille, and embossed plant shapes, designed for visitors with visual impairments.
- **Youth observation cards**, available at a trailhead kiosk, that encourage children to track which pollinators they see at each station.
- **Shaded resting points**, with benches placed every quarter-mile under pergolas draped with pollinator-friendly vines.
- **Volunteer-led weekend walks**, offered every Saturday and Sunday morning through August, led by trained garden docents.

The trail is not a commercial exhibit or a political statement. Its sole focus is education—providing practical, science-based guidance on the ecological role of pollinators and the plants that support them. No funding partners, celebrity endorsers, or political supporters are associated with the project. Harborleaf is a private nonprofit operating solely on admissions, memberships, and individual donations.

Ortiz emphasized that the Pollinator Path is designed for all skill levels. “You don’t need to be a master gardener. You don’t need a big yard. You can start with a single pot of lavender on a balcony,” she said. “The trail shows you how.”

The opening coincides with peak bloom season for many of the meadow’s native wildflowers. Harborleaf expects the trail to remain active year-round, with volunteer-led walks continuing through October and winter programming focused on seed identification and dormant plant habitats.

The Pollinator Path will open to the public at 9:00 a.m. on July 12, 2026. Admission to Harborleaf Botanical Garden is included with standard entry. No special tickets or reservations are required for the trail, though weekend walk groups are limited to 20 participants and fill on a first-come basis.

For more information, visit the garden’s website or stop by the visitor center.

**Media Contact:**
Elena Vasquez
Communications Coordinator
Harborleaf Botanical Garden
555-012-3400
e.vasquez@harborleafbotanical.org

messages.content:
Write an 800–1,000 word non-erotic literary romance scene in English. Scenario: two former neighbors meet by chance in a small coastal laundromat during a power outage. Years ago, one of them left without saying goodbye. Tonight, a battery lantern, a half-finished load of towels, and an old voicemail become the center of the conversation.

Requirements:
- Focus on restrained emotional realism: regret, tenderness, defensiveness, and the difficulty of apologizing.
- Keep the entire piece to one scene.
- Do not use coercion, stalking, humiliation, explicit sexual content, or melodrama.
- Let physical details and subtext carry the emotion; dialogue should be sparse but meaningful.
- End with a quiet unresolved gesture rather than a full confession.

message.content:
The power cut at 9:17 p.m., according to the blinking clock on the wall of the Shorebreak Laundromat. For a long moment, the only sound was the dying whine of the industrial dryers. Then the emergency lights flickered on, casting the small space in a jaundiced glow.

Maya had been folding a warm towel, her hands buried in its floral-scented steam. She stood still, waiting for the lights to return. They did not. The dryers had stopped, but the washing machine in slot four, the one she’d been nursing through a stubborn cycle, gave a final, apologetic gurgle and fell silent. She swore under her breath—just a quiet, exhausted syllable. The towels were for her rental cottage, a last-minute booking she’d taken to escape a city she no longer recognized. A half-finished load meant wet laundry in a suitcase come morning.

She heard the door’s sticky hinge before she saw who entered. The man who stepped inside was tall, carrying a small wire basket with a single pair of jeans. The battery lantern he held cast a halogen circle at his feet, illuminating the salt-crusted toes of his boots. He stopped when he saw her.

Maya’s throat tightened. The recognition was instantaneous, a cold bell ringing in her chest. Leo.

He looked the same, only rougher at the edges. His dark hair was longer, pushed back from a face that had acquired new lines around the eyes. He wore a worn canvas jacket, the collar turned up against the coastal damp. He stood in the doorframe, the lantern making his shadow stretch long and distorted across the tiled floor.

“Maya,” he said. Not a question.

She folded the towel she was holding, her movements deliberate and slow. “Leo.”

He didn’t come closer. He set the basket on the bench by the door and placed the lantern beside it, adjusting the beam so that it pooled between them like a small, neutral sun. “Didn’t expect to see you here.”

“I didn’t expect to be here.” She gestured vaguely at the dead machines. “The power went out.”

“Whole block’s out. Flickered down the street like dominoes.” He paused. “I live two blocks over now. Since the divorce.”

She did not ask about the divorce. She did not ask about the wife he’d met six months after Maya had moved away, the one someone had mailed her a newspaper clipping about, with a Sharpie heart drawn around the bridal couple. She simply nodded.

The silence stretched. It was not comfortable. It was the kind of silence that had weight, that pressed against the eardrums.

“I still have that lamp,” she said, surprising herself. She pointed at the lantern. “The one you left on the porch. It lasted three winters until a raccoon knocked it off the railing.”

He almost smiled. “That lamp was older than we were. My grandmother used it for hurricane season.” He paused, then added, quietly, “You kept it.”

“It was useful.”

“That’s not why you kept it.” His voice was soft, but not insistent. He was not pushing. He was simply stating a fact.

She looked away, at the dark window where the ocean was an invisible presence, a low, rhythmic breathing beyond the glass. “You left a voicemail,” she said. “That morning. I listened to it a few weeks ago.”

She did not tell him that she had saved it across three phone upgrades. That she had listened to it on the train that brought her to this coast, the vowels of his voice worn smooth as sea glass from repetition.

He pulled his hands from his jacket pockets and held them in front of him, studying his own palms. “I shouldn’t have said the things I said in that message.”

“You didn’t say much,” she said. “Just my name, and that you were sorry. That you wished I’d told you I was leaving. That the door was open. And then you hung up.”

He looked up. “I practiced it for an hour. I wanted to sound calm. Not desperate.”

“You sounded sad.”

“I was sad.” The words came out raw, without artifice. “I was sad for a long time.”

She felt the heat of the folded towel against her palms. She pressed it into her chest like a shield. “I didn’t know how to say goodbye to you. So I just didn’t.”

“You made a choice.”

“I made a bad choice.” The admission came out tight, defensive. She hadn’t meant to say it. The words had formed in her throat and escaped before she could catch them.

Leo moved then. He walked to the bench and sat down, the lantern at his feet. He did not look at her. He stared at the dead washing machine, its dark door a porthole into nothing. “I called you for six weeks after you left. Every Wednesday, around eight. I thought maybe if I kept it routine, you’d pick up.”

“I didn’t know what to say.” Her voice was barely audible.

“You could have said anything. Even ‘I don’t want to talk to you.’ That would have been something.”

The wind pushed against the laundromat’s glass door, and the building creaked. The sound was like a ship settling in its moorings. Maya set the folded towel on the bench beside him, a careful distance away.

“I have this moment I think about,” he said, still not looking at her. “The last time we sat on your porch. It was September, and you were reading a book with a cracked spine. You read me a paragraph about a woman who builds a boat out of her old furniture. I laughed, and you said, ‘That’s not funny, it’s hopeful.’” He finally turned his head. His eyes were dark in the low light, but there was no accusation in them. “I didn’t know then that you were already building your boat.”

She swallowed. The floor seemed to tilt under her feet. “I wasn’t building a boat. I was drowning.”

He exhaled, a long, slow breath. “That’s what I didn’t see. I thought you were just quiet. I thought you were peaceful. I didn’t know you were holding your breath.”

She sat down on the floor, not on the bench, but on the cold linoleum, her back against the humming husk of a vending machine. The lantern’s light caught the silver threads in her hair, strands that had not been there when she lived on his street.

“I’m sorry I didn’t tell you,” she said. “I’m sorry I let you call an empty phone. I’m sorry I kept the lamp but not the courage.”

He bent down and picked up the lantern. He held it out to her, handle first. “Take it. For the walk back to wherever you’re staying.”

She didn’t move to take it. “The power will come back on.”

“Probably not for a while. And I know this town in the dark. You don’t.”

She reached up and wrapped her fingers around the warm metal handle. Their hands did not touch. He let go immediately, leaving the weight of the lantern in her grip.

“Leo.”

“Yeah.”

“The voicemail. I listened to it so many times I wore out the words. I think I was trying to hear you forgive me.”
