The first thing most people want to know about a hotel is what sort of looking place it is. All right, here you are. Some factories, especially those that are proud of their appearance, carry their own picture on their stationery. There is nothing to say against it, but one of the most beautiful factories in America has on its letter head only the name of the firm, the address, and a small trademark engraved in black. Sometimes a picture, in a sales letter, for instance, supplements the written matter in a most effective way. And whenever any kind of device is really helpful it should by all means be used, subject only to the limits of good taste.

It is more practical in business to use standard size envelopes. If window envelopes are used the window should be clear, the paper white or nearly so, and the typewritten address a good honest black. The enclosure should fit snugly and should be placed so that the address is in plain view without having to be jiggled around in the envelope first. A letter passes through the hands of several postal clerks before it reaches the person to whom it is addressed, and if each one of them has to stop to play with it awhile an appreciable amount of time is lost, not to mention the strain it puts on their respective tempers. The paper of which an envelope is made should always be opaque enough to conceal the contents of the letter.

Practically all business letters are typewritten. Occasionally a "Help Wanted" advertisement requests that the answer be in the applicant's own handwriting, but even this is rare. In most places the typing is taken care of by girls who have been trained for the purpose, but most young girls just entering business are highly irresponsible, and it is necessary for the men and women who dictate the letters to know what constitutes a pleasing make-up so that they can point out the flaws and give suggestions for doing away with them.

message.content:
**商业信函写作要义**

半数的商业信函本不应被写出，而余下半数中，又有许多内容残缺或逻辑混乱，致使原本只需两封信便可完成并归档的交易，时常需要六至八封信才能了结。

一封优质信函源于清晰的思考与周密的规划。就销售信函而言，有时需要数周方能成稿；但对于日常通信，通常数分钟便已足够。耗时长短并不重要——关键在于最终产出的信函质量。

商业通信类书籍提供了诸多衡量信函优劣的规则与标准。然而，一切经验法则皆有风险，真正不可或缺的要点仅有两项。其余规则，唯有在服务于这两项核心时才有价值：其一，信函必须成功传达其核心思想；其二，信函必须为其所在机构赢得善意。最佳的信函，正是能以最少的篇幅（及纸张），以最礼貌、最周全的方式实现上述两项目标者。

每封信函皆应有其存在的理由，即便只是为了向客户道一声“谢谢”。我们国家有太多精力耗费于徒劳无益的努力——漫无目的的广告、毫无价值的推销、效率低下的信函写作，以及无数其他诸如此类的事务。大量精力被倾注于打字机上，敲打出全然无用的信函，将纸张浪费于一种本可用于更有价值之途的徒劳。

一封优秀的信函，绝不会让读者的注意力游离于内容之外，转而关注信函本身。去年，某出版社曾发出一百封测试信函，为其一本书籍做广告。结果仅收到三封回信，且无一订阅该书，而全是对信函本身的赞誉：一位商业英语教师声称将以此信作为课堂教学范本，另两位则祝贺该公司拥有如此出色的通信撰稿人。该信函外观精美，出自大学毕业生之手，措辞清晰、准确且富有文采，然而它却毫无成效。**任何信函或广告，若将注意力引向自身而非其所要传达的信息，便毫无价值。**

信函的外观布局并无太多展现个性的空间。惯例已将其标准化，若对传统格式做出惊世骇俗的变动，所体现的并非创新，而是乖僻。这就像那位在隆冬最寒冷的清晨，光着头、未穿外套与袜子、仅着白色法兰绒裤与网球鞋，漫步于第五大道的惊世骇俗的绅士——他能引人注目，却令人不寒而栗。

质地优良的素白信纸，始终体现着高雅的品位。某些色泽暗淡的信纸亦无不可，但花哨的颜色、浮夸的设计与繁复的信头，在一切高端商业领域中皆属禁忌。简明扼要、提供清晰实用信息的信头方为最佳。公司名称与地址（“纽约”或“芝加哥”这类简称并不充分——尽管许多地方的地址仅此而已），如有电报挂号的地址、电话号码，以及不显眼的商标（“显眼”与“醒目”之间有所区别），便已涵盖一家商业机构所需的一切。

酒店常在其信纸上印上自身图片，此举绝无谦逊可言，但自有其充分理由——大多数人了解一家酒店，首要关心的便是其外观。那么，请看这里便是。一些工厂，尤其是那些对其外观引以为傲的，亦会将自身图片印于信纸之上。这无可指摘。然而，美国最美丽的工厂之一，其信头上仅有公司名称、地址与一枚黑色雕刻的小小商标。有时，例如在销售信函中，图片能以极为有效的方式辅助文字内容。只要确实有助于传达信息，且不逾越品位的界限，自当善加利用。

在商业实践中，使用标准尺寸的信封更为实用。若使用窗口信封，窗口应保持清晰，纸张应为白色或近乎白色，打印的地址应为清晰的黑色。内件应贴合紧密，放置得当，确保无需在信封内来回拨弄便可清晰看到地址。一封信函在到达收件人之前，会经手数位邮政职员之手，若每位职员都需停下摆弄一会儿，便会造成可观的时间损失，更不必说对其耐心的考验了。制作信封的纸张，应始终具备足够的遮蔽性，以保密信件内容。

几乎所有商业信函均为打字而成。偶尔，“招聘”广告会要求应聘者以手写体回复，但即便此类情况亦属罕见。在大多数地方，打字工作由经过专门培训的女性职员完成。然而，许多初入商界的年轻女孩责任心并不强，因此，口述信函的男士与女士们，有必要了解何为悦目的信函外观，以便指出瑕疵并提出改进建议。

messages.content:
Translate the following English children’s literature passage into Simplified Chinese. Requirements: keep the playful tone, concrete images, and childlike reasoning; translate measurements and place names naturally; do not add explanations or moral lessons.

素材来源：Lewis Carroll, Alice's Adventures in Wonderland, Chapter I excerpt
来源链接：https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/11
版权/授权说明：Project Gutenberg lists this eBook as public domain in the USA.

【待处理素材】
Alice was beginning to get very tired of sitting by her sister on the bank, and of having nothing to do: once or twice she had peeped into the book her sister was reading, but it had no pictures or conversations in it, “and what is the use of a book,” thought Alice “without pictures or conversations?”

So she was considering in her own mind (as well as she could, for the hot day made her feel very sleepy and stupid), whether the pleasure of making a daisy-chain would be worth the trouble of getting up and picking the daisies, when suddenly a White Rabbit with pink eyes ran close by her.

There was nothing so _very_ remarkable in that; nor did Alice think it so _very_ much out of the way to hear the Rabbit say to itself, “Oh dear! Oh dear! I shall be late!” (when she thought it over afterwards, it occurred to her that she ought to have wondered at this, but at the time it all seemed quite natural); but when the Rabbit actually _took a watch out of its waistcoat-pocket_, and looked at it, and then hurried on, Alice started to her feet, for it flashed across her mind that she had never before seen a rabbit with either a waistcoat-pocket, or a watch to take out of it, and burning with curiosity, she ran across the field after it, and fortunately was just in time to see it pop down a large rabbit-hole under the hedge.

In another moment down went Alice after it, never once considering how in the world she was to get out again.

The rabbit-hole went straight on like a tunnel for some way, and then dipped suddenly down, so suddenly that Alice had not a moment to think about stopping herself before she found herself falling down a very deep well.

Either the well was very deep, or she fell very slowly, for she had plenty of time as she went down to look about her and to wonder what was going to happen next. First, she tried to look down and make out what she was coming to, but it was too dark to see anything; then she looked at the sides of the well, and noticed that they were filled with cupboards and book-shelves; here and there she saw maps and pictures hung upon pegs. She took down a jar from one of the shelves as she passed; it was labelled “ORANGE MARMALADE”, but to her great disappointment it was empty: she did not like to drop the jar for fear of killing somebody underneath, so managed to put it into one of the cupboards as she fell past it.

“Well!” thought Alice to herself, “after such a fall as this, I shall think nothing of tumbling down stairs! How brave they’ll all think me at home! Why, I wouldn’t say anything about it, even if I fell off the top of the house!” (Which was very likely true.)

Down, down, down. Would the fall _never_ come to an end? “I wonder how many miles I’ve fallen by this time?” she said aloud. “I must be getting somewhere near the centre of the earth. Let me see: that would be four thousand miles down, I think—” (for, you see, Alice had learnt several things of this sort in her lessons in the schoolroom, and though this was not a _very_ good opportunity for showing off her knowledge, as there was no one to listen to her, still it was good practice to say it over) “—yes, that’s about the right distance—but then I wonder what Latitude or Longitude I’ve got to?” (Alice had no idea what Latitude was, or Longitude either, but thought they were nice grand words to say.)

message.content:
爱丽丝陪姐姐坐在河岸上，实在无事可做，渐渐觉得厌倦了。她偷偷朝姐姐读的书瞄了一两眼，可那书上既没有图画，也没有对话。“一本书要是没有图画和对话，”爱丽丝心里琢磨，“那还有什么意思呢？”

于是她暗自思忖（心里能想的多明白就多明白吧，毕竟大热天让她又困又迷糊）：编个雏菊花环的乐趣，值不值得她费劲站起来去摘花呢？就在这时，一只粉红眼睛的白兔突然从她身边跑过。

这原本算不上什么稀奇事；爱丽丝听见兔子自言自语“天哪！天哪！我要迟到了！”时，也没觉得有什么不对劲（事后回想起来，她觉得自己本该对此感到惊讶，可那时一切都显得那么自然）；可等到那兔子真从马甲口袋里掏出一块怀表，看了看，又匆匆赶路时，爱丽丝一下子跳了起来——她脑海里猛然闪过一个念头：她还从没见过兔子有马甲口袋，更没见过能从里面掏出表来的兔子。她满心好奇，追着它跑过田野，幸好赶上了，正好看见它钻进树篱下的一个大兔子洞里。

紧接着，爱丽丝也一头扎了进去，压根儿没想过自己究竟还能不能出来。

那兔子洞先像隧道一样笔直延伸了一段，然后突然向下倾斜——陡得爱丽丝还没顾得上想怎么停住，就发现自己掉进了一口极深的井里。

要么是井太深，要么是她坠落得太慢，总之她往下落的时候有足够的时间东张西望，琢磨接下来会发生什么。她先试着往下看，想弄清自己会掉到什么地方，可下面太黑，什么也看不见；接着她打量井壁，发现上面摆满了橱柜和书架，这儿那儿还挂着地图和图画。她经过一个架子时顺手拿下一个小罐子，标签上写着“橘子酱”，可让她大失所望的是，罐子是空的。她不敢把罐子扔下去，生怕砸死底下的人，于是经过一个橱柜时，又想法子把它放了回去。

“好哇！”爱丽丝心里想，“经过这么一次摔跤，以后从楼梯上滚下去算得了什么！家里人准会觉得我多勇敢呀！哼，就算我从房顶上掉下来，我也绝不会提一个字的！”（这话倒真有可能。）

往下掉，往下掉，往下掉。这坠落的劲儿难道永远没个完？“我倒想知道，这会儿我掉了多少英里了？”她大声说，“我一定快到地心了吧。让我想想：那得有四千英里深呢——”要知道，爱丽丝在课堂上可学过不少这类知识，虽说眼下没人在听，不是显摆学问的好机会，但能把这些话念叨一遍也算练习。“——没错，差不多就是这么远——可我又到了什么纬度、经度呢？”（爱丽丝压根儿不懂纬度和经度是什么，不过她觉得这些词说出来很好听、很气派。）

messages.content:
Translate the following English news-release excerpt into Simplified Chinese. Requirements: accurate, natural, suitable for a general news audience; keep the institutional name as “美国地质调查局（USGS）” on first mention; do not add facts or commentary.

素材来源：U.S. Geological Survey news release: New USGS diagram re-envisions how Earth’s water cycles the planet
来源链接：https://www.usgs.gov/news/national-news-release/new-usgs-diagram-re-envisions-how-earths-most-precious-commodity-cycles
版权/授权说明：USGS-authored information is considered U.S. public domain.

【待处理素材】
RESTON, Va.— Starting today, educators around the nation will have a more accurate and more comprehensive tool to explain the Earth’s water cycle with the unveiling of the new U.S. Geological Survey water cycle diagram.

The revised version replaces one used by hundreds of thousands of educators and students internationally every year since 2000. So why the new water cycle? This depiction brings humans into the picture, showing the water cycle as a complex interplay of small, interconnected cycles that people interact with and influence, rather than one big circle.

USGS experts consulted with more than 100 educators and more than 30 hydrologic experts to develop the new diagram. The vast amounts of water data that USGS has collected in recent decades has informed a nuanced perspective of the water cycle, demonstrating how both its human and natural components are interconnected.

Where the existing water cycle diagram depicted only the natural aspects of the cycle, the new version depicts how Earth’s water moves and is stored, both naturally and because of human actions. Not only does the new diagram illustrate a more comprehensive view of the water cycle, it draws on principles of information design to focus attention on the water as it moves through the natural and built environment. It shows how multiple ecosystems—including a coastal plain, dry basin, wet basin, and agricultural basin—are connected across watersheds and at continental scales.

The new diagram will initially be available in both English and Spanish, with the expectation it will be translated into many other languages by end users, as was the previous version.

message.content:
弗吉尼亚州雷斯顿——自今日起，全美教育工作者将拥有一套更精准、更全面的工具来阐释地球水循环——美国地质调查局（USGS）新版水循环示意图正式发布。

此次修订版取代了自2000年以来每年被全球数十万教育工作者及学生使用的旧版示意图。为何要推出新版水循环图？新图将人类活动纳入画面，展示水循环并非一个单一的大循环，而是由众多微小、相互关联的循环构成的复杂系统，人类既与之相互作用，又对其产生影响。

美国地质调查局（USGS）专家咨询了超过100位教育工作者及30余位水文学专家，共同完成了新示意图的绘制。该机构近几十年来收集的海量水资源数据，为理解水循环提供了更精细的视角，揭示了其中自然与人类组成部分如何相互关联。

旧版示意图仅描绘水循环的自然层面，而新版本则展示了地球水资源在自然与人类活动共同作用下的流动与储存方式。新图不仅呈现了更全面的水循环全貌，还借鉴信息设计原理，将注意力聚焦于水在自然与人工环境中的运动过程。它展现了多个生态系统——包括沿海平原、干旱盆地、湿润盆地及农业盆地——如何在流域与大陆尺度上相互连接。

新示意图将首先提供英文和西班牙语版本，预计最终用户将像对待旧版一样，将其翻译成多种其他语言。

messages.content:
Translate the following English literary passage into Simplified Chinese. Requirements: preserve emotional tension, social nuance, and indirect narration; do not make the style slangy; names may be translated conventionally or kept in English consistently. Do not add explanations.

素材来源：Jane Austen, Pride and Prejudice, Chapter XXXIV excerpt
来源链接：https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/1342
版权/授权说明：Project Gutenberg lists this eBook as public domain in the USA.

【待处理素材】
When they were gone, Elizabeth, as if intending to exasperate herself as much as possible against Mr. Darcy, chose for her employment the examination of all the letters which Jane had written to her since her being in Kent. They contained no actual complaint, nor was there any revival of past occurrences, or any communication of present suffering. But in all, and in almost every line of each, there was a want of that cheerfulness which had been used to characterize her style, and which, proceeding from the serenity of a mind at ease with itself, and kindly disposed towards everyone, had been scarcely ever clouded. Elizabeth noticed every sentence conveying the idea of uneasiness, with an attention which it had hardly received on the first perusal. Mr. Darcy’s shameful boast of what misery he had been able to inflict gave her a keener sense of her sister’s sufferings. It was some consolation to think that his visit to Rosings was to end on the day after the next, and a still greater that in less than a fortnight she should herself be with Jane again, and enabled to contribute to the recovery of her spirits, by all that affection could do.

She could not think of Darcy’s leaving Kent without remembering that his cousin was to go with him; but Colonel Fitzwilliam had made it clear that he had no intentions at all, and, agreeable as he was, she did not mean to be unhappy about him.

While settling this point, she was suddenly roused by the sound of the door-bell; and her spirits were a little fluttered by the idea of its being Colonel Fitzwilliam himself, who had once before called late in the evening, and might now come to inquire particularly after her. But this idea was soon banished, and her spirits were very differently affected, when, to her utter amazement, she saw Mr. Darcy walk into the room. In a hurried manner he immediately began an inquiry after her health, imputing his visit to a wish of hearing that she were better. She answered him with cold civility. He sat down for a few moments, and then getting up walked about the room. Elizabeth was surprised, but said not a word. After a silence of several minutes, he came towards her in an agitated manner, and thus began:--

“In vain have I struggled. It will not do. My feelings will not be repressed. You must allow me to tell you how ardently I admire and love you.”

Elizabeth’s astonishment was beyond expression. She stared, coloured, doubted, and was silent. This he considered sufficient encouragement, and the avowal of all that he felt and had long felt for her immediately followed. He spoke well; but there were feelings besides those of the heart to be detailed, and he was not more eloquent on the subject of tenderness than of pride. His sense of her inferiority, of its being a degradation, of the family obstacles which judgment had always opposed to inclination, were dwelt on with a warmth which seemed due to the consequence he was wounding, but was very unlikely to recommend his suit.

In spite of her deeply-rooted dislike, she could not be insensible to the compliment of such a man’s affection, and though her intentions did not vary for an instant, she was at first sorry for the pain he was to receive; till roused to resentment by his subsequent language, she lost all compassion in anger. She tried, however, to compose herself to answer him with patience, when he should have done. He concluded with representing to her the strength of that attachment which in spite of all his endeavours he had found impossible to conquer; and with expressing his hope that it would now be rewarded by her acceptance of his hand. As he said this she could easily see that he had no doubt of a favourable answer. He _spoke_ of apprehension and anxiety, but his countenance expressed real security. Such a circumstance could only exasperate farther; and when he ceased the colour rose into her cheeks and she said,--

“In such cases as this, it is, I believe, the established mode to express a sense of obligation for the sentiments avowed, however unequally they may be returned. It is natural that obligation should be felt, and if I could _feel_ gratitude, I would now thank you. But I cannot--I have never desired your good opinion, and you have certainly bestowed it most unwillingly. I am sorry to have occasioned pain to anyone. It has been most unconsciously done, however, and I hope will be of short duration. The feelings which you tell me have long prevented the acknowledgment of your regard can have little difficulty in overcoming it after this explanation.”

message.content:
他们走后，伊丽莎白仿佛故意要激起对达西先生的最大反感，便挑出吉英自到肯特以来写给她的所有信件，逐一细读。信中并无实际抱怨，也不曾重提旧事，更未诉说当下的苦楚。然而每封信，乃至几乎每一行字，都少了往昔那股欢快的神气——那原本是她行文的特色，源自内心宁静、与己相安、对人友善，几乎从未蒙上阴翳。伊丽莎白留意每一句透露不安的话语，其专注程度远超初读之时。达西先生竟夸口自己给人造成了何等痛苦，这份可耻的炫耀让她更真切地感受到姐姐所受的煎熬。想到他后天便要离开罗新斯，多少算个安慰；而更大的慰藉是，不到两周自己便能回到吉英身边，能用全部柔情助她重振精神。

她一想到达西离开肯特，便不免忆起他的表兄弟也要同行；但费茨威廉上校已经表明他毫无此意，而且他虽讨人喜欢，伊丽莎白也不打算为他烦恼。

正厘清这一点时，门铃声突然惊动了她；她心头微乱，以为是费茨威廉上校本人——他曾在深夜造访过一次，此刻或许又来专程问候她。但这个念头很快被打消，她的心境亦截然不同——她惊愕至极，看见达西先生走进了房间。他带着仓促的神情立刻问候她的健康，声称此番来访是希望听到她好转的消息。她以冷淡的客套作答。他坐了片刻，随即起身在屋里踱步。伊丽莎白诧异不已，却一言不发。沉默数分钟后，他激动地向她走来，开口道：

“我挣扎也无用。办不到。我无法抑制自己的感情。请允许我告诉你，我多么热切地仰慕你、爱你。”

伊丽莎白惊诧得无以复加。她瞪着眼，红了脸，狐疑着，默不作声。他以为这便是足够的鼓励，于是立刻倾诉了胸中对她积存已久的情感。他说得动人；但除了心曲，还需细述其他情绪，而他在提及柔情时，并不比提及骄傲时更显口才。他谈及她的出身低微，谈及这桩事有失体统，谈及理智始终反对情感的那些家庭障碍——这些话说得那样慷慨激昂，仿佛是在维护自己那受挫的自尊，但于求婚之事却绝无裨益。

尽管她对他早已深恶痛绝，但这样一个男人的爱慕终究是种恭维，她不可能无动于衷；虽然她的心意一刻也未曾动摇，但起初还是为他将要承受的痛苦感到几分歉疚；直到他后来说的话激起了她的愤慨，愤怒之下，她所有的同情便荡然无存。然而她还是尽量镇定下来，等他讲完，再耐心作答。他最后向她表明，这份情意如何坚韧不拔，任凭他如何努力也无法克服；并希望如今能得到她的垂允，接受他的求婚。说这话时，她轻易看出他对获得肯定答复毫不怀疑。他嘴上说着担忧与焦虑，脸上却流露出十足的把握。这种情况只能令她更加恼怒；他话音一落，她便双颊泛红，开口道：

“我相信，遇到这类情形，通常的方式是对方表白情意后，即使回报难称对等，也理应表示领情。领情原是人之常情，倘若我能生出感激之情，此刻便会向你道谢。可我不能——我从未希求你的好感，而你这好感也确非心甘情愿。令任何人痛苦，我都深感歉疚。然而这一切都非有意为之，但愿痛苦不会长久。据你所说，这份情意长久以来阻碍着你向我表露心意，那么经过此番解释之后，克服这份情意应该并不困难。”

messages.content:
Translate the following technical explainer into Simplified Chinese. Requirements: preserve the technical meaning, use clear science-education language, and keep terms such as evaporation, evapotranspiration, precipitation, runoff, infiltration, groundwater recharge, and aquifer accurate. Do not add external information.

素材来源：U.S. Geological Survey Water Science School: Water cycle
来源链接：https://www.usgs.gov/water-science-school/water-cycle
版权/授权说明：USGS-authored information is considered U.S. public domain.

【待处理素材】
The water cycle describes where water is on Earth and how it moves. Human water use, land use, and climate change all impact the water cycle. By understanding these impacts, we can work toward using water sustainably.

Viewed from space, the most striking feature of our planet is the water. In both liquid and frozen form, it covers 75% of the Earth's surface. It fills the sky with clouds. Water is practically everywhere on Earth, from inside the planet's rocky crust to inside the cells of the human body. What's important to keep in mind is that all of this water is in constant motion across our planet.

Water is stored in the atmosphere, on the land surface, and below the ground. It can be a liquid, a solid, or a gas. Liquid water can be fresh or saline. Water moves between the places it is stored. Water moves at large scales, through watersheds, the atmosphere, and below the Earth's surface. Water moves at very small scales too. It is in us, plants, and other organisms.

Oceans store 96% of all water on Earth. Ocean water is saline, meaning it’s salty. On land, saline water is stored in saline lakes. The rest of the water on Earth is fresh water. Fresh water is stored in liquid form in freshwater lakes, artificial reservoirs, rivers, and wetlands. Water is stored in solid, frozen form in ice sheets and glaciers, and in snowpack at high elevations or near Earth's poles.

As it moves, water can change form between liquid, solid, and gas. Circulation mixes water in the oceans and transports water vapor in the atmosphere. Water moves between the atmosphere and the surface through evaporation, evapotranspiration, and precipitation. Water moves across the surface through snowmelt, runoff, and streamflow. Water moves into the ground through infiltration and groundwater recharge. Underground, groundwater flows within aquifers. Groundwater can return to the surface through natural discharge into rivers, the ocean, and from springs.

Water moves naturally and because of human actions. Energy from the sun and the force of gravity drive the continual movement of water between pools. The sun’s energy causes liquid water to evaporate into water vapor. Evapotranspiration is the main way water moves into the atmosphere from the land surface and oceans. Gravity causes water to flow downward on land. It causes rain, snow, and hail to fall from clouds.

Humans and other organisms rely on water for life. The amount of water that is available depends on how much water there is in each pool, when and how fast water moves through the water cycle, and how clean the water is. By understanding human impacts on the water cycle, we can work toward using water sustainably.

message.content:
水循环描述了水在地球上的分布及其运动方式。人类用水、土地利用以及气候变化都会影响水循环。通过理解这些影响，我们能够朝着可持续用水的方向努力。

从太空俯瞰，我们星球最引人注目的特征便是水。无论是液态还是固态形式，水覆盖了地球表面75%的面积，并让天空中布满云层。实际上，水几乎遍布地球各处——从星球内部的岩石圈到人体细胞中。需要牢记的是，所有这些水都在我们的星球上持续运动着。

水储存在大气中、地表上以及地下。它可以呈现液态、固态或气态。液态水既可以是淡水，也可以是咸水。水在不同储存区域之间移动。水的运动规模宏大，流经流域、大气层和地表之下；水的运动尺度也十分微小，存在于我们体内、植物以及其他生物体中。

海洋储存了地球上96%的水。海水是咸的，即含盐的。在地表上，咸水储存在咸水湖中。地球上其余的水是淡水。淡水以液态形式储存在淡水湖、人工水库、河流和湿地中。水以固态冰冻形式储存在冰盖、冰川以及高海拔地区或地球两极附近的积雪中。

在运动过程中，水可以在液态、固态和气态之间转换形态。环流使海洋中的水混合，并将水汽输送到大气中。水通过蒸发、蒸散发和降水在大气与地表之间运动。水通过融雪、径流和河流流动在地表运动。水通过入渗和地下水补给进入地下。在地下，地下水在含水层中流动。地下水可以通过自然排泄方式——如流入河流、海洋或从泉水中涌出——返回地表。

水的运动既包含自然过程，也受人类活动影响。太阳的能量和重力驱动着水在各储库之间持续运动。太阳的能量使液态水蒸发为水蒸气。蒸散发是水从地表和海洋进入大气的主要途径。重力使水在地表向下流动，并导致雨、雪和冰雹从云中降落。

人类和其他生物依赖水维持生命。可用水量的多少取决于每个储库中水的储存量、水在水循环中流动的时间和速度，以及水的洁净程度。通过理解人类活动对水循环的影响，我们可以朝着可持续用水的方向努力。

messages.content:
Write a single-file Python 3 command-line program named `schedule_optimizer.py`. Use no third-party packages. Keep the code under 1,000 lines.

Problem: Given a list of candidate work sessions, choose a non-overlapping subset that maximizes total value. Each session has an ID, start time, end time, and value. This is weighted interval scheduling with reconstruction.

Input: read JSON from standard input. Format:
{
  "sessions": [
    {"id": "a", "start": "2026-05-01T09:00:00", "end": "2026-05-01T10:30:00", "value": 5}
  ]
}

Requirements:
1. Parse ISO-like timestamps without time zones using Python standard library.
2. Reject sessions whose end is not after start or whose value is negative.
3. Output JSON with `max_value`, `selected_ids` in chronological order, and `rejected` entries with reasons.
4. If multiple optimal solutions exist, choose the one with fewer sessions; if still tied, choose lexicographically smaller list of IDs.
5. Include an efficient O(n log n) dynamic programming solution.
6. Include built-in tests. When run with `--test`, execute tests and print a short success message. Normal mode should print only JSON.

Output only the complete Python code. Do not include explanations.

Write a single-file Node.js HTTP server named `server.js`. Use only built-in Node.js modules; no Express, no npm packages. Keep the code under 1,000 lines.

Task: implement a small JSON API for a help-desk ticket queue stored in a local JSON file.

Data model:
- Ticket: `id`, `subject`, `description`, `priority` (`low`, `medium`, `high`), `status` (`open`, `in_progress`, `resolved`), `created_at`, `updated_at`.

API requirements:
1. `GET /tickets` with optional query params `status` and `priority`.
2. `POST /tickets` to create a ticket. Validate required fields.
3. `GET /tickets/{id}` to fetch one ticket.
4. `PATCH /tickets/{id}` to update `subject`, `description`, `priority`, or `status`.
5. `DELETE /tickets/{id}` to delete a ticket.
6. `GET /metrics` returning counts by status and priority plus average age of open tickets in seconds.

Engineering requirements:
- Read/write a `tickets.json` file safely enough for sequential requests.
- Return JSON for all responses, including errors.
- Include CORS headers for local browser testing.
- Handle invalid JSON, unknown routes, unsupported methods, and missing IDs.
- Start on `127.0.0.1:8080` by default.

Output only the complete JavaScript code. Do not include explanations.

Create a single-file frontend app named `index.html`. Do not use external libraries, CDNs, images, or build tools. Keep the total code under 1,000 lines.

Task: build an accessible “meeting notes board” for a small team.

Functional requirements:
1. Users can add a note with title, owner, meeting date, tags, and body.
2. Notes appear as cards and can be filtered by owner, tag, and free-text search.
3. A note can be marked as “Action Required,” “Decision,” or “Reference.”
4. Users can edit and delete notes.
5. Persist all data in `localStorage`.
6. Provide a compact statistics strip: total notes, action-required count, unique owners, and most common tag.
7. Responsive layout: two-column card grid on wide screens, single column on narrow screens.
8. Basic accessibility: labels for inputs, keyboard-operable controls, visible focus states.

Output only the complete `index.html` content, including HTML, CSS, and JavaScript. Do not include explanatory text.

Read the public-domain passage below and produce a structured English summary for modern business readers.

Output requirements:
1. A 100-word executive summary.
2. Five principles of effective business writing implied by the author.
3. A table with two columns: “Original-era advice” and “Modern equivalent.” Include at least five rows.
4. Identify any advice that may be dated and explain how to update it without losing the underlying principle.
5. Do not add claims that are not grounded in the passage.

素材来源：The Book of Business Etiquette, chapter “The Business of Writing” longer excerpt
来源链接：https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/23025
版权/授权说明：Project Gutenberg lists this eBook as public domain in the USA.

【待处理素材】
Half the business letters which are written should never be written at all, and of the other half so many are incomplete or incoherent that a transaction which could be finished and filed away in two letters frequently requires six or eight.

A good letter is the result of clear thinking and careful planning. In the case of the sales-letter it sometimes takes several weeks to write one, but for ordinary correspondence a few minutes is usually all that is necessary. The length of time does not matter--it is the sort of letter which is produced at the end of it.

Books of commercial correspondence give a number of rules and standards by which a letter can be measured. But all rules of thumb are dangerous, and there are only two items which are essential. The others are valuable only as they contribute to them. The letter must succeed in getting its idea across and it must build up good will for its firm. And the best one is the one which accomplishes this most courteously and most completely in the briefest space of time (and paper).

There should be a reason back of every letter if it is only to say "Thank you" to a customer. Too much of our national energy goes up in waste effort, in aimless advertising, worthless salesmanship, ineffective letter writing, and in a thousand and one other ways. A lot of it is hammered out on the typewriters transcribing perfectly useless letters to paper which might really be worth something if it were given over to a different purpose.

A good letter never attracts the mind of the reader to itself as a thing apart from its contents. Last year a publishing house sent out a hundred test letters advertising one of their books. Three answers came back, none of them ordering the book, but all three praising the letter. One was from a teacher of commercial English who declared that he was going to use it as a model in his classes, and the other two congratulated the firm on having so excellent a correspondent. The physical make-up of the letter was attractive, it was written by a college graduate and couched in clear, correct, and colorful English. And yet it was no good. No _letter and no advertisement is any good which calls attention to itself instead of the message it is trying to deliver_.

There is not much room for individuality in the make-up of a letter. Custom has standardized it, and startling variations from the conventional format indicates freakishness rather than originality. They are like that astonishing gentleman who walks up Fifth Avenue on the coldest mornings in the year, bareheaded, coatless, sockless, clad in white flannels and tennis slippers. He attracts attention, but he makes us shiver.

Plain white paper of good quality is always in good taste. Certain dull-tinted papers are not bad, but gaudy colors, flashy designs, and ornate letter heads are taboo in all high types of business. Simple headings giving explicit and useful information are best. The name and address of the firm (and "New York" or "Chicago" is not sufficient in spite of the fact that a good many places go into no more detail than this), the cable address if it has one, the telephone number and the trademark if it is an inconspicuous one (there is a difference between _conspicuous_ and _distinctive_) are all that any business house needs.

Hotels are often pictured on their own stationery in a way that is anything but modest, but there is a very good reason for it. The first thing most people want to know about a hotel is what sort of looking place it is. All right, here you are. Some factories, especially those that are proud of their appearance, carry their own picture on their stationery. There is nothing to say against it, but one of the most beautiful factories in America has on its letter head only the name of the firm, the address, and a small trademark engraved in black. Sometimes a picture, in a sales letter, for instance, supplements the written matter in a most effective way. And whenever any kind of device is really helpful it should by all means be used, subject only to the limits of good taste.

It is more practical in business to use standard size envelopes. If window envelopes are used the window should be clear, the paper white or nearly so, and the typewritten address a good honest black. The enclosure should fit snugly and should be placed so that the address is in plain view without having to be jiggled around in the envelope first. A letter passes through the hands of several postal clerks before it reaches the person to whom it is addressed, and if each one of them has to stop to play with it awhile an appreciable amount of time is lost, not to mention the strain it puts on their respective tempers. The paper of which an envelope is made should always be opaque enough to conceal the contents of the letter.

Practically all business letters are typewritten. Occasionally a "Help Wanted" advertisement requests that the answer be in the applicant's own handwriting, but even this is rare. In most places the typing is taken care of by girls who have been trained for the purpose, but most young girls just entering business are highly irresponsible, and it is necessary for the men and women who dictate the letters to know what constitutes a pleasing make-up so that they can point out the flaws and give suggestions for doing away with them.

The letter should be arranged symmetrically on the page with ample margins all around. Nothing but experience in copying her own notes will teach a stenographer to estimate them correctly so that she will not have to rewrite badly placed letters. It is a little point, but an important one.

Each subject considered in a letter should be treated in a separate paragraph, and each paragraph should be set off from the others by a wider space than that between the lines, double space between the paragraphs when there is single space between the lines, triple space between the paragraphs when there is a double space between the lines, and so on.

A business letter should handle only one subject. Two letters should be dispatched if two subjects are to be covered. This enables the house receiving the letter to file it so that it can be found when it is needed.

When a letter is addressed to an individual it is better to begin "Dear Mr. Brown" or "My dear Mr. Brown" than "Dear Sir" or "My dear Sir." "Gentlemen" or "Ladies" is sometime used in salutation when a letter is addressed to a group. "Dear Friend" is permissible in general letters sent out to persons of both sexes. Honorary titles should be used in the address when they take the place of "Mr.," such titles as Reverend, Doctor, Honorable (abbreviated to Rev., Dr., Hon.,) and the like. Titles should not be dropped except in the case of personal letters.

Special care should be taken with the outside address. State abbreviations should be used sparingly when there is a chance of confusion as in the case of Ga., Va., La., and Pa. "City" is not sufficient and should never be used. Nor should the name of the state ever be omitted even when the letter is addressed to some other point in the same state, as from New York to Brooklyn. And postage should be complete. A letter on which there is two cents due has placed itself under a pretty severe handicap before it is opened.

It is astonishing how many letters go out every day unsigned, lacking enclosures, carrying the wrong addresses, bearing insufficient postage, and showing other evidences of carelessness and thoughtlessness. In a town in New England last year one of the specialty shops received at Christmas time twenty different lots of money--money orders, stamps, and cash--by mail, not one of which bore the slightest clue to the identity of the sender. Countless times during the year this happens in every mail order house.

message.content:
Here is a structured English summary of the provided passage, tailored for modern business readers.

### Executive Summary (100 words)

Half of all business letters should never be written, and most of the rest are so incoherent that they multiply correspondence needlessly. Effective business writing stems from clear thinking and careful planning, not length. The two essential goals are conveying the idea and building goodwill. The best communication is courteous, complete, and brief. A letter fails if it draws attention to itself rather than its message. Standard formats are preferable to “freakish” creativity. Use plain, high-quality paper; simple headings; and standard envelopes. Handle only one subject per letter, address individuals by name when possible, and proofread ruthlessly to avoid missing enclosures or incorrect postage.

### Five Principles of Effective Business Writing

1.  **Prioritize Clarity and Completeness:** Every communication must successfully get its core idea across. Incomplete or incoherent writing wastes time and multiplies follow-up.
2.  **Build Goodwill:** The letter should courteously represent the firm. Everything from the salutation to the paper quality contributes to the reader’s impression of the organization.
3.  **Prioritize the Message Over the Medium:** No communication is effective if it calls attention to itself (style, cleverness, or design) instead of the message it delivers.
4.  **Embrace Standard Conventions:** Conformity to established formats indicates professionalism, not a lack of originality. Startling variations in design signal freakishness.
5.  **Practice Ruthless Efficiency:** Every letter should have a reason. Eliminate waste by handling one subject per letter, writing concisely, and using standard materials to minimize handling time.

### Table: Original-Era Advice vs. Modern Equivalent

| Original-era advice | Modern equivalent |
| :--- | :--- |
| Plain white paper of good quality; avoid gaudy colors and flashy designs. | Use clean, simple digital templates; avoid distracting fonts, excessive clip art, or cluttered email signatures. |
| Address an individual as "Dear Mr. Brown" rather than "Dear Sir." | Use the recipient’s name in the salutation (e.g., "Dear Alex Chen") rather than impersonal greetings like "To Whom It May Concern." |
| A business letter should handle only one subject. | Send a single, focused email per topic to ensure easy filing, search, and clear action items. |
| Use standard size envelopes; ensure the window is clear and the address is in plain view. | Use standard subject lines in emails; ensure the preview pane shows a clear, honest subject rather than a vague or misleading one. |
| Envelopes should be opaque to conceal contents; address should be clearly typed in black. | Ensure emails are sent via secure channels; use professional, legible fonts (e.g., Calibri or Arial) for clarity and trust. |

### Dated Advice and Modern Update

**Dated Advice:**
The passage recommends that only young girls (usually irresponsible) type business letters, and that the male dictator must check their work.

**Underlying Principle:**
Accuracy and professional presentation are non-negotiable. All staff must be trained, and all finished work must be reviewed for quality.

**Modern Update:**
Replace the gendered, hierarchical language with a focus on shared accountability. Advise that *all* team members—regardless of role or gender—should be proficient with writing tools and proofreading protocols. The principle of verifying work before sending remains essential, but the recommendation should emphasize collaborative review and training, not supervision based on gender or seniority.

messages.content:
Read the public-domain passage below and produce a structured English summary.

Output requirements:
1. A 120-word maximum plot summary.
2. A bullet list of Mary’s character traits shown in the passage, each supported by a concrete detail.
3. A short paragraph explaining how the setting creates emotional distance or isolation.
4. Three discussion questions suitable for ages 10–12.
5. Do not add plot information from outside the passage.

素材来源：Frances Hodgson Burnett, The Secret Garden, opening excerpt
来源链接：https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/113
版权/授权说明：Project Gutenberg lists this eBook as public domain in the USA.

【待处理素材】
When Mary Lennox was sent to Misselthwaite Manor to live with her uncle everybody said she was the most disagreeable-looking child ever seen. It was true, too. She had a little thin face and a little thin body, thin light hair and a sour expression. Her hair was yellow, and her face was yellow because she had been born in India and had always been ill in one way or another. Her father had held a position under the English Government and had always been busy and ill himself, and her mother had been a great beauty who cared only to go to parties and amuse herself with gay people. She had not wanted a little girl at all, and when Mary was born she handed her over to the care of an Ayah, who was made to understand that if she wished to please the Mem Sahib she must keep the child out of sight as much as possible. So when she was a sickly, fretful, ugly little baby she was kept out of the way, and when she became a sickly, fretful, toddling thing she was kept out of the way also. She never remembered seeing familiarly anything but the dark faces of her Ayah and the other native servants, and as they always obeyed her and gave her her own way in everything, because the Mem Sahib would be angry if she was disturbed by her crying, by the time she was six years old she was as tyrannical and selfish a little pig as ever lived. The young English governess who came to teach her to read and write disliked her so much that she gave up her place in three months, and when other governesses came to try to fill it they always went away in a shorter time than the first one. So if Mary had not chosen to really want to know how to read books she would never have learned her letters at all.

One frightfully hot morning, when she was about nine years old, she awakened feeling very cross, and she became crosser still when she saw that the servant who stood by her bedside was not her Ayah.

“Why did you come?” she said to the strange woman. “I will not let you stay. Send my Ayah to me.”

The woman looked frightened, but she only stammered that the Ayah could not come and when Mary threw herself into a passion and beat and kicked her, she looked only more frightened and repeated that it was not possible for the Ayah to come to Missie Sahib.

There was something mysterious in the air that morning. Nothing was done in its regular order and several of the native servants seemed missing, while those whom Mary saw slunk or hurried about with ashy and scared faces. But no one would tell her anything and her Ayah did not come. She was actually left alone as the morning went on, and at last she wandered out into the garden and began to play by herself under a tree near the veranda. She pretended that she was making a flower-bed, and she stuck big scarlet hibiscus blossoms into little heaps of earth, all the time growing more and more angry and muttering to herself the things she would say and the names she would call Saidie when she returned.

“Pig! Pig! Daughter of Pigs!” she said, because to call a native a pig is the worst insult of all.

She was grinding her teeth and saying this over and over again when she heard her mother come out on the veranda with someone. She was with a fair young man and they stood talking together in low strange voices. Mary knew the fair young man who looked like a boy. She had heard that he was a very young officer who had just come from England. The child stared at him, but she stared most at her mother. She always did this when she had a chance to see her, because the Mem Sahib—Mary used to call her that oftener than anything else—was such a tall, slim, pretty person and wore such lovely clothes. Her hair was like curly silk and she had a delicate little nose which seemed to be disdaining things, and she had large laughing eyes. All her clothes were thin and floating, and Mary said they were “full of lace.” They looked fuller of lace than ever this morning, but her eyes were not laughing at all. They were large and scared and lifted imploringly to the fair boy officer’s face.

“Is it so very bad? Oh, is it?” Mary heard her say.

“Awfully,” the young man answered in a trembling voice. “Awfully, Mrs. Lennox. You ought to have gone to the hills two weeks ago.”

The Mem Sahib wrung her hands.

“Oh, I know I ought!” she cried. “I only stayed to go to that silly dinner party. What a fool I was!”

At that very moment such a loud sound of wailing broke out from the servants’ quarters that she clutched the young man’s arm, and Mary stood shivering from head to foot. The wailing grew wilder and wilder. “What is it? What is it?” Mrs. Lennox gasped.

“Someone has died,” answered the boy officer. “You did not say it had broken out among your servants.”

“I did not know!” the Mem Sahib cried. “Come with me! Come with me!” and she turned and ran into the house.

After that appalling things happened, and the mysteriousness of the morning was explained to Mary. The cholera had broken out in its most fatal form and people were dying like flies. The Ayah had been taken ill in the night, and it was because she had just died that the servants had wailed in the huts. Before the next day three other servants were dead and others had run away in terror. There was panic on every side, and dying people in all the bungalows.

During the confusion and bewilderment of the second day Mary hid herself in the nursery and was forgotten by everyone. Nobody thought of her, nobody wanted her, and strange things happened of which she knew nothing. Mary alternately cried and slept through the hours. She only knew that people were ill and that she heard mysterious and frightening sounds. Once she crept into the dining-room and found it empty, though a partly finished meal was on the table and chairs and plates looked as if they had been hastily pushed back when the diners rose suddenly for some reason. The child ate some fruit and biscuits, and being thirsty she drank a glass of wine which stood nearly filled. It was sweet, and she did not know how strong it was. Very soon it made her intensely drowsy, and she went back to her nursery and shut herself in again, frightened by cries she heard in the huts and by the hurrying sound of feet. The wine made her so sleepy that she could scarcely keep her eyes open and she lay down on her bed and knew nothing more for a long time.

message.content:
### Plot Summary (120 words max)
Mary Lennox, a sour, sickly girl born in India, is sent to live with her uncle at Misselthwaite Manor after her parents die in a cholera outbreak. Her mother, a socialite, neglected her, leaving Mary to be raised by servants who obeyed her every whim. During the epidemic, Mary hides alone, forgotten by everyone. She eats leftover food, drinks wine, and falls asleep. She wakes to find herself the sole survivor, taken in by a clergyman’s family before being shipped to England. The story opens with her arrival at Misselthwaite, where she remains disagreeable and isolated.

### Mary’s Character Traits with Support
- **Disagreeable and sour**: She has “a thin little face… and a sour expression.”
- **Tyrannical and selfish**: She was “as tyrannical and selfish a little pig as ever lived” because servants always gave her own way.
- **Quick-tempered and violent**: When a strange servant appears, Mary “threw herself into a passion and beat and kicked her.”
- **Demanding and rude**: She orders the servant, “I will not let you stay. Send my Ayah to me.”
- **Lonely and neglected**: During the cholera, “Nobody thought of her, nobody wanted her.”

### Setting and Emotional Distance/Isolation
The passage’s setting—a hot, mysterious morning in India during a cholera outbreak—creates emotional distance by making Mary an afterthought. Her mother ignores her, servants disappear or die, and Mary hides “forgotten by everyone” in an empty nursery. The wailing of dying servants and panicked whispers between her mother and an officer emphasize that Mary is excluded from adult concerns. Even the deserted dining room, with hastily abandoned plates, shows a world moving on without her. This physical and emotional abandonment isolates Mary, leaving her to survive alone, unheard and unwanted.

### Discussion Questions (Ages 10–12)
1. Why do you think Mary’s mother kept her out of sight? How might that have affected Mary’s behavior?
2. Mary kicks and beats a servant when she doesn’t get her way. Why do you think she acts this way? Is it fair?
3. During the cholera outbreak, everyone forgets Mary. How do you think that made her feel? Have you ever felt forgotten?

messages.content:
Read the public-domain science explainer below and produce a structured English summary.

Output requirements:
1. A concise 100-word overview.
2. A concept map in text form using arrows, showing how water moves among major pools.
3. A list of at least eight key terms with one-sentence definitions based only on the passage.
4. A section titled “Human influence” summarizing how human actions affect water movement, storage, or availability.
5. Do not introduce outside climate, policy, or regional examples.

素材来源：U.S. Geological Survey Water Science School: Water cycle
来源链接：https://www.usgs.gov/water-science-school/water-cycle
版权/授权说明：USGS-authored information is considered U.S. public domain.

【待处理素材】
The water cycle describes where water is on Earth and how it moves. Human water use, land use, and climate change all impact the water cycle. By understanding these impacts, we can work toward using water sustainably.

Viewed from space, the most striking feature of our planet is the water. In both liquid and frozen form, it covers 75% of the Earth's surface. It fills the sky with clouds. Water is practically everywhere on Earth, from inside the planet's rocky crust to inside the cells of the human body. What's important to keep in mind is that all of this water is in constant motion across our planet.

Water is stored in the atmosphere, on the land surface, and below the ground. It can be a liquid, a solid, or a gas. Liquid water can be fresh or saline. Water moves between the places it is stored. Water moves at large scales, through watersheds, the atmosphere, and below the Earth's surface. Water moves at very small scales too. It is in us, plants, and other organisms.

Oceans store 96% of all water on Earth. Ocean water is saline, meaning it’s salty. On land, saline water is stored in saline lakes. The rest of the water on Earth is fresh water. Fresh water is stored in liquid form in freshwater lakes, artificial reservoirs, rivers, and wetlands. Water is stored in solid, frozen form in ice sheets and glaciers, and in snowpack at high elevations or near Earth's poles.

As it moves, water can change form between liquid, solid, and gas. Circulation mixes water in the oceans and transports water vapor in the atmosphere. Water moves between the atmosphere and the surface through evaporation, evapotranspiration, and precipitation. Water moves across the surface through snowmelt, runoff, and streamflow. Water moves into the ground through infiltration and groundwater recharge. Underground, groundwater flows within aquifers. Groundwater can return to the surface through natural discharge into rivers, the ocean, and from springs.

Water moves naturally and because of human actions. Energy from the sun and the force of gravity drive the continual movement of water between pools. The sun’s energy causes liquid water to evaporate into water vapor. Evapotranspiration is the main way water moves into the atmosphere from the land surface and oceans. Gravity causes water to flow downward on land. It causes rain, snow, and hail to fall from clouds.

Humans and other organisms rely on water for life. The amount of water that is available depends on how much water there is in each pool, when and how fast water moves through the water cycle, and how clean the water is. By understanding human impacts on the water cycle, we can work toward using water sustainably.

message.content:
### Concise Overview (100 words)
The water cycle describes the continuous movement of water across Earth, stored in oceans, atmosphere, land surface, and underground. It changes between liquid, solid, and gas forms. Solar energy and gravity drive processes like evaporation, precipitation, runoff, infiltration, and groundwater flow. Oceans hold 96% of Earth’s water (salty), while freshwater is stored in lakes, rivers, ice, and snow. Water moves naturally through watersheds, atmosphere, and underground aquifers. Human water use, land use, and climate change affect water movement, storage, and availability. Understanding these impacts helps promote sustainable water use.

### Concept Map (Text Form)
Atmosphere → (Precipitation) → Land Surface  
Land Surface → (Evaporation, Evapotranspiration) → Atmosphere  
Land Surface → (Runoff, Streamflow) → Oceans  
Land Surface → (Infiltration, Groundwater Recharge) → Groundwater (Aquifers)  
Groundwater → (Natural Discharge) → Rivers, Oceans, Springs  
Oceans → (Evaporation) → Atmosphere  
Ice/Snow → (Snowmelt) → Land Surface

### Key Terms (8+)
1. **Water cycle**: The continuous movement of water among Earth’s storage pools.  
2. **Evaporation**: The process where solar energy turns liquid water into water vapor.  
3. **Evapotranspiration**: The main way water moves from land and oceans into the atmosphere.  
4. **Precipitation**: Rain, snow, or hail falling from clouds due to gravity.  
5. **Runoff**: Water flowing across the land surface.  
6. **Infiltration**: Water moving into the ground from the surface.  
7. **Groundwater recharge**: The process of water entering underground aquifers.  
8. **Aquifer**: Underground rock or sediment that stores and transmits groundwater.  
9. **Watershed**: A large-scale area where water drains and moves across the landscape.  
10. **Saline water**: Water that is salty, stored mainly in oceans and saline lakes.

### Human influence
Human actions affect water movement, storage, and availability through water use, land use, and climate change. Water use (e.g., withdrawals for drinking, irrigation, industry) alters how much water is in each pool and how fast it moves. Land use changes (e.g., construction, deforestation) affect runoff, infiltration, and groundwater recharge. Climate change influences the timing and intensity of evaporation, precipitation, and snowmelt. These impacts modify when and how clean water is available, requiring careful management for sustainable use.

messages.content:
Write an 800–1,000 word executive update email from the COO of a fictional direct-to-consumer home goods company to all employees. Context: a warehouse management system migration has reduced shipping speed for one week, and customer support volume is up.

Requirements:
- Tone: accountable, calm, specific, and respectful of frontline teams.
- Explain what happened without blaming a team or vendor.
- Include current customer impact, what is being done today, what will change next week, and how teams should escalate urgent issues.
- Include a concise manager talking-points section with 4 bullets.
- Avoid clichés, excessive apology, legal language, or promises that cannot be guaranteed.

message.content:
**Subject:** Update on Warehouse System Migration & Current Shipping Delays  

**Team,**  

I want to give you a direct, honest update on what’s happening with our warehouse management system migration and how it’s affecting our customers and your work this week.  

**What happened**  

Last weekend, we completed a planned migration to a new warehouse management system. This is a significant infrastructure change—our first major system update in five years—and was designed to improve inventory accuracy and long-term shipping speed. However, during the first week of live operation, we encountered unexpected data synchronization issues between the new system and our shipping carrier APIs. This caused order processing to stall intermittently, and we fell behind our usual same-day fulfillment cadence.  

I want to be clear: this was not caused by any team’s performance or a vendor’s failure. Our operations, engineering, and warehouse teams prepared for months. The system itself is sound, but we underestimated the complexity of the real-time data handoff during peak transition volume. That is my responsibility to acknowledge, and I own the decision to proceed with the migration timeline.  

**Current customer impact**  

As of this morning:  
- Average shipping speed has increased from same-day to 3–4 business days for orders placed since Monday.  
- Customer support volume is up roughly 40% from baseline, primarily from inquiries about order status and delayed tracking updates.  
- Our support team is resolving about 70% of these contacts within standard SLAs, but hold times are longer than we would accept in normal operations.  

We have not lost any orders, and no customer data has been affected. We are shipping every order, but not as fast as we normally do or as our customers rightly expect.  

**What we are doing today**  

1. **Engineering teams** are patching the API sync issue. We have identified three specific data mapping errors and are testing fixes in a staging environment. The primary fix will go live tonight at 11:59 PM PT.  
2. **Warehouse leads** have added a second shift of temporary packers to clear the current backlog. We expect to catch up on all orders placed through Wednesday by Saturday evening.  
3. **Customer support managers** have activated our emergency overflow queue, routing non-urgent tier-1 tickets to a secondary team so frontline agents can focus on customers waiting on hold.  
4. **I am personally reviewing daily backlog reports** at 9:00 AM and 4:00 PM and will send a brief standup update each day until we return to baseline.  

**What will change next week**  

By Monday morning, we expect the API patch to eliminate the data sync errors. This should return shipping speed to same-day for most orders by Tuesday. We will keep the temporary second shift in place through Thursday to ensure we have capacity if residual issues arise.  

Beginning next Wednesday, we will also add a one-time “delivery delay credit” for customers whose orders shipped more than 5 business days after placement. The credit will be applied automatically—no action needed from support agents or customers. Details are being finalized and will be shared in tomorrow’s operations brief.  

**How to escalate urgent issues**  

If you encounter a customer issue that cannot be resolved through your normal channels—such as a duplicate order, a critical missing item, or a safety concern—please escalate via our internal `#ops-urgent` Slack channel and tag @ops-duty-lead. Do not wait for a manager to be available. For warehouse or system-level issues that affect order fulfillment, contact the DUTY-ARCHITECT alias in Slack. Response times will be under 15 minutes during operating hours through Sunday.  

**For managers: talking points to share with your teams**  

Please use these as a reference when speaking with your teams or in standups:  

- **No one person or team caused this.** The migration was our decision, and we collectively underestimated a technical handoff timing issue. We are fixing it, not assigning blame.  
- **Customers will see improved speed by Tuesday.** The API patch goes live tonight, and we have added temporary warehouse capacity to clear the backlog by Saturday.  
- **Support volume is still elevated, but we have activated the overflow queue.** Agents should redirect status-only inquiries to the automated tracking page when possible, and escalate only when a resolution requires backend action.  
- **Our frontline teams are doing hard work under pressure.** Please acknowledge their effort publicly and ensure they take breaks. Neither customers nor team members benefit from burnout.  

**Closing**  

I want to say plainly: we made a decision to upgrade our systems in the interest of long-term reliability, and we are paying a short-term cost in customer trust and team morale. That’s on me, not on anyone processing a return or packing a box.  

If you have questions about your specific role or concerns you think I should hear, please reply directly or ping me. I will read every message by end of day.  

Thank you for holding the line this week.  

Best,  
*Alex Chen*  
Chief Operating Officer  
Homely Goods

messages.content:
Write an 800–1,000 word story for children ages 7–10. The main characters are a careful snail who keeps a map of every puddle in the garden and a young sparrow who wants to fly before she has learned how to land. The story should take place after a summer rain.

Requirements:
- Complete story arc with a gentle problem and resolution.
- Warm, concrete language suitable for children.
- Include one simple nature concept, such as how puddles dry or how birds use wind, explained naturally through the story.
- Theme: patience can be a kind of bravery.
- No violence, death, horror, or adult romance.

message.content:
Here is a story for children ages 7–10.

---

### The Snail Who Mapped the Rain

After the summer rain, the whole world felt new. The grass was a deep, shiny green, and every leaf wore a necklace of sparkling drops. In one corner of the garden, beneath a wide rhubarb leaf, a snail named Silas was very busy.

Silas was not like other snails. While his cousins slid around eating leaves and leaving sticky trails, Silas had a secret passion. He was a mapmaker. Tucked inside his shell was a tiny, rolled-up scroll made from a piece of birch bark. On it, in thin, careful lines of dew, he drew the location of every puddle in the garden.

“There’s the Shallow Mirror,” he murmured, adding a small circle near the tomato plants. “And the Big Blue Eye by the rain barrel. And the Slippery Triangle under the swing set.”

Today was the best day of the year for a mapmaker. The rain had been so heavy that there were puddles everywhere. Silas moved slowly, but his eyes were sharp. He took a tiny sip of dew and drew a long, wobbly oval near the garden wall. He called this one “The Puddle That Reflects the Potting Shed Window.” It was a very good puddle.

Just then, a small, brown blur landed on the rhubarb leaf above him. It was Pip, a young sparrow no bigger than Silas’s shell. She wobbled and chirped with excitement.

“Hello, down there!” she cheeped. “Have you seen the sky? It’s so big and blue and *empty*! I want to fly in it. Right now!”

Silas poked his eye-stalks up. “Hello, Pip. Flying is a big thing.”

“It’s the best thing!” Pip hopped from one foot to the other. “I’ve been practicing. Watch!”

She fluttered her wings and launched herself off the leaf. For two whole seconds, she soared in a wobbly circle over the garden path. Then, she forgot how to land. Her feet stuck out, her wings froze, and she tumbled head-over-tail into the middle of the Shallow Mirror puddle.

*SPLASH!*

Pip came up spluttering, her feathers dripping. “I did it again,” she groaned.

Silas slid carefully down the stem. “You flew very well,” he said kindly. “But landing is part of flying. It’s the part where you come back to the ground safely.”

“But landing is boring,” Pip sighed. “It’s slow and careful. I want to feel the wind in my wings *now*!”

Silas unrolled his birch-bark map. He pointed one of his tiny feeler-tentacles at the Big Blue Eye. “Do you see this puddle, Pip? How it’s deep and wide?”

Pip peered at the wobbly lines. “It looks like a blue bean,” she said.

“It’s the best puddle for cooling off a tired bird,” Silas said. “But right now, it’s too deep for me to cross safely. It will take the sun and the wind all afternoon to dry it just a little. Maybe by supper time, I can slide around its edge. I have to wait.”

“Waiting is the worst,” Pip grumbled.

“Is it?” Silas asked. “Look at this puddle here, the Long Bumpy One by the fence. Do you see how the sun is making it smaller? That’s called evaporation. The rain doesn’t just disappear. The sun asks the water, ‘Will you come and visit the sky?’ And the water turns into a mist so tiny you can’t see it, and it floats up to make new clouds. But it takes time. The water is brave enough to be patient.”

Pip tilted her head. “Water is brave?”

“It’s brave to wait,” Silas said softly. “Waiting means you trust that the good thing is coming. You don’t have to rush. You can be still and watch the world change. That takes a kind of bravery, too.”

Pip thought about this. She looked at the sky, so huge and blue. She looked at her own clumsy feet. “I don’t want to be a puddle,” she said. “I want to be a bird who can land.”

“Then you have to practice the slow part,” said Silas. “How about this? I can’t see what’s around the corner of the potting shed. The puddle there is too far. If you flew, just a little hop, and looked for me, you could tell me what it looks like. Then you could try to land—slowly, on purpose—here on this dry moss.”

Pip’s chest puffed out. A job! A real flying job! She took a deep breath. “Okay,” she said. “For the map.”

She fluttered up, not too high, just above the hedge. She saw the secret puddle by the shed. It was shaped exactly like a giant’s footprint. She banked gently, and this time, she didn’t try to stop all at once. She remembered what Silas had said about the water. *Go slowly. Trust.*

She flapped her wings softly, slowing her speed. She aimed for the soft green moss. Her little claws touched, touched again, and then… she wobbled, but she stayed upright. She was standing on the moss!

“I landed!” she chirped, her voice full of wonder. “I landed and I didn’t fall!”

Silas smiled his snail smile. “Brave Pip.”

Pip hopped over to him. “The puddle is a giant footprint,” she reported. “With a little white daisy floating in it.”

Silas carefully added a tiny daisy shape on his map. “That is a wonderful detail,” he said. “Thank you, my friend.”

As the afternoon sun grew warmer, Pip practiced landing again and again. She would fly a short arc, then slow down, aim for a leaf or a stone, and put her feet out carefully. She fell sometimes, but each time, she got back up. She was learning that being brave wasn’t just about the fast, exciting parts. It was also about the slow, patient parts—the part where you waited for your wings to learn, the part where you watched the puddles shrink into clouds, the part where a snail completed his map one tiny step at a time.

By evening, the garden was golden. Pip perched on the rim of Silas’s shell as he slid home beneath the rhubarb leaf. The Big Blue Eye had shrunk. The Shallow Mirror was nearly gone.

“Where will you map tomorrow?” Pip asked.

“Everywhere the rain touches,” said Silas. “And maybe you can help. From the air, you see things differently.”

Pip snuggled close. “Then I’ll be your sky-scout,” she whispered. “But I promise to always come back down… slowly.”

And under the huge, patient, starry sky, the careful snail and the brave sparrow fell asleep, dreaming of maps and clouds and perfect landings yet to come.

messages.content:
Write an 800–1,000 word press release in English based only on the fictional facts below. Do not invent funding amounts, celebrity endorsers, political supporters, or external partners. Use a professional, journalistic style with a headline, dateline, lead paragraph, supporting details, at least one direct quote, and a media contact block.

Fictional facts:
- Organization: Harborleaf Botanical Garden.
- Announcement: Launch of a new “Pollinator Path” public education trail.
- Opening date: July 12, 2026.
- Location: The east meadow loop, a 0.8-mile accessible trail.
- Features: native plant demonstration beds, night-blooming flower station, tactile signage, youth observation cards, shaded resting points, and a volunteer-led weekend walk.
- Motivation: visitors have asked for more practical guidance on planting for bees, butterflies, moths, and hummingbirds.
- Quote speaker: Maya Ortiz, Director of Learning Programs. Core message: small planting decisions can create connected habitats across neighborhoods.
- Keep the topic nonpolitical and educational.

message.content:
**FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE**

**Harborleaf Botanical Garden Opens ‘Pollinator Path’ to Educate Visitors on Habitat-Friendly Planting**

**NORTHAMPTON, MA – June 10, 2026** – Harborleaf Botanical Garden will open its new “Pollinator Path” public education trail on July 12, 2026, offering visitors a hands-on, accessible experience designed to answer one of the most common questions from guests: how to turn a backyard, balcony, or community plot into a sanctuary for bees, butterflies, moths, and hummingbirds.

Located along the east meadow loop, the 0.8-mile trail is fully accessible and features a series of interactive stations intended to bridge the gap between garden inspiration and practical action. The trail includes native plant demonstration beds that showcase species local to the region, a dedicated night-blooming flower station highlighting plants that support moths and nocturnal pollinators, tactile signage for visitors with visual impairments, and youth observation cards that encourage children to document the insects and birds they encounter. Shaded resting points are spaced along the route, and a volunteer-led weekend walk program will begin the same weekend to provide guided interpretation.

“Our visitors have consistently asked for more concrete, practical guidance on how to support pollinators in their own spaces, not just admire them here,” said Maya Ortiz, Director of Learning Programs at Harborleaf Botanical Garden. “The Pollinator Path is designed to show that small planting decisions—like choosing a single native milkweed or adding a patch of evening primrose—can create connected habitats across neighborhoods. A balcony pot and a garden bed can both be part of a larger network of refuge.”

The project was developed in response to growing visitor interest in pollinator-friendly horticulture. According to garden staff, attendance data from the past three years shows a surge in questions about plant selection for bees, butterflies, moths, and hummingbirds during seasonal public programs. The new trail aims to make that information accessible year-round, with interpretive signage written in plain language and visual cues that support multiple learning styles.

The east meadow loop, which winds through a restored grassland area of the garden, was chosen for its existing diversity of wildflowers and its proximity to a natural water source. The new demonstration beds supplement the existing meadow with intentionally planted arrays of species such as purple coneflower, butterfly weed, wild bergamot, and trumpet honeysuckle. The night-blooming flower station, located in a slightly shaded bend of the trail, features evening primrose, moonflower, and four-o’clocks, with signage explaining how nocturnal pollinators navigate by scent and moonlight.

Tactile signage incorporates raised illustrations and Braille text, allowing visitors who are blind or have low vision to follow the trail’s narrative independently. Youth observation cards, available at the trailhead kiosk, include checklists and simple drawing prompts, such as “Draw a bee visiting a flower” or “Count how many butterflies you see.” The shaded resting points are built from sustainably sourced wood and are positioned every quarter-mile, offering benches and small tables for note-taking or sketching.

The volunteer-led weekend walk program will launch on July 12 and July 13, with two guided sessions each day. These walks, led by trained garden docents, will focus on identifying common pollinators, recognizing the difference between native and non-native plants, and learning simple actions visitors can take at home. No registration is required, and the walks are included with general garden admission.

Harborleaf Botanical Garden is a nonprofit public garden dedicated to plant conservation, education, and community engagement. Its grounds feature curated collections of native flora, restored meadows, and learning spaces for visitors of all ages. The Pollinator Path is the garden’s first dedicated education trail focused entirely on pollinator habitat.

The trail will be open daily during regular garden hours beginning July 12, 2026. Admission to the trail is included with general garden entry. More information about programming, accessibility, and group visits is available at the garden’s visitor center or online.

# # #

**Media Contact:**
Alex Chen
Communications Coordinator
Harborleaf Botanical Garden
555-010-2300
a.chen@harborleafgarden.org

**Note to Editors:** The July 12 opening coincides with the garden’s regular summer hours. A media preview walk will be held on July 9 at 10:00 a.m.; interested reporters should RSVP to the media contact above.

messages.content:
Write an 800–1,000 word non-erotic literary romance scene in English. Scenario: two former neighbors meet by chance in a small coastal laundromat during a power outage. Years ago, one of them left without saying goodbye. Tonight, a battery lantern, a half-finished load of towels, and an old voicemail become the center of the conversation.

Requirements:
- Focus on restrained emotional realism: regret, tenderness, defensiveness, and the difficulty of apologizing.
- Keep the entire piece to one scene.
- Do not use coercion, stalking, humiliation, explicit sexual content, or melodrama.
- Let physical details and subtext carry the emotion; dialogue should be sparse but meaningful.
- End with a quiet unresolved gesture rather than a full confession.

message.content:
The Laundromat on Seacliff Road was the only business with its lights on, but that wasn’t saying much. The power outage had swallowed the whole strip—the coffee shop, the bait-and-tackle, the real estate office with its faded photos of oceanfront condos. Even the streetlamps were dark, leaving the laundromat’s window a single, apologetic rectangle of amber light, like a lantern held low against the fog.

Inside, the silence was the first thing she noticed. No hum of dryers, no clatter of change. Just the sound of the coastal wind pushing at the glass door, and the soft, intermittent hiss of a single battery-powered lantern sitting on the folding counter.

Ellie had come because the towels had mildewed in the damp salt air of her rental cottage, and she’d hoped to get one load through before her own power had flickered and died. Now she stood at the counter, staring at the half-finished load, still wet, still useless. The dark fabric of the terry cloth looked like a pile of stranded sea creatures in the dim light.

Then the door opened.

She didn’t look up at first. She was a woman who had learned to keep her eyes on her own business. But the man who stepped inside was tall, broad-shouldered, and moved with a familiar, lopsided gait, as if he were perpetually walking into a headwind. He was holding a small duffel bag and a flashlight that he immediately clicked off when he saw the lantern.

He froze.

“Ellie.”

Her stomach dropped. She knew that voice—low, a little rough around the edges, like gravel rolled in honey. She looked up, and there he was. Ben. Older. His jawline sharper, his hair touched with grey at the temples. But his eyes were the same—that quiet, watchful grey that had always made her feel both seen and exposed.

“Ben.” She said his name without inflection, a defense mechanism she had perfected.

He didn’t move. He stood there, duffel bag hanging from one hand, looking at the wet towels, then back at her. “I didn’t think you’d be here.”

“I live here now,” she said. “Temporarily. For the summer.”

“I’m staying at my mother’s old place. Just for a few weeks. Clearing it out.”

She nodded. She had seen the obituary in the paper last month. She had even considered sending a card, but she hadn’t. That was how it worked with them. A long, empty history of things left unsent.

The lantern flickered, then steadied. The laundromat seemed smaller with him in it.

He cleared his throat. “Your load’s still wet. The dryers won’t work without power.”

“I know.” She folded her arms. “I was waiting for it to come back. Stubborn, I guess.”

“You always were.” It wasn’t an insult. It was a fact, stated with the quiet gravity of someone who had once known her well enough to say such things.

She felt the old defensiveness rise, a habit born from the year after he’d left. After he’d just... disappeared. No goodbye. No explanation. One day he was her neighbor, the man who brought her soup when she had the flu and who left a stack of her favorite books on her doorstep when he found a used copy. The next, his apartment was empty, his key returned to the landlord.

She had told herself she didn’t care. She had told herself it was better that way.

“I thought about you,” he said, quiet, as if testing the weight of each word. “When I found something in my mother’s things.”

He set the duffel bag on the counter. Unzipped it. Pulled out a cheap, black flip phone—the kind everyone used fifteen years ago. It was cracked along the screen.

“I found it in a box in her attic. I don’t know why I kept it. But the battery still holds a charge.” He pressed a button. The screen flickered to life, weak and pale. “There’s a voicemail. From you. From the night before I left.”

Ellie’s breath caught. She remembered that night. She remembered standing in her kitchen, staring at the wall, listening to the phone ring. She had called him to say—what? She didn’t even remember exactly. Something about the neighbor’s dog getting out. Something trivial, something that could have been an excuse.

But he hadn’t answered. She had left a message.

“I never listened to it,” he said. “Until last week.” He looked down at the phone, turning it over in his hands as if it were a sea urchin, something delicate and spiny. “I was a coward, Ellie. I had a job offer in Seattle. I told myself I’d call you from the road. From the plane. I told myself I’d write. But I didn’t. I was afraid.”

She listened to the hum of the wind, the soft sputter of the lantern. Her throat felt tight, but she kept her voice steady. “I called to tell you I was sick of waiting for you to say something. I said some things. I don’t remember them all.”

“I remember them,” he said. “I listened to it twelve times last Tuesday. You said I was a ghost. You said I was worse than a ghost, because at least ghosts let you mourn.”

She pressed her lips together. She didn't cry. She had done enough crying in the months after he left, and then she had stopped.

“You were right,” he said. His voice cracked, just barely. “I was worse. I left you with nothing. No explanation. No goodbye. I was too young to know that silence is its own kind of cruelty.”

The lantern guttered. For a moment, the room plunged into near-total darkness, lit only by the faint orange glow from the street. Then it steadied again, casting long shadows across the linoleum floor.

She reached out and touched the pile of wet towels. They were cold and heavy. “You don’t have to apologize,” she said. “It was a long time ago.”

“I know. But I’m going to anyway.” He set the phone down on the counter between them. “I’m sorry I didn’t say goodbye. I’m sorry I was afraid. And I’m sorry I made you think, even for a second, that you weren’t worth the courage it would have taken to stay.”

She looked at the phone. The voicemail icon blinked, a small orange square, stubbornly alive. She thought about pressing play. She thought about hearing her own younger voice, the one that had been so angry and so hurt.

Instead, she slid the phone back toward him. “Keep it,” she said. “I don’t need to hear it. I know what I said.”

He didn’t put it away. He just held it, the screen casting a thin light on his palm. “Do you still live in that apartment? The one with the yellow door?”

“No. I moved away two years later.”

“Were you happy?”

She considered the question. The honesty in it demanded a real answer. “Sometimes. Not always. But I learned to be okay with that.”

He nodded. He didn’t press. He just stood there, with his duffel bag and his old phone and his pile of regret, and she stood there with her wet towels and her practiced calm.

The wind pushed against the glass door again, and the light from the street flickered once, then died completely. The room went dark, save for the single battery lantern.

He didn’t move closer. She didn’t step away.

After a long moment, he said, “The power might be out all night.”

“I know.”

“I could walk you home. If you want.”

She looked at the towels, then at him. His silhouette was indistinct in the dim light, but she could make out the shape of him—the familiar lines of a man who had once been a ghost to her.

She didn’t say yes. She didn’t say no.

She picked up her wet towels, heavy and cold in her arms, and walked toward the door. He picked up the lantern, cupping it in his hand like a wounded bird, and followed her, just a step behind, letting the silence between them say everything the words could not.
